Costs arising from defects *detected after* the product reaches the customer — i.e., failures in the field. These are the most damaging category because they also affect brand equity.
*Examples:* Warranty claims, product returns, recalls, field service costs, litigation, loss of customer goodwill, complaint handling.

**Behaviour vs. Quality Level (2 marks):**
As quality level $x$ increases:
- **PC rises** (exponentially) — more investment in training, design, SPC is needed to push quality higher.
- **AC rises** (linearly) — more thorough inspection to maintain high quality.
- **IFC falls** (sharply) — fewer defects internally because prevention is working.
- **EFC falls** (very sharply) — warranty costs drop dramatically.
- **TQC has a U-shape** with a minimum at the **optimal quality level $x^*$**.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Classify each item (3 marks):**

| Cost Item | Amount (₹ L) | Category |
|-----------|-------------:|----------|
| Quality training of employees | 8 | **PC (Prevention)** |
| Incoming raw material inspection | 6 | **AC (Appraisal)** |
| Scrap and rework | 25 | **IFC (Internal Failure)** |
| Warranty claims | 18 | **EFC (External Failure)** |
| Product returns and recalls | 12 | **EFC (External Failure)** |
| Process capability studies | 5 | **PC (Prevention)** |
| Final product testing | 9 | **AC (Appraisal)** |
| Re-inspection of reworked items | 4 | **IFC (Internal Failure)** |

**Step 2: Calculate subtotals (2 marks):**

$$PC = 8 + 5 = 13 \; ₹ \text{ lakhs}$$

$$AC = 6 + 9 = 15 \; ₹ \text{ lakhs}$$

$$IFC = 25 + 4 = 29 \; ₹ \text{ lakhs}$$

$$EFC = 18 + 12 = 30 \; ₹ \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 3: Total Quality Cost (1 mark):**

$$TQC = 13 + 15 + 29 + 30 = 87 \; ₹ \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 4: % COQ to Production Cost (1 mark):**

$$\%COQ = \dfrac{87}{400} \times 100 = 21.75\%$$

**Comment on Quality Performance (1 mark):**
At **21.75%**, the COQ is **significantly higher than the industry benchmark (5%–25%, with best-in-class at ~2.5%)**. The dominant categories are **External Failure (30/87 = 34.5%)** and **Internal Failure (29/87 = 33.3%)**, indicating that prevention and appraisal are under-resourced. Management should:
- Increase investment in **Prevention** (training, SPC, supplier development).
- Tighten **Appraisal** at incoming stage to prevent defects flowing downstream.
- Target an **80:20 reduction in CNCoC** within 12 months using a DMAIC project.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is meant by **Cost of Quality (COQ)**? Discuss the **economic model of optimal quality level** with a neat sketch of the cost curves. Derive the condition for minimum total quality cost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm estimates its quality cost functions (₹ lakhs) as a function of quality level $x$ (where $x$ is the fraction of good units, $0 < x \leq 1$):

$$PC(x) = 10x^2, \quad AC(x) = 5x, \quad IFC(x) = \dfrac{15}{x}, \quad EFC(x) = \dfrac{8}{x^2}$$

**Required:**
(i) Write the Total Quality Cost function. **(2 marks)**
(ii) Find the optimal quality level $x^*$ by applying the first-order optimality condition. **(3 marks)**
(iii) Calculate the minimum TQC at $x^*$. **(2 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer to Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total cost associated with achieving and maintaining product quality. It includes the cost of conformance (PC + AC) and the cost of non-conformance (IFC + EFC).

**Economic Model — Optimal Quality Level (4 marks):**

As the quality level $x$ of a product increases from 0 to 1, the four cost categories behave as follows (described with a graph):

- **Prevention Cost (PC) and Appraisal Cost (AC):** Rise with $x$ (inverted U-shape when plotted in mirror).
- **Internal Failure (IFC) and External Failure (EFC):** Fall as $x$ rises.
- **Total Quality Cost (TQC):** The sum of the above is a **U-shaped curve** with a minimum.

**Sketch (describe verbally for 2 marks):**
- X-axis: Quality level $x$ (0 → 1).
- Y-axis: Cost in ₹.
- PC, AC curves: rising, convex.
- IFC, EFC curves: falling, convex.
- TQC curve: U-shaped, with minimum at $x^*$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$TQC(x) = PC(x) + AC(x) + IFC(x) + EFC(x)$$

Differentiate with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dx} = \dfrac{d(PC)}{dx} + \dfrac{d(AC)}{dx} + \dfrac{d(IFC)}{dx} + \dfrac{d(EFC)}{dx}$$

For minimum TQC, set first derivative to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=x^*} = 0$$

The economic interpretation:

$$\underbrace{\dfrac{d(PC)}{dx} + \dfrac{d(AC)}{dx}}_{\text{Marginal cost of conformance}} \;=\; -\underbrace{\left[\dfrac{d(IFC)}{dx} + \dfrac{d(EFC)}{dx}\right]}_{\text{Marginal savings from failure reduction}}$$

At $x^*$, the **marginal cost of investing in one more unit of quality equals the marginal savings from reduced failures**. The second derivative $\dfrac{d^2(TQC)}{dx^2} > 0$ confirms a minimum.

---

### **Model Answer to Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Total Quality Cost function (2 marks):**

$$TQC(x) = 10x^2 + 5x + \dfrac{15}{x} + \dfrac{8}{x^2}$$

**(ii) Optimal quality level $x^*$ (3 marks):**

Differentiate:

$$\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dx} = 20x + 5 - \dfrac{15}{x^2} - \dfrac{16}{x^3}$$

Set to zero:

$$20x + 5 - \dfrac{15}{x^2} - \dfrac{16}{x^3} = 0$$

Multiply by $x^3$:

$$20x^4 + 5x^3 - 15x - 16 = 0$$

Solving numerically (Newton-Raphson or trial) yields:

$$x^* \approx 0.92$$

**Verification via economic meaning:**
At $x^* = 0.92$, marginal cost of prevention/appraisal = marginal savings from failure reduction. The firm operates at 92% good-quality yield, with 8% defect rate being the **economically optimal** defect level (not zero!).

**(iii) Minimum TQC at $x^* = 0.92$ (2 marks):**

$$TQC_{min} = 10(0.92)^2 + 5(0.92) + \dfrac{15}{0.92} + \dfrac{8}{(0.92)^2}$$

$$TQC_{min} = 10(0.8464) + 4.60 + 16.304 + 9.451$$

$$TQC_{min} = 8.464 + 4.60 + 16.304 + 9.451$$

$$TQC_{min} = 38.82 \; ₹ \text{ lakhs}$$

> **Strategic insight:** Trying to push $x$ from 0.92 → 1.0 would *increase* TQC to ~ ₹ 45+ lakhs due to over-investment in prevention. The economically optimal defect rate is **not zero** — a key insight from Juran's economic theory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Re-inspection" as AC** — *Re-inspection of reworked items is IFC, not AC*, because rework has already happened. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the second-order condition** — When deriving optimum, you must state $\dfrac{d^2(TQC)}{dx^2} > 0$ to confirm a minimum. Otherwise, marks are cut.
> 3. **Mistaking "Zero Defects = Zero Cost"** — Many students claim the goal is zero defects. The correct economic answer is the **Juran optimum**, not zero. Examiners explicitly deduct for this misconception.
> 4. **Mixing up Warranty Costs** — Warranty is **EFC**, not PC, even if it is a "planned cost." Examiners test this trap.
> 5. **Sketching graph incorrectly** — When asked for a "neat sketch," students often draw PC and AC as constant (flat lines). They must be **rising** curves, and IFC, EFC must be **falling** curves. TQC must be a **U-shape**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PECHT413 Module 1 — Quality Costs**

- **PAF Model = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure.** This is the **single most important classification** in TQM economics.
- **Modern Two-Bucket Model = CoC (PC + AC) + CNCoC (IFC + EFC).** Both classifications are accepted; examiners prefer PAF for detailed questions.
- **Prevention costs are the cheapest in the long run** — high PC today = low IFC + EFC tomorrow.
- **External failure is the most expensive** — it carries both direct costs (warranty, return) AND indirect costs (lost reputation, litigation).
- **TQC is a U-shaped function of quality level $x$** with a minimum at $x^*$. The optimal $x^*$ is found by setting $\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dx} = 0$.
- **At $x^*$:** Marginal cost of prevention/appraisal = Marginal savings from failure reduction.
- **Second-order condition:** $\dfrac{d^2(TQC)}{dx^2} > 0$ confirms a minimum.
- **Industry benchmark for % COQ:** 2.5%–6.5% of sales; 5%–25% of production cost.
- **Easily-confused classifications to memorize:**
  - Re-inspection of reworked items = **IFC** (not AC)
  - Supplier audit cost = **PC** (not AC)
  - Calibration of measuring equipment = **AC**
  - Product recall = **EFC** (not PC, even if "planned")
  - Statistical Process Control (SPC) implementation = **PC** (prevention)
- **Quality Cost Analysis Technique (9 steps):** Define scope → Collect data → Classify into PAF → Calculate TQC → Benchmark → Identify Pareto hotspots in CNCoC → Propose prevention investments → Recalculate $x^*$ and project savings → Management review and PDCA cycle.
- **Pareto Principle in COQ:** Typically, 80% of quality costs come from 20% of the cost items — focus on the largest line items first.
- **Zero defects ≠ Optimal.** Juran's economic optimum is the *right* defect level, not zero.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question gives you a list of 8–10 cost items and asks classification, **draw a T-format table** with PC, AC, IFC, EFC columns and slot each item. Examiners reward structured presentation with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Total Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Total Quality Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a **comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy** that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving **all members** of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — to meet or exceed **customer expectations**.

According to the **ISO 8402:1994** standard, *Quality* is defined as:

> "The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs."

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, TQM is positioned as a **customer-driven, process-oriented, people-centric** management system that integrates **quality assurance**, **quality control**, and **quality improvement** into a single unified organizational culture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Five-Star Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a five-star restaurant. The head chef (top management) sets the standard — every dish must taste perfect, look elegant, and be served hot. Now, the *total* in TQM means **every single person** in that kitchen — from the vegetable cutter to the dishwasher — takes ownership of the dish's quality. If the cutter chops unevenly, the dish fails before it even reaches the pan. If the waiter serves cold food, the kitchen's effort is wasted.
>
> TQM says: **quality is not just the chef's job — it is everyone's responsibility, every minute, every process.**

### 1.3 Key Quality Gurus (Foundational Pillars)

The philosophy of TQM rests on the contributions of several **Quality Gurus**. The KTU syllabus places heavy emphasis on these pioneers:

| Guru | Core Contribution | Country / Era |
|---|---|---|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management; PDSA Cycle | USA, 1950s |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | USA, 1950s |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | "Quality is Free"; Zero Defects; **4 Absolutes of Quality** | USA, 1970s |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles; Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram | Japan, 1960s |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (TQC) — the original "Total" | USA, 1956 |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Quality Loss Function; Robust Design | Japan, 1970s |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Process Control (SPC); PDCA Originator | USA, 1920s |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A direct 7-mark question often asks, *"Explain the contributions of any two quality gurus."* Always pair each guru with their **distinctive concept** (e.g., Deming → 14 Points, not just "TQM").

### 1.4 Definition of Quality — Three Layers

Academically, "Quality" is multi-dimensional. KTU expects students to distinguish:

1. **Transcendent Quality** — innate excellence, not precisely measurable (e.g., a Picasso painting).
2. **Product-Based Quality** — quality is a precise, measurable variable (e.g., tensile strength of steel in MPa).
3. **User-Based Quality** — fitness for the intended user's purpose ("the customer is the judge").
4. **Manufacturing-Based Quality** — conformance to specifications, drawings, and tolerances.
5. **Value-Based Quality** — quality measured against price paid (best value for money).

### 1.5 Dimensions of Quality (David Garvin's 8 Dimensions)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to list and briefly explain **Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality** as they form the bridge between abstract quality and measurable product/service attributes.

The dimensions are: **Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, and Perceived Quality.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Principles of TQM

TQM is built upon a **set of universal principles**. The following structured breakdown is the most frequently tested material in KTU Module 1:

- **Customer Focus:** The customer (internal + external) defines quality. The voice of the customer (VOC) drives every process.
- **Total Employee Involvement / Participation:** Every employee, from CEO to sweeper, is responsible for quality.
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** Quality is not a destination but a journey — the PDCA cycle must never stop.
- **Process-Centered Approach:** A process is a series of steps that transform inputs into outputs. TQM focuses on improving the **process** (root cause) rather than blaming the **person** (symptom).
- **Integrated System:** All departments (production, marketing, finance, HR) must work as one interconnected system, not in silos.
- **Strategic & Systematic Approach:** Quality management must be a core part of the **strategic plan**, not an afterthought.
- **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Decisions are driven by **data, statistical analysis**, and not by gut feel.
- **Communication:** Effective two-way communication sustains morale and motivation during change.
- **Supplier Partnership:** Building long-term win-win relationships with suppliers ensures inbound quality.

### 2.2 The PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The PDCA Cycle** is the engine of TQM. Every improvement project in TQM runs through these four stages. KTU frequently asks for a 7-mark descriptive answer on PDCA.

- **P — Plan:** Identify the problem, analyse it, and develop a plan for improvement (define objectives, methods, metrics).
- **D — Do:** Implement the plan on a small scale (pilot run), collect data.
- **C — Check / Study:** Analyse the data collected during the "Do" phase; compare results against expected outcomes.
- **A — Act:** If successful, standardize the change; if not, revise the plan and start the cycle again.

The cycle is **continuous** — once "Act" is complete, a new "Plan" begins, hence the circular nature.

### 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Analytical Framework

Cost of Quality is a critical quantitative concept in TQM. It classifies quality-related expenditure into **four categories**:

| Category | Type | Description | Cost Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prevention Costs (P)** | Cost of *doing it right the first time* | Training, quality planning, supplier capability assurance, design reviews | ↑ Prevention ⇒ ↓ Failure |
| **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Cost of *detecting defects* | Inspection, testing, calibration, audits | Cost of measurement |
| **Internal Failure Costs (IF)** | Cost of defects *found before delivery* | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, downtime | Waste inside the plant |
| **External Failure Costs (EF)** | Cost of defects *found after delivery* | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill | Most damaging cost |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete mathematical toolkit** for Module 1 of PECHT413. KTU Numerical/Analytical questions in the ESE may test these formulas directly.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Quality (COQ)** | $COQ = P + A + IF + EF$ | Currency units (₹, $, etc.) |
| 2 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = IF + EF$ | The "fire-fighting" cost |
| 3 | **Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)** | $COGQ = P + A$ | The "prevention" investment |
| 4 | **Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)** | $QCR = \dfrac{COPQ}{COGQ}$ | Optimal ≈ 1:1 (classical model) |
| 5 | **Failure Cost as % of Sales** | $FC\% = \dfrac{IF + EF}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100$ | Benchmark ≈ 5–10% of sales |
| 6 | **Quality Level (Yield)** | $Y = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $N$ = number of units |
| 7 | **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | $D$=defects, $U$=units, $O$=opportunities |
| 8 | **Process Sigma Level** | $\sigma = \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{s}$ | $s$ = standard deviation |
| 9 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passed first time}}{\text{Units started}}$ | Expressed as a decimal or % |
| 10 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$ | Product of all individual process yields |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the vertical pipe symbol** (e.g., $|x|$) inside the table cells above. In markdown tables, the pipe `|` breaks the column structure. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value notation in LaTeX.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The COQ model is not merely academic. In industries such as **automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)**, **aerospace (HAL, Boeing)**, and **electronics (Samsung, Foxconn)**, COQ analysis guides annual budgets — companies often discover that **paying more for prevention reduces total cost dramatically** (the **1-10-100 rule**: $1 to prevent a defect at design stage, $10 to detect it in production, $100 (or more) to fix it after delivery).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Quality Masters (Deming, Juran, Crosby)

This analytical comparison is a **very common 14-mark question** in KTU. Mastering this matrix allows students to score full marks by listing contrasts systematically.

| Parameter | **W. Edwards Deming** | **Joseph M. Juran** | **Philip B. Crosby** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core Philosophy** | System thinking; variation management | Fitness for use; quality planning | Conformance to requirements |
| **Famous Output** | 14 Points for Management | Juran Trilogy | Zero Defects; 4 Absolutes |
| **Cycle Used** | PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) | Quality Trilogy (QP, QC, QI) | 14 Steps to Quality Improvement |
| **View on Defects** | Reduce variation, not blame people | Focus on chronic & sporadic problems | Prevention, not inspection |
| **Role of Statistics** | Statistical Process Control (SPC) is central | Statistical methods are important but not the only tool | Management-driven, less statistical |
| **Customer View** | Customer delight via long-term relationship | Customer satisfaction is paramount | Quality = conformance = customer gets what was specified |
| **Cost View** | Poor quality = cost of non-conformance | Quality costs are measurable | "Quality is Free" — cost of prevention is always less than cost of correction |
| **Origin Era** | Post-WWII Japan, 1950s | 1950s onward, USA + Japan | 1970s, USA |
| **Key Slogan** | "Constancy of purpose" | "Fitness for use" | "Do it right the first time" |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation — Cost of Quality (COQ) Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently poses a **numerical/analytical question worth 7 marks** where students are given cost data and must compute COQ, COPQ, COGQ, and interpret the trend. The full working is shown below.

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Style):**

A manufacturing company reported the following quality-related expenditure for the financial year 2023–24:

- Quality training of employees: **₹ 2,00,000**
- Inspection and testing: **₹ 3,00,000**
- Rework on defective products: **₹ 1,50,000**
- Warranty claims paid to customers: **₹ 4,00,000**
- Scrap and wastage: **₹ 1,00,000**
- Calibration of measuring instruments: **₹ 1,50,000**
- Product recall due to field failure: **₹ 2,50,000**
- Supplier capability audit: **₹ 50,000**

Total annual sales = **₹ 1,00,00,000**. Compute:
1. Prevention Cost (P)
2. Appraisal Cost (A)
3. Internal Failure Cost (IF)
4. External Failure Cost (EF)
5. Total Cost of Quality (COQ)
6. Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)
7. Failure cost as a percentage of total sales.

#### Step 1 — Classify Each Item into a COQ Category

| Item | Amount (₹) | Category |
|---|---:|---|
| Quality training of employees | 2,00,000 | Prevention (P) |
| Supplier capability audit | 50,000 | Prevention (P) |
| Inspection and testing | 3,00,000 | Appraisal (A) |
| Calibration of measuring instruments | 1,50,000 | Appraisal (A) |
| Rework on defective products | 1,50,000 | Internal Failure (IF) |
| Scrap and wastage | 1,00,000 | Internal Failure (IF) |
| Warranty claims paid to customers | 4,00,000 | External Failure (EF) |
| Product recall due to field failure | 2,50,000 | External Failure (EF) |

#### Step 2 — Compute Prevention Cost (P)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \text{Quality training} + \text{Supplier audit} \\
  &= 2{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}000 \\
  &= 2{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Appraisal Cost (A)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \text{Inspection and testing} + \text{Calibration} \\
  &= 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 \\
  &= 4{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Internal Failure Cost (IF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
IF &= \text{Rework} + \text{Scrap and wastage} \\
   &= 1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 \\
   &= 2{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Compute External Failure Cost (EF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EF &= \text{Warranty claims} + \text{Product recall} \\
   &= 4{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 \\
   &= 6{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute Total Cost of Quality (COQ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ &= P + A + IF + EF \\
    &= 2{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}50{,}000 \\
    &= 16{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Compute Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)

$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= IF + EF \\
     &= 2{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}50{,}000 \\
     &= 9{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 8 — Compute Failure Cost as % of Total Sales

$$
\begin{aligned}
FC\% &= \frac{IF + EF}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
     &= 0.09 \times 100 \\
     &= 9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 9 — Interpret the Result

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for the KTU Board Examiner:**
> A failure cost of **9% of total sales** is above the **classical benchmark of 5–10%** and is dangerously high. The company is spending more on **fire-fighting** (₹9,00,000) than on **fire-prevention** (₹2,50,000). The recommended action is to **increase Prevention and Appraisal investment** (training, supplier audits, process capability studies) so that future failure costs drop — this is the **Juran / Crosby "invest in prevention" principle**.

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Why "Quality is Free" (Crosby's Theorem)

Philip Crosby's famous assertion that "Quality is Free" can be mathematically justified using the COQ model:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Savings} &= \Delta COPQ - \Delta COGQ \\
                   &= (\text{Old } IF + EF - \text{New } IF + EF) - (\text{New } P + A - \text{Old } P + A)
\end{aligned}
$$

If prevention investment is increased and the percentage reduction in failure costs exceeds the percentage increase in prevention costs, then **Net Savings > 0**, proving Crosby's claim.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TQM — A Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a TQM system, showing how customer needs drive every subsystem of the organization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Needs and Expectations] --> B[Top Management Commitment]
    B --> C[Strategic Quality Planning]
    C --> D[Process Design and Standardization]
    D --> E[Supplier Partnership and Input Quality]
    E --> F[Production and Service Delivery]
    F --> G[Quality Measurement and SPC]
    G --> H{Conformance Check}
    H -- Yes --> I[Customer Satisfaction and Loyalty]
    H -- No --> J[Root Cause Analysis and CAPA]
    J --> K[Continuous Improvement PDCA]
    K --> D
    I --> L[Business Excellence and Growth]
    K --> L
    B --> M[Total Employee Involvement and Training]
    M --> F
    G --> N[Cost of Quality Analysis]
    N --> C
```

### 4.2 The PDCA Cycle as a Rotating Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PLAN Identify Problem and Set Objectives] --> D[DO Implement on Pilot Scale]
    D --> C[CHECK Study Results vs Expected]
    C --> A[ACT Standardize or Revise]
    A --> P
```

### 4.3 Cost of Quality — PAF Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    COQ[Total Cost of Quality COQ] --> COGQ[Cost of Good Quality COGQ]
    COQ --> COPQ[Cost of Poor Quality COPQ]
    COGQ --> P[Prevention Costs]
    COGQ --> AP[Appraisal Costs]
    COPQ --> IF[Internal Failure Costs]
    COPQ --> EF[External Failure Costs]
    P --> P1[Training]
    P --> P2[Quality Planning]
    P --> P3[Supplier Audits]
    AP --> AP1[Inspection]
    AP --> AP2[Testing]
    AP --> AP3[Calibration]
    IF --> IF1[Rework]
    IF --> IF2[Scrap]
    EF --> EF1[Warranty Claims]
    EF --> EF2[Product Recall]
    EF --> EF3[Lost Goodwill]
```

### 4.4 TQM Implementation Roadmap (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1 Management Commitment] --> S2[Stage 2 Awareness and Training]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Goal Setting and Measurement]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Process Improvement]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Continuous Improvement Culture]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Recognition and Reward System]
    S6 --> S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams in the exam:** Use these block architectures to structure your 14-mark descriptive answers. Start with the topmost block, explain the input, move through the process stages, and end with the feedback loop (continuous improvement).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]**

**Q1. Define Total Quality Management. List any four of its key principles.**

**Model Answer:**

TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, total-system approach to quality management that seeks to improve quality and productivity by involving all members of an organization in a continuous improvement effort.

Four key principles of TQM:
1. **Customer focus** — Quality is defined by the customer.
2. **Total employee involvement** — Everyone is responsible for quality.
3. **Process-centered approach** — Focus on improving the process.
4. **Continuous improvement (Kaizen)** — Improvement is a never-ending journey.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for principles with brief explanation.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, May 2024]**

**Q2. What is the "Cost of Quality"? Differentiate between Prevention Costs and Appraisal Costs.**

**Model Answer:**

**Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product/service quality, comprising prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs.

**Difference:**

| Aspect | Prevention Costs | Appraisal Costs |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | *Prevent* defects from occurring | *Detect* defects that have occurred |
| **Examples** | Training, quality planning, supplier audits | Inspection, testing, calibration |
| **Stage** | Design and pre-production stage | Production and post-production stage |
| **Cost Behaviour** | Investing more here *reduces* total COQ | Necessary but does not prevent future defects |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for COQ definition + 2 marks for difference with examples.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **Deming 14 Points for Management** and bring out their relevance in implementing TQM. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **Juran Quality Trilogy** and explain how each of its three processes contributes to total quality. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer — Question A:**

**Part (a) — Deming's 14 Points for Management:**

1. **Create constancy of purpose** for improvement of product and service.
2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — quality is a competitive necessity.
3. **Cease dependence on inspection** to achieve quality — build quality *in*.
4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone** — minimize total cost.
5. **Improve constantly** every process — planning, production, service.
6. **Institute training on the job.**
7. **Institute leadership** — supervision should help people and machines do a better job.
8. **Drive out fear** so that everyone may work effectively.
9. **Break down barriers between departments** — team work.
10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** that ask for zero defects without method.
11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** for the workforce and numerical goals for management.
12. **Remove barriers** that rob people of pride of workmanship.
13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.**
14. **Put everybody to work** to accomplish the transformation.

**Relevance in TQM Implementation:**
These points guide managers to create a quality-driven culture, focus on systems, build employee capability, and emphasize long-term thinking — all of which form the foundation of TQM.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for listing and briefly explaining all 14 points + 2 marks for TQM relevance.)*

**Part (b) — Juran's Quality Trilogy:**

Juran's Quality Trilogy consists of three interlinked processes:

1. **Quality Planning (QP):** Identify customers and their needs; develop product features that respond to those needs; develop processes to produce the product features; establish process controls and transfer the plans to operating forces.
2. **Quality Control (QC):** Evaluate actual quality performance; compare actual performance to quality goals; act on the difference (corrective action).
3. **Quality Improvement (QI):** Establish the infrastructure to ensure quality improvement; identify specific projects for improvement; establish project teams; provide resources, training, and motivation for teams to diagnose causes and implement remedies.

**Contribution to TQM:**
- **QP** ensures the organization *designs* for quality.
- **QC** ensures the organization *maintains* quality during operations.
- **QI** ensures the organization *advances* quality beyond current levels.

Together they form a closed-loop system that operationalizes TQM.

*(7 marks: 2 marks per process × 3 = 6 marks + 1 mark for synthesis.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**(a)** Explain the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept. Compute the **Total COQ, COPQ, and Failure Cost % of Sales** for the following data: Total Sales = ₹50,00,000; Prevention = ₹1,00,000; Appraisal = ₹1,50,000; Internal Failure = ₹80,000; External Failure = ₹2,70,000. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **PDCA Cycle** and its application in a continuous improvement project. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Answer — Question B:**

**Part (a) — COQ Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given data.
- $P = 1{,}00{,}000$, $A = 1{,}50{,}000$, $IF = 80{,}000$, $EF = 2{,}70{,}000$, Total Sales $= 50{,}00{,}000$

**Step 2:** Total COQ.
$$
\begin{aligned}
COQ &= P + A + IF + EF \\
    &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 \\
    &= 6{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** COPQ.
$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= IF + EF \\
     &= 80{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 \\
     &= 3{,}50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Failure cost as % of Sales.
$$
\begin{aligned}
FC\% &= \frac{IF + EF}{\text{Sales}} \times 100 \\
     &= \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
     &= 7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpret — 7% failure cost is within the *acceptable* benchmark (5–10%) but still indicates room for improvement by increasing prevention spending.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for concept + 4 marks for calculations + 2 marks for interpretation.)*

**Part (b) — PDCA Cycle with Block Diagram:**

*(Insert the PDCA block diagram from Section 4.2 of these notes — students are expected to draw a four-block circular flow.)*

**PDCA in a Continuous Improvement Project — Application:**

1. **Plan:** Define the problem (e.g., customer complaints about a defective batch). Collect baseline data. Set a measurable objective (e.g., reduce defects from 5% to 1% in 3 months).
2. **Do:** Pilot the proposed change on a single production line. Train operators. Document observations.
3. **Check:** Compare the pilot results with the original baseline. Run statistical tests to confirm whether the reduction is significant.
4. **Act:** If the pilot succeeds, standardize the change across all lines. If it fails, return to Plan with revised hypotheses.

The cycle then **restarts** for the next level of improvement — this is the engine of Kaizen.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 5 marks for step-by-step application.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PECHT413 Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Memorizing without mapping:** Students often write 14 points of Deming or 4 absolutes of Crosby as a *list* without explaining their *intent*. KTU examiners award full marks only when each principle is paired with its *strategic purpose*.
> 2. **Confusing PDCA with PDSA:** Both names exist (Shewhart used PDCA, Deming renamed it PDSA — "Study" instead of "Check"). Either is acceptable, but mixing them in a single answer is a mark loss.
> 3. **COQ Numerical Mistakes:** Forgetting to sum **all four** components; misclassifying *supplier audit* as Appraisal (it is Prevention).
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** In a 7-mark PDCA question, a missing block diagram forfeits at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the customer dimension:** Any TQM answer that does not mention *customer focus* is considered incomplete by the KTU board.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Basic Concepts of TQM**
>
> - **TQM = Total + Quality + Management** — every person, every process, every customer.
> - Quality is **defined by the customer**, not by the manufacturer.
> - Three master gurus: **Deming (System/Variation), Juran (Trilogy), Crosby (Conformance/Zero Defects)**.
> - **Deming's PDCA / PDSA** is the foundational cycle; it must be drawn as a **circular** block diagram.
> - **Crosby's 4 Absolutes:** Quality = Conformance to requirements; Prevention, not inspection; Zero Defects performance standard; Measure quality by the price of non-conformance.
> - **COQ has 4 buckets:** Prevention (P), Appraisal (A), Internal Failure (IF), External Failure (EF).
> - Key formulas: $COQ = P+A+IF+EF$; $COPQ = IF+EF$; $FC\% = (IF+EF) / \text{Sales} \times 100$.
> - **Classical optimum:** COPQ ≈ COGQ (1:1 ratio) — invest in prevention until this balance is reached.
> - **Garvin's 8 dimensions** must be memorized verbatim: Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.
> - **Juran Trilogy:** Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement.
> - **Ishikawa** = Quality Circles + Fishbone Diagram.
> - **Taguchi** = Quality Loss Function (loss begins the instant a product deviates from target, even within spec).
> - Benchmark failure cost: **5–10% of sales** is typical; >15% indicates a quality crisis.
> - **1-10-100 Rule:** $1 to prevent at design, $10 to detect in production, $100+ to fix in the field.
> - TQM is a **culture**, not a *program* — it has no end date.
> - Always end a TQM answer with the **continuous improvement** philosophy to signal holistic understanding to the KTU examiner.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Historical Review
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Historical Review ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Historical Review (KTU Definition):** The chronological study of the evolution of quality management practices, tracing the progression from traditional craft-based inspection to modern Total Quality Management (TQM). It documents the philosophical contributions of quality gurus, the impact of World War II, the Japanese quality revolution, and the integration of statistical and humanistic approaches that shaped contemporary quality management systems. The historical review of Total Quality Management is fundamentally a study of **paradigm shifts** in how organizations view quality. It examines the transition through four distinct evolutionary phases: 1. **Inspection Era (Pre-1900s – 1930s)** – Reactive detection of defects 2. **Quality Control Era (1930s – 1960s)** – Statistical measurement and process control 3. **Quality Assurance Era (1960s – 1980s)** – Systematic prevention across the system 4. **Total Quality Management Era (1980s – Present)** – Organization-wide customer-focused excellence ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Evolution of Healthcare** > > Imagine how medicine evolved: > - **Ancient times**: A healer only treated symptoms after the patient got sick (Inspection) > - **1800s**: Doctors began running tests and measuring vital signs to diagnose (Quality Control) > - **1900s**: Hospitals built entire systems to ensure patient safety and prevent disease (Quality Assurance) > - **Today**: Healthcare focuses on holistic wellness — diet, mental health, exercise, prevention — involving every stakeholder (TQM) > > Similarly, **quality management evolved from "catching bad products" to "building a system where every person in the organization is responsible for excellence."** ### 1.3 Key Historical Constants and Metrics The following constants and milestones form the **backbone** of any TQM historical review: - **1924**: Walter Shewhart introduces the **Statistical Quality Control (SQC) chart** at Bell Laboratories - **1931**: Shewhart publishes *Economic Control of Quality of Manufactured Product* - **1950**: W. Edwards Deming delivers his famous lectures in Japan (**Deming's 14 Points** emerge later) - **1951**: Joseph Juran publishes *Quality Control Handbook* - **1954**: Joseph Juran lectures in Japan — introduces the **Juran Trilogy** - **1961**: Philip Crosby is born as a quality figure (later "Zero Defects" concept in **1979** at Martin Marietta) - **1970s**: Kaoru Ishikawa promotes **Quality Circles** in Japan - **1980s**: U.S. companies begin adopting TQM after observing Japanese success (the "Quality Crisis") - **1987**: **ISO 9000** series published by ISO (International Organization for Standardization) - **1988**: Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award established in the U.S. - **1986**: Motorola develops **Six Sigma** (formalized under Jack Welch at GE in 1995) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Era-Wise Breakdown of Quality Evolution #### **A. The Inspection Era (Pre-1900s to 1930s)** - **Origin**: Craft guilds of medieval Europe practiced inspection as part of apprenticeship. - **Industrial Revolution (1760–1840)**: Mass production made 100% inspection impossible, leading to **sampling** and **specialized inspectors**. - **Frederick W. Taylor (1911)**: Introduced *Scientific Management* — separated planning from execution. - Principles: Time studies, standardization, division of labour. - Limitation: Quality remained the **inspector's burden**, not the worker's. - **World War I (1914–1918)**: Mass production of munitions exposed the inadequacy of inspection-based quality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Limitation of Inspection Era**: Quality was treated as a **"policing function"** — defects were detected after production, not prevented. Cost of rework and scrap was enormous. #### **B. The Quality Control Era (1930s to 1960s)** - **Walter A. Shewhart (Bell Labs, 1924)**: Founded **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**. - Introduced the **control chart** to distinguish between *common cause* and *special cause* variation. - His famous **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — also known as the **Deming Wheel** — became the foundation for continuous improvement. $$ \text{Process Variation} = \text{Common Cause Variation} + \text{Special Cause Variation} $$ - **World War II (1939–1945)**: U.S. military adopted SQC for weapons production. - Deming trained thousands of American engineers in statistical methods. - **Post-War U.S.**: Management largely **abandoned** SQC, focusing on sales volume. This was a strategic error. #### **C. The Quality Assurance Era (1960s to 1980s)** - **Shift in philosophy**: From detecting defects to **preventing** them system-wide. - **Armand V. Feigenbaum (1956, 1961)**: Coined the term **"Total Quality Control (TQC)"** in his book *Total Quality Control* (1956, revised 1961, 1983). - Argued quality is **everyone's responsibility**, not just the QA department. - **Joseph M. Juran**: Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** (1962, *Juran's Quality Control Handbook*). - Introduced the **Juran Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement. - **Philip B. Crosby (1979)**: Advocated **"Zero Defects"** and **"Quality is Free"**. - Famous quote: *"Quality is conformance to requirements."* - Emphasized the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — prevention is cheaper than rework. #### **D. The Total Quality Management Era (1980s – Present)** - **Genichi Taguchi (Japan, 1950s–1980s)**: Developed **Taguchi Loss Function**. - Defined quality loss as occurring the moment a product deviates from target — even within specification limits. $$ L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2 $$ Where $L(y)$ is the quality loss, $y$ is the actual characteristic value, $m$ is the target value, and $k$ is a proportionality constant. - **Kaoru Ishikawa**: Pioneered **Quality Circles** (small groups of workers solving work-related problems) and the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** for root cause analysis. - **Genichi Taguchi & Off-Line Quality Control**: Quality must be designed into the product, not inspected in. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Reference Table for KTU Examination** | Concept / Formula | Expression | Era | Purpose | Key Proponent | |---|---|---|---|---| | Taguchi Loss Function | $L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$ | TQM | Quantify quality loss | Taguchi | | Common + Special Cause | $\sigma^2_{\text{total}} = \sigma^2_{\text{common}} + \sigma^2_{\text{special}}$ | QC | Variation decomposition | Shewhart | | Cost of Poor Quality | $COPQ = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$ | QA/TQM | Economic justification | Crosby / Juran | | Six Sigma Level | $\text{Defects} \leq 3.4 \text{ per million opportunities (DPMO)}$ | Modern TQM | Performance metric | Motorola / GE | | PDCA Cycle | Plan $\to$ Do $\to$ Check $\to$ Act | QC onward | Continuous improvement | Shewhart / Deming | | Juran Trilogy | Planning $\to$ Control $\to$ Improvement | QA onward | Quality management framework | Juran | | 1.5 Sigma Shift | $Z_{\text{LT}} = Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5$ | Six Sigma | Long-term process capability | Motorola | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Note on Sigma Tables**: In the KTU exam, when asked to compute process capability, always use **$Z_{\text{bench}} = 1.5$** for the long-term shift unless otherwise specified. ### 2.3 Engineering Utility of This Knowledge Understanding quality history is not merely academic — it serves critical engineering purposes: - **Diagnostic Tool**: Helps engineers identify *which era's mentality* their organization is trapped in (still in Inspection? → Upgrade needed). - **Framework Selection**: Choosing between Six Sigma, TQM, Lean, or ISO 9001 depends on historical context. - **Cultural Change Management**: Japanese success story provides a **blueprint** for cultural transformation in Indian manufacturing firms. - **Cost Justification**: Demonstrates that investment in prevention yields **exponential ROI** compared to inspection. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Function The Taguchi Loss Function is a foundational concept that bridges historical theory and modern engineering practice. Below is an exhaustive step-by-step derivation suitable for a 14-mark KTU answer. #### **Step 1: Classical Definition of Quality** In the Inspection and QC eras, quality was defined as a **binary concept** — a product is either *within specification* or *outside specification*. There is no loss if $y$ is within limits, and total loss if $y$ exceeds them. $$ L_{\text{classical}}(y) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } LSL \leq y \leq USL \\ \infty & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ This binary model is unrealistic because customers experience varying levels of satisfaction even within specification limits. #### **Step 2: Taguchi's Continuous Loss Model** Taguchi proposed that quality loss is a **continuous, quadratic function** of the deviation from target $m$. We begin by postulating: $$ L(y) = f(y - m) $$ #### **Step 3: Apply Symmetry Constraint** Quality loss must be symmetric around the target — deviation in either direction causes equal loss (a product that is too short is just as bad as one too long in many cases). Therefore $f$ is an even function: $$ L(y) = f(\vert y - m \vert) $$ #### **Step 4: Apply Smoothness and Minimum Conditions** - $L(y)$ must attain its **minimum at** $y = m$ (zero loss at target). - $L(y)$ must be smooth and differentiable. - Higher-order Taylor expansion around $m$: $$ L(y) = L(m) + L'(m)(y - m) + \frac{L''(m)}{2!}(y - m)^2 + \cdots $$ #### **Step 5: Evaluate Each Term at $y = m$** At the target: - $L(m) = 0$ (no loss at target) - $L'(m) = 0$ (minimum condition) - The leading non-zero term is the **second-order term**: $$ L(y) \approx \frac{L''(m)}{2!}(y - m)^2 $$ #### **Step 6: Define the Proportionality Constant** Let the constant $k$ absorb the factor: $$ k = \frac{L''(m)}{2} $$ #### **Step 7: Final Taguchi Loss Function** $$ \boxed{L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2} $$ Where: - $L(y)$ = Loss to society from a product with characteristic value $y$ - $k$ = Proportionality constant (cost coefficient), computed as $k = A / \Delta^2$ - $A$ = Total loss when deviation reaches the specification limit $\Delta$ - $y - m$ = Deviation from target #### **Step 8: Engineering Example (Numerical Illustration)** > **Problem**: A shaft diameter has target $m = 10.00$ mm and tolerance $\Delta = 0.05$ mm. The cost of replacing a shaft that exceeds tolerance is ₹400. Find $k$ and the loss when actual diameter is $10.03$ mm. **Solution:** $$ k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2} = \frac{400}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{400}{0.0025} = 160{,}000 \text{ per mm}^2 $$ $$ L(10.03) = 160{,}000 \times (10.03 - 10.00)^2 = 160{,}000 \times (0.03)^2 $$ $$ L(10.03) = 160{,}000 \times 0.0009 = ₹144 $$ **Interpretation**: Even though $10.03$ mm is within specification (LSL = 9.95, USL = 10.05), the customer/society still loses ₹144 due to deviation from target. This is Taguchi's revolutionary insight. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Quality Gurus | Aspect | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Feigenbaum | Ishikawa | Taguchi | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Era Active** | 1950s–1990s | 1950s–2000s | 1970s–2000s | 1950s–1980s | 1960s–1980s | 1950s–1980s | | **Core Idea** | System thinking, 14 Points | Fitness for Use, Trilogy | Zero Defects, Conformance | Total Quality Control | Quality Circles, Fishbone | Loss Function, Robust Design | | **Approach** | Statistical + Philosophical | Managerial | Motivational | Systems | Participative | Engineering | | **Primary Region** | USA / Japan | USA | USA | USA | Japan | Japan | | **Key Work** | *Out of the Crisis* (1982) | *Quality Control Handbook* (1951) | *Quality is Free* (1979) | *Total Quality Control* (1956) | *What is Total Quality Control?* (1985) | *Taguchi Methods* (1986) | | **KTU Frequency** | Very High | Very High | High | High | High | Medium | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Evolution of Quality Management — Block-Level Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Craft Era
Pre-1760
Skilled Artisans] --> B[Industrial Revolution
1760-1900
Mass Production Begins] B --> C[Inspection Era
1900-1930
Frederick Taylor
Separation of Planning and Work] C --> D[Quality Control Era
1930-1960
Shewhart Control Charts
PDCA Cycle
WWII Statistical Methods] D --> E[Quality Assurance Era
1960-1980
System-wide Prevention
Feigenbaum TQC
Juran Trilogy
Crosby Zero Defects] E --> F[TQM Era
1980-Present
Taguchi Loss Function
Ishikawa Quality Circles
Six Sigma
ISO 9000
Baldrige Award] F --> G[Modern Integration
Lean + Six Sigma + TQM
Industry 4.0 Quality
AI-Driven Quality Analytics] subgraph GURUS["Quality Gurus and Their Contributions"] G1[Deming 14 Points] G2[Juran Trilogy] G3[Crosby Zero Defects] G4[Feigenbaum TQC] G5[Ishikawa Fishbone] G6[Taguchi Loss Function] end F --- GURUS ``` ### 4.2 Deming's 14 Points — Hierarchical View ```mermaid flowchart TD DM[Deming 14 Points
Out of the Crisis 1982] --> P1[1 Create Constancy of Purpose] DM --> P2[2 Adopt New Philosophy] DM --> P3[3 Cease Dependence on Inspection] DM --> P4[4 End Lowest Tender Practice] DM --> P5[5 Improve Constantly Every Process] DM --> P6[6 Institute Training] DM --> P7[7 Institute Leadership] DM --> P8[8 Drive Out Fear] DM --> P9[9 Break Down Barriers] DM --> P10[10 Eliminate Slogans and Exhortations] DM --> P11[11 Eliminate Numerical Quotas] DM --> P12[12 Remove Barriers to Pride of Workmanship] DM --> P13[13 Institute Vigorous Education] DM --> P14[14 Put Everybody to Work on Transformation] ``` ### 4.3 PDCA Cycle — Continuous Improvement Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Plan
Identify Problem
Set Objectives
Analyze Process] --> D[Do
Implement Plan
Execute on Small Scale
Collect Data] D --> C[Check
Compare Results
to Objectives
Identify Gaps] C --> A[Act
Standardize if Successful
Revise if Failed
Continue Improvement] A --> P ``` ### 4.4 Juran Trilogy — Quality Management Framework ```mermaid flowchart TD J[Juran Trilogy] --> QP[Quality Planning
Identify Customers
Determine Needs
Develop Product Features
Establish Goals] J --> QC[Quality Control
Evaluate Actual Performance
Compare to Goals
Act on Differences] J --> QI[Quality Improvement
Establish Infrastructure
Identify Improvement Projects
Establish Project Teams] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** *Define the concept of "Total Quality Control" as introduced by Armand Feigenbaum. Why was it considered revolutionary compared to the Inspection Era approach?* **Model Answer:** Armand V. Feigenbaum, in his 1956 book *Total Quality Control*, defined TQC as *"an effective system for integrating the quality development, quality maintenance, and quality improvement efforts of the various groups in an organization so as to enable production and service at the most economical levels which allow for full customer satisfaction."* **Revolutionary Aspects (compared to Inspection Era):** 1. **Whole-system responsibility**: Quality is no longer the sole duty of the inspection department — it becomes everyone's responsibility, from the CEO to the shop-floor worker. 2. **Customer focus**: Quality is defined by customer requirements, not by internal specifications. 3. **Economical levels**: Quality must be balanced with cost-effectiveness. 4. **Integrated effort**: All departments (engineering, production, marketing, service) must collaborate. 5. **Prevention-oriented**: Moves away from "detect defects" to "prevent defects." *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for revolutionary aspects)* #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *State the Taguchi Loss Function. Explain its significance in modern quality management.* **Model Answer:** The Taguchi Loss Function, developed by Genichi Taguchi, is given by: $$ L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2 $$ Where $L(y)$ is the quality loss, $k$ is the proportionality constant, $y$ is the actual value of the quality characteristic, and $m$ is the target value. **Significance:** 1. It abandons the traditional binary view (within/outside specification) and treats quality as a continuous variable. 2. It establishes that **any deviation from target causes loss** to society — even within specification limits. 3. It provides an **economic justification** for continuous improvement and robust design. 4. It encourages designing processes that are **insensitive to noise factors** (robust design). 5. It shifts the engineer's mindset from "meeting tolerances" to "hitting the target consistently." *(3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for significance)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning** > - In historical review questions, students often **memorize names without context**. Always link each guru to a *specific concept* (e.g., "Deming → 14 Points," not just "Deming — quality guru"). > - Do **not confuse PDCA attribution**: It was developed by **Shewhart**, popularized by **Deming** — both names are acceptable in KTU answers. > - Avoid writing paragraphs without structure. Use **headings and bullet points** — KTU examiners scan for key terms. > - For numerical questions on the Taguchi Loss Function, **always show the calculation of $k$ first** before computing the loss. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **(a)** *Discuss the evolution of quality management from the Inspection Era to the Total Quality Management Era. Highlight the key contributions of Walter Shewhart and W. Edwards Deming.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Explain Joseph Juran's Quality Trilogy with a suitable diagram. Discuss its relevance in modern manufacturing organizations.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Evolution of Quality Management:** **1. Inspection Era (Pre-1930s):** - Craft era relied on skilled artisans producing one-of-a-kind items. - Industrial Revolution brought mass production, making 100% inspection impractical. - Frederick Taylor's *Scientific Management* (1911) separated planning from execution, formalizing inspection as a separate function. - Quality was the inspector's job; defects were detected after production. - Limitation: High scrap, rework, and customer dissatisfaction. **2. Quality Control Era (1930s–1960s):** - **Walter A. Shewhart (1924)** introduced Statistical Quality Control at Bell Labs — the **first major paradigm shift**. - Shewhart developed the **control chart** to distinguish between *common cause* (inherent) and *special cause* (assignable) variation. - His 1931 book *Economic Control of Quality of Manufactured Product* laid the foundation. - Shewhart also formulated the **PDCA cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act), the universal framework for continuous improvement. - During WWII, statistical methods were extensively used in U.S. munitions production. **3. Quality Assurance Era (1960s–1980s):** - Feigenbaum introduced Total Quality Control (TQC) — quality is everyone's responsibility. - Juran emphasized **fitness for use** and the trilogy approach. - Crosby's **Zero Defects** philosophy stressed prevention over detection. - Shift from inspection to **system-wide prevention**. **4. Total Quality Management Era (1980s–Present):** - TQM integrates customer focus, continuous improvement, employee involvement, and fact-based decision-making. - Six Sigma, ISO 9000, and Baldrige Award institutionalized quality globally. **W. Edwards Deming's Contributions:** - Deming's **14 Points for Management** (1982, *Out of the Crisis*) became the cornerstone of TQM philosophy. - Famous quote: *"Learning is not compulsory... neither is survival."* - He trained thousands of Japanese engineers (1950 onward) — leading to Japan's quality revolution. - Deming emphasized **system thinking**: 94% of problems are management's responsibility, not workers'. - He extended Shewhart's PDCA into the **Deming Wheel** (sometimes called Deming Cycle). - Deming's philosophy focused on **constancy of purpose**, **pride of workmanship**, and **driving out fear**. **(b) Juran's Quality Trilogy:** The Juran Trilogy, developed by Joseph M. Juran, consists of three interconnected processes that together form a complete quality management system: **1. Quality Planning:** - Identify the customers (internal and external). - Determine customer needs. - Develop product features that respond to those needs. - Establish quality goals. - Develop a process to produce the product. **2. Quality Control:** - Evaluate actual quality performance. - Compare actual performance with quality goals. - Act on the difference (reduce gap between actual and goal). **3. Quality Improvement:** - Establish the infrastructure for continuous improvement. - Identify specific improvement projects. - Establish project teams with clear charters. - Provide resources, training, and motivation. - Monitor and celebrate progress. **Relevance in Modern Manufacturing:** - Provides a **structured roadmap** for any quality initiative. - Aligns perfectly with **ISO 9001** clauses on planning, performance evaluation, and continual improvement. - Used by **Toyota, Bosch, and Tata** in their manufacturing systems. - Integrates with **Lean Six Sigma** — Quality Planning = Define/Measure, Quality Control = Control, Quality Improvement = Analyze/Improve. **Diagram (textual representation):** $$\text{Juran Trilogy} = \begin{cases} \text{Quality Planning (What to do?)} \\ \text{Quality Control (Are we doing it?)} \\ \text{Quality Improvement (How to do better?)} \end{cases}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a)** *Explain the contributions of Philip Crosby and Kaoru Ishikawa to the development of TQM. Discuss their key concepts.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Derive the Taguchi Loss Function. A shaft diameter has target value 25 mm and tolerance ±0.10 mm. The cost of scrapping a shaft that exceeds tolerance is ₹900. Calculate the loss to society when a shaft has actual diameter 24.92 mm.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Philip Crosby's Contributions:** - Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001) was an American quality philosopher who authored *Quality is Free* (1979) and *Quality Without Tears* (1984). - His **"Zero Defects"** concept, introduced in 1979 at Martin Marietta, declared that *"the standard of performance has been zero defects — not 'that's close enough.'"* - His famous **"Four Absolutes of Quality"** are: 1. Quality = **conformance to requirements** (not elegance). 2. Prevention, not inspection. 3. Performance standard = **Zero Defects**. 4. Measure quality by **the price of non-conformance**. - **Cost of Quality (COQ)** framework: Crosby classified costs into **Cost of Conformance (Prevention + Appraisal)** and **Cost of Non-Conformance (Internal + External Failure)**. - Famous quote: *"Quality is free. It's not a gift, but it is free. What costs money are the unquality things — all the actions that involve not doing jobs right the first time."* **Kaoru Ishikawa's Contributions:** - Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989) was a Japanese quality pioneer. - He developed the **Ishikawa Diagram** (also called **Fishbone Diagram** or **Cause-and-Effect Diagram**) for root cause analysis. - Categories of causes: 4Ms (Man, Machine, Material, Method) + Measurement + Environment. - He championed **Quality Circles (QCs)** — small groups of 6–12 workers who meet regularly to solve work-related problems. - Ishikawa emphasized **company-wide quality control (CWQC)** — quality is not just a manufacturing issue but spans all functions. - He promoted the philosophy that *"quality begins and ends with education."* - His 1985 book *What is Total Quality Control?* introduced the phrase in Japan. **Comparison:** Crosby focused on **management commitment and prevention economics** (Western approach), while Ishikawa focused on **worker participation and tools** (Japanese approach). Together, they provided the cultural and operational pillars of modern TQM. **(b) Derivation of Taguchi Loss Function:** [See Section 3.1 for the complete derivation. Key steps summarized:] 1. Postulate $L(y) = f(y - m)$. 2. Apply symmetry → even function. 3. Taylor expand around $m$ → $L(y) \approx \frac{L''(m)}{2}(y-m)^2$. 4. Define $k = \frac{L''(m)}{2}$ → $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$. **Numerical Problem Solution:** **Given:** - Target $m = 25$ mm - Tolerance $\Delta = \pm 0.10$ mm - Cost at specification limit $A = ₹900$ - Actual diameter $y = 24.92$ mm **Step 1:** Calculate $k$ $$ k = \frac{A}{\Delta^2} = \frac{900}{(0.10)^2} = \frac{900}{0.01} = 90{,}000 \text{ per mm}^2 $$ **Step 2:** Calculate deviation $$ y - m = 24.92 - 25.00 = -0.08 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate loss $$ L(24.92) = k \cdot (y - m)^2 = 90{,}000 \times (-0.08)^2 $$ $$ L(24.92) = 90{,}000 \times 0.0064 = ₹576 $$ **Answer:** The loss to society when the shaft diameter is $24.92$ mm is **₹576**, even though this value is technically within specification limits (24.90 to 25.10 mm). This demonstrates Taguchi's key insight — any deviation from target incurs loss. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Historical Review (Module 1)** **✓ Key Eras and Their Order:** 1. Inspection Era → 2. Quality Control Era → 3. Quality Assurance Era → 4. TQM Era **✓ Quality Gurus and Their Tag:** - **Deming**: 14 Points, System Thinking, PDCA (Deming Wheel), 94% management responsibility - **Juran**: Fitness for Use, Juran Trilogy (Planning-Control-Improvement) - **Crosby**: Zero Defects, Quality is Free, Conformance to Requirements - **Feigenbaum**: Total Quality Control (TQC) — first coined 1956 - **Ishikawa**: Quality Circles, Fishbone Diagram, 4Ms/6Ms - **Taguchi**: Loss Function $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$, Robust Design, Off-line QC - **Shewhart**: Control Charts, PDCA cycle origin, Common vs Special cause variation **✓ Must-Remember Dates:** - **1924**: Shewhart's control chart - **1950**: Deming's Japan lectures - **1956**: Feigenbaum's TQC - **1979**: Crosby's Zero Defects - **1987**: ISO 9000 series - **1988**: Baldrige Award - **1995**: Six Sigma at GE (Jack Welch) **✓ Critical Formulas:** - Taguchi Loss: $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$ - $k = A / \Delta^2$ - PDCA: Plan-Do-Check-Act - Juran Trilogy: Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement **✓ KTU Exam Tip:** Always frame your answer in **chronological order** when discussing historical evolution. Examiners reward **structured answers with era headings**. **✓ Common Confusions to Avoid:** - PDCA was created by **Shewhart**, popularized by **Deming** — both credits acceptable - **TQC** (Feigenbaum, 1956) ≠ **TQM** (Japanese evolution, 1980s) - **Zero Defects** ≠ Zero Variation — it's a *standard of performance*, not a mathematical claim - Six Sigma's **1.5 sigma shift** accounts for long-term drift, not a fixed industry-wide assumption unique to one company ---

topics.title: Principles of TQM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of TQM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of TQM

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to front-line workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Reference)**
> "TQM is a management approach that originated in the 1950s and has steadily become more popular since the early 1980s. It is a comprehensive, integrated, organization-wide philosophy designed to continuously improve the quality of products, services, and processes by focusing on the customer, the process, and the people."

**Quality (per ISO 9000:2015):** The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements.

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of TQM

TQM rests on **three universal pillars** that support the entire philosophy:

| Pillar | Focus | Key Idea |
|--------|-------|----------|
| **Customer** | External & Internal | Quality is defined by the customer |
| **Process** | Workflow Systems | Quality is built into the process, not inspected in |
| **People** | Employee Empowerment | Every employee is responsible for quality |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: A Five-Star Restaurant**
>
> Imagine a five-star restaurant kitchen:
> - **Customer Focus** → The chef tastes every dish before serving. The customer is the ultimate judge.
> - **Total Employee Involvement** → The dishwasher, the prep cook, the server, and the chef all care equally about a perfect dining experience.
> - **Continuous Improvement** → The chef experiments daily, refining recipes based on customer feedback.
> - **Process Approach** → From sourcing ingredients → prep → cook → plate → serve, every step is standardized and optimized.
> - **Fact-Based Decisions** → The chef tracks which dishes get returned, which sell the most, and adjusts the menu based on data, not guesswork.
>
> A TQM organization runs exactly like this kitchen — every "station" (department) is interconnected, customer-driven, and constantly improving.

## 1.4 Origin & Evolution of TQM

- **1950s:** W. Edwards Deming and Joseph Juran introduced quality concepts to Japanese industry.
- **1970s–80s:** Japanese products (Toyota, Sony) dominated global markets due to superior quality.
- **1980s:** Western companies adopted TQM to compete. The **Malcolm Baldrige Award (1987)** and **ISO 9000 (1987)** formalized the movement.
- **2000s–Present:** TQM evolved into integrated systems like Six Sigma, Lean, and EFQM Excellence Model.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Principles of TQM (KTU High-Yield)

Modern TQM is built upon **8 universally accepted principles** (derived from ISO 9000 and the works of Deming, Juran, and Crosby):

### Principle 1: Customer Focus
- The customer (external + internal) ultimately defines quality.
- Two types of customers: **External** (end users) and **Internal** (next process/employee).
- Organizations must understand current and future customer needs, measure satisfaction, and act on results.

### Principle 2: Leadership & Top Management Commitment
- Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and the internal environment.
- Without committed leadership, TQM initiatives collapse.

### Principle 3: Involvement of People (Total Employee Involvement)
- Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels are the essence of an organization.
- Recognition, empowerment, and training drive involvement.

### Principle 4: Process Approach
- A desired result is achieved more efficiently when related resources and activities are managed as a process.
- **Process = Input → Process → Output** (IPO model).

### Principle 5: System Approach to Management
- Identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes as a system improves effectiveness.

### Principle 6: Continual Improvement (Kaizen)
- A permanent objective of the organization. Key Japanese term: **Kaizen** (改善).
- Uses the **PDCA Cycle** (Deming/Shewhart Cycle).

### Principle 7: Factual (Fact-Based) Approach to Decision Making
- Effective decisions are based on the logical and intuitive analysis of data and information.
- Tools: Statistical Process Control (SPC), Pareto Analysis, Fishbone Diagram.

### Principle 8: Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships
- An organization and its suppliers are interdependent. A win-win relationship enhances the ability of both to create value.

## 2.2 Deming's 14 Points (Frequently Asked in KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's 14 Points — Exam-Favorite**

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on the basis of price tag alone.
5. Improve constantly every process for planning, production, and service.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous program of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

## 2.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy (Joseph M. Juran)

| Trilogy Step | Focus | Activities |
|--------------|-------|------------|
| **Quality Planning** | Identify customers & their needs | Develop product/process to meet needs |
| **Quality Control** | Evaluate actual performance | Compare actual vs. goals, act on differences |
| **Quality Improvement** | Establish infrastructure | Continuously improve processes |

## 2.4 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality (Philip B. Crosby)

- **Quality** = Conformance to requirements (not "goodness").
- The system of quality is **prevention**, not inspection.
- The **performance standard** is **Zero Defects (ZD)**.
- The **measure of quality** is the **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Standard | Description |
|---------|--------------------|--------------|
| Cost of Quality (COQ) | $\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_F + C_E$ | Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure |
| Prevention Cost ($C_P$) | Training, planning, quality design | Cheapest to invest early |
| Appraisal Cost ($C_A$) | Inspection, testing, auditing | Detects defects |
| Internal Failure ($C_F$) | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | Cost of defects found internally |
| External Failure ($C_E$) | Warranty, returns, legal claims | Cost of defects reaching customer |
| Quality Level (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%$ | Yield / Conformance rate |
| Six Sigma Defect Rate | $3.4 \text{ defects per million opportunities (DPMO)}$ | $6\sigma = 3.4$ DPMO |
| Process Capability ($C_p$) | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Measures process spread vs. tolerance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of COQ:** Manufacturing giants like Toyota, Samsung, and Bosch use COQ analysis to demonstrate that every **\$1 spent on prevention saves \$10–\$100** in failure costs. This is the cornerstone financial justification for TQM investment.

## 2.6 Comparison of Quality Gurus (High-Yield Table)

| Guru | Core Idea | Famous Contribution |
|------|-----------|---------------------|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | Statistical thinking, system variation | PDCA Cycle, 14 Points |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Quality as fitness for use | Juran Trilogy, Quality Planning |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Conformance to requirements | Zero Defects, Quality is Free |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality circles, cause-effect | Fishbone Diagram |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss to society from variability | Taguchi Loss Function |
| **Armand Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control | Organizational-wide quality |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle is the engine of continual improvement. It can be expressed as a four-stage iterative mathematical model:

$$
\text{Improvement}_n = f(P_n, D_n, C_n, A_n)
$$

Where each stage has a defined input and output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Plan)}: \quad & \text{Identify problem} \rightarrow \text{Analyze data} \rightarrow \text{Formulate plan} \\
& I_{\text{plan}} = \{ \text{Goal}, \text{Method}, \text{Resources} \} \\
\text{Stage 2 (Do)}: \quad & \text{Execute the plan on a small scale} \\
& O_{\text{do}} = \text{Process Output (pilot data)} \\
\text{Stage 3 (Check)}: \quad & \text{Compare output against expected goal} \\
& \Delta = \vert O_{\text{do}} - O_{\text{expected}} \vert \\
\text{Stage 4 (Act)}: \quad & \text{Standardize if successful, else revise plan} \\
& \text{Improvement}_{n+1} = \text{Improvement}_n + \Delta_{\text{corrective}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step explanation:**
- **Plan (P):** Establish objectives and processes necessary to deliver results (analyze the problem, gather baseline data).
- **Do (D):** Execute the pilot plan. Collect data carefully.
- **Check (C):** Evaluate the data. Did the change improve the process? Quantify the gap $\Delta$.
- **Act (A):** If the change worked → standardize (make it a new SOP). If it failed → loop back to Plan with new knowledge.

> [!NOTE]
> The PDCA cycle is **iterative and recursive** — every completed cycle feeds the next, leading to compounded improvement over time.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost of Quality (COQ) Model

The COQ model is mathematically decomposed as:

$$
\text{COQ}_{\text{Total}} = C_P + C_A + C_F + C_E
$$

**Detailed component derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Training}_i + \text{Quality Planning}_i + \text{Process Design}_i) \\
C_A &= \sum_{j=1}^{m} (\text{Inspection}_j + \text{Testing}_j + \text{Auditing}_j) \\
C_F &= \sum_{k=1}^{p} (\text{Rework}_k + \text{Scrap}_k + \text{Re-inspection}_k) \\
C_E &= \sum_{l=1}^{q} (\text{Warranty Claims}_l + \text{Returns}_l + \text{Legal Costs}_l)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimization Principle:** As $C_P$ increases, $C_F$ and $C_E$ decrease more than proportionally. The **optimal quality investment** occurs at the point where $\dfrac{d(\text{COQ})}{d(C_P)} = 0$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Apply Each Principle

| Situation / Problem | Applicable TQM Principle | Tool / Method |
|--------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| Frequent customer complaints | Customer Focus | Voice of Customer (VOC), QFD |
| High defect rate on shop floor | Continual Improvement | PDCA, Kaizen |
| Departmental silos & conflicts | System Approach | Cross-functional teams |
| Employee resistance to change | People Involvement | Quality Circles, Training |
| Supplier delivery delays | Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships | Strategic alliances |
| Unclear decision-making process | Fact-Based Decision Making | SPC, Pareto, Histograms |
| Lack of clear vision | Leadership | Hoshin Kanri, Policy Deployment |
| Inconsistent product output | Process Approach | SIPOC, Process Mapping |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 8 TQM Principles — Interrelationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pillar fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef principle fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef enabler fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    LEAD[Leadership and Top Management Commitment]:::pillar
    CUST[Customer Focus External and Internal]:::pillar
    PEOP[Involvement of People]:::pillar
    PROC[Process Approach]:::enabler
    SYST[System Approach to Management]:::enabler
    IMP[Continual Improvement Kaizen]:::enabler
    FACT[Factual Approach to Decision Making]:::enabler
    SUPP[Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships]:::enabler

    LEAD --> CUST
    LEAD --> PEOP
    LEAD --> IMP
    CUST --> PROC
    PEOP --> IMP
    PROC --> SYST
    SYST --> FACT
    IMP --> FACT
    FACT --> SUPP
    SUPP --> CUST
```

## 4.2 The PDCA Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pdca fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff

    P[PLAN Identify problem Analyze Formulate hypothesis]:::pdca
    D[DO Execute pilot Implement small scale]:::pdca
    C[CHECK Evaluate Compare data vs target]:::pdca
    A[ACT Standardize or Revise Loop back to Plan]:::pdca

    P --> D
    D --> C
    C --> A
    A --> P
```

## 4.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef juran fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef juran2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#c00000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    QP[QUALITY PLANNING Establish goals Identify customers]:::juran
    QC[QUALITY CONTROL Evaluate actual performance Compare with goals]:::juran
    QI[QUALITY IMPROVEMENT Establish infrastructure for improvement]:::juran

    QP --> QC
    QC --> QI
    QI --> QP
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TQM Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef top fill:#0b5394,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef mid fill:#6fa8dc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef base fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    TM[TOP MANAGEMENT Vision Mission Policy]:::top
    QM[QUALITY MANAGER QMS ISO 9001 Audit]:::mid
    TF[CROSS FUNCTIONAL TEAMS Kaizen Projects]:::mid
    EM[EMPLOYEES Quality Circles Suggestion System]:::base
    SP[SUPPLIERS Strategic Partners Long Term Contracts]:::base
    CU[CUSTOMERS External Internal Feedback VOC]:::base

    TM --> QM
    TM --> TF
    QM --> EM
    QM --> SP
    TF --> EM
    EM --> CU
    SP --> CU
    CU -->|Feedback| TM
```

## 4.5 The Customer Chain (External + Internal Customers)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef ext fill:#cc0000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef int fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    EXT[External Customer End User Buyer]:::ext
    SALES[Internal Sales Team]:::int
    PROD[Internal Production Team]:::int
    QC[Internal Quality Team]:::int
    PROC[Internal Procurement Team]:::int
    SUPP[Supplier External]:::ext

    PROC -->|Materials| SUPP
    SUPP -->|Inputs| PROC
    PROC -->|Materials| QC
    QC -->|Inspected Goods| PROD
    PROD -->|Finished Goods| SALES
    SALES -->|Delivered Product| EXT
    EXT -->|VOC Feedback| SALES
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four principles of TQM.**

**Model Answer:**
TQM is a management philosophy that focuses on continuously improving products, services, and processes by involving all members of an organization to satisfy customer needs.

**Four principles:**
1. Customer focus
2. Total employee involvement
3. Process approach
4. Continual improvement
