topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kaizen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Kaizen** (改善) is a Japanese management philosophy and methodology that embodies the principle of **continuous, incremental improvement** involving everyone in the organization — from top management to shop-floor workers. The word is derived from two Japanese kanji characters:

- **改 (kai)** = "to change" or "to modify"
- **善 (zen)** = "good" or "for the better"

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Kaizen is formally defined as a **process-oriented, employee-driven, customer-focused quality philosophy** that seeks to eliminate waste (*muda*), inconsistency (*mura*), and overburden (*muri*) through small, low-cost, ongoing improvements rather than large, capital-intensive technological innovations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Kaizen is a system of continuous improvement in which all employees — managers and workers alike — participate proactively. It relies on small, frequent, low-cost improvements built on practical common sense, standardized processes, and the scientific PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle."*

The philosophy was popularized globally by **Masaaki Imai** through his seminal 1986 work *"Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success,"* where he positioned Kaizen as the unifying umbrella strategy under which several Japanese quality tools — including Just-In-Time (JIT), Total Productive Maintenance (TPM), Quality Circles, and the 5S methodology — operate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of Kaizen like **maintaining a garden every single day**:

Imagine you own a small home garden. There are two ways to keep it beautiful:

1. **The "Innovation" Approach** — Wait until the garden is overgrown and withered, then hire a landscaping company to bulldoze everything and install a brand-new automated irrigation system, imported soil, and exotic plants. This is expensive, disruptive, and takes weeks.

2. **The "Kaizen" Approach** — Every morning, spend **just 15 minutes** pulling out a few weeds, watering the plants, trimming dead leaves, and adjusting the soil. The garden never becomes overgrown, costs almost nothing, and gradually becomes a paradise over months.

Kaizen is the **second approach applied to organizations**. Instead of waiting for a massive overhaul every few years, employees make **small, daily improvements** — tightening a loose bolt, rearranging a workstation, simplifying a form, removing one unnecessary step in a process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Kaizen = Many small **steps (踏石 - stepping stones)** rather than one giant **leap**. In Japanese culture, this is captured by the proverb *"踏石一つ一つが道を作る"* — "Each stepping stone makes the path."

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Boundary Conditions

| Parameter | Standard Value in Kaizen Context |
|-----------|-------------------------------|
| Improvement Cost per Idea | **Typically zero or near-zero capital** |
| Implementation Timeframe | **Days to weeks (not months)** |
| Originator of Improvement | **Anyone in the organization** |
| Focus | **Process (not people)** |
| Frequency | **Continuous / daily** |
| Magnitude | **Small, incremental (1–10% gains)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD PRINCIPLE SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Core Principles of Kaizen (KTU Frequently Asked)

Masaaki Imai identified **five foundational principles** that govern every genuine Kaizen initiative. These are heavily tested in KTU Part A questions.

### Principle 1 — Process-Oriented Thinking
Kaizen believes that **bad results originate from bad processes**, not bad people. To improve outcomes, you must first improve the process that generates them.

- **Why?** Punishing individuals destroys morale; fixing processes yields permanent gains.
- **How?** Use process mapping, value-stream mapping, and flowcharts before making changes.

### Principle 2 — PDCA / Deming Cycle as the Operating Engine
Every Kaizen improvement must follow the four-phase scientific loop:
1. **Plan (P)** — Identify the problem, analyze root cause, propose a countermeasure.
2. **Do (D)** — Implement the countermeasure on a small scale (pilot).
3. **Check (C)** — Verify whether the result matched the plan using measurable data.
4. **Act (A)** — Standardize if successful; revise the plan and repeat if not.

### Principle 3 — Gemba (現場) — Go to the Real Place
Decisions and observations must be made at the **actual site of work** — the shop floor, the call center, the hospital ward — not in distant boardrooms. Only at *gemba* can the true nature of a problem be understood.

### Principle 4 — Elimination of *Muda* (Waste)
Kaizen relentlessly targets the **seven classical wastes (七つの無駄 — nanatsu no muda)**:

| # | Type of Waste (Japanese) | English Meaning | Real Example |
|---|--------------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Overproduction** | Producing more than demanded | Excess inventory stacking |
| 2 | **Waiting** | Idle time of people/machines | Operator waiting for material |
| 3 | **Transport** | Unnecessary movement of goods | Forklift moving parts twice |
| 4 | **Over-processing** | Doing more work than required | Excessive polishing beyond spec |
| 5 | **Inventory** | Excess raw material / WIP | Piles of unsold stock |
| 6 | **Motion** | Unnecessary human movement | Worker walking 10 m for a tool |
| 7 | **Defects** | Rework and scrap | Defective products returned |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extended (8th) Waste — Now widely accepted:** **Unused employee creativity (Skills)**. Workers often have the best ideas but are never asked.

### Principle 5 — Standardization as the Foundation
Kaizen **requires** an existing standard to improve upon. Without a baseline, "improvement" is meaningless. Therefore, every successful Kaizen cycle ends by **raising the standard** for the next iteration.

$$\text{Standard} \xrightarrow{\text{Kaizen}} \text{Improved Standard} \xrightarrow{\text{Kaizen}} \text{Further Improved Standard} \dots$$

## 2.2 Kaizen vs. Innovation — The Critical Contrast (KTU High-Yield Table)

This comparison is one of the **most frequently asked 14-mark questions** in KTU ESE.

| Dimension | **Kaizen** | **Innovation** |
|-----------|------------|----------------|
| Origin | Japanese: "Continuous improvement" | Western: "Radical breakthrough" |
| Magnitude | Small, incremental | Large, discontinuous |
| Time Horizon | Continuous, ongoing | Periodic, sporadic |
| Investment | **Low cost / zero capital** | **High capital expenditure** |
| Risk | Low — easy to revert | High — failure is costly |
| Source of Ideas | **Bottom-up (every employee)** | **Top-down (R&D specialists)** |
| Implementation Speed | Fast (days) | Slow (months–years) |
| Focus | **Process & people** | **Product & technology** |
| Approach | Maintain & improve existing | Replace with new |
| Workers' Attitude | "How can **we** do this better?" | "How can **they** find a new way?" |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Kaizen and Innovation are **complementary**, not opposing. Both are needed. Kaizen maintains daily operations; Innovation provides the leap forward.

## 2.3 The 5S Framework (Kaizen's Foundation Tool)

**5S** is the bedrock methodology that enables Kaizen to function in a workplace. Each "S" denotes a Japanese word beginning with that letter.

| Step | Japanese | English | Purpose |
|------|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Remove unnecessary items from workplace |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange remaining items for easy access |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace thoroughly |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish norms for the first three S |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Discipline to maintain the previous four S |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Symbolic Representation)

While Kaizen is qualitative, KTU often tests the **Kaizen Cost formula** used to measure savings:

$$\Delta C_{\text{Kaizen}} = C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}$$

Where:
- $\Delta C_{\text{Kaizen}}$ = Cost reduction achieved by Kaizen
- $C_{\text{before}}$ = Cost per unit before improvement (in ₹ / \$)
- $C_{\text{after}}$ = Cost per unit after improvement (in ₹ / \$)

**Percentage Improvement Formula:**

$$\eta_{\text{Kaizen}} = \left(\frac{C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}}{C_{\text{before}}}\right) \times 100 \%$$

**Kaizen Suggestion Productivity Rate:**

$$KPR = \frac{N_{\text{accepted suggestions}}}{N_{\text{total employees}} \times T_{\text{period}}}$$

Where $T_{\text{period}}$ is typically one month. A healthy Kaizen culture expects $KPR \geq 1.0$ suggestion per employee per month.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK & IMPLEMENTATION DERIVATIONS

## 3.1 The Kaizen Event (Blitz) — 10-Step Implementation Model

A **Kaizen Event** (also called a **Kaizen Blitz** or **Focused Improvement**) is a short, intensive, 3-to-5-day workshop where a cross-functional team applies the Kaizen methodology to a specific process. The following exhaustive derivation walks through every operational step:

### Step 1 — Topic Selection
$$\text{Topic} = \arg\max_{i} \left[ \text{Impact}_i \times \text{Feasibility}_i \right]$$
Choose the area with the highest combined business impact AND easiest implementation (typically bottlenecks, defects, or delays).

### Step 2 — Team Formation
A typical team:
- 1 Team Leader
- 3–6 Frontline workers from the target area
- 1 Facilitator (Kaizen-trained)
- 1 Management sponsor

### Step 3 — Baseline Measurement
Measure the current state using observable, quantifiable metrics:
$$S_{\text{baseline}} = \{ \text{Cycle time}, \text{Defect rate}\%, \text{Setup time}, \text{Distance travelled}, \text{WIP count} \}$$

### Step 4 — Value Stream Mapping (VSM)
Document the current state of every step from raw input to final output.

### Step 5 — Waste Identification
Map each step to one or more of the **7 muda** categories.

### Step 6 — Root Cause Analysis (5-Why Method)
For each identified waste, ask "Why?" **five times** to reach the root cause.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example of 5-Why:**
> - Problem: Machine stopped.
> - Why 1? — Fuse blew.
> - Why 2? — Bearing was overloaded.
> - Why 3? — Lubrication was insufficient.
> - Why 4? — Lubrication pump was not working.
> - Why 5? — Pump maintenance schedule was not followed.
> - **Root Cause:** Missing preventive maintenance SOP.

### Step 7 — Countermeasure Design
Brainstorm **low-cost, no-cost** solutions. Eliminate the root cause, not the symptom.

### Step 8 — Pilot Implementation (Plan–Do–Check–Act)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plan:} \quad & \text{Define countermeasure, baseline metric} \\
\text{Do:} \quad & \text{Implement on a small scale for a trial period} \\
\text{Check:} \quad & \text{Measure new value: } S_{\text{after}} \\
\text{Act:} \quad & \text{If } S_{\text{after}} < S_{\text{baseline}}, \text{ standardize. Else, replan.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 9 — Standardization
Update the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** with the new method. Train all affected employees.

### Step 10 — Replication & Recognition
Apply the same improvement to similar processes elsewhere. Publicly recognize the team.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** In a manufacturing cell, the cost of producing one component was **₹480**. After a Kaizen event, the cost reduced to **₹420**. The cell produces **12,000 units per month**.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $C_{\text{before}} = 480$ ₹
- $C_{\text{after}} = 420$ ₹
- $N = 12{,}000$ units/month

**Step 1 — Calculate cost reduction per unit:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta C_{\text{Kaizen}} &= C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}} \\
&= 480 - 420 \\
&= 60 \text{ ₹/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate percentage improvement:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Kaizen}} &= \left( \frac{60}{480} \right) \times 100 \% \\
&= 0.125 \times 100 \% \\
&= 12.5 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate monthly monetary savings:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{monthly}} &= \Delta C_{\text{Kaizen}} \times N \\
&= 60 \times 12{,}000 \\
&= 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate annual savings:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= S_{\text{monthly}} \times 12 \\
&= 7{,}20{,}000 \times 12 \\
&= 86{,}40{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} \\
&= 86.4 \text{ Lakhs/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** A single Kaizen event saves **₹60 per unit**, a **12.5% improvement**, totaling **₹86.4 Lakhs per year** — without any capital investment. This is the power of Kaizen.

## 3.3 PDCA Cycle — The Mathematical Representation

Kaizen's PDCA cycle can be expressed as a recursive improvement function:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{n+1} &= S_n + \Delta \text{Kaizen}_n \\
\text{where } \Delta \text{Kaizen}_n &\geq 0 \quad \forall n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $S_n$ = Standard at iteration $n$
- $\Delta \text{Kaizen}_n$ = Improvement achieved in cycle $n$
- $S_{n+1}$ = New, higher standard for the next cycle

Since Kaizen is **continuous**, as $n \to \infty$, the process approaches a state of perfection (theoretical asymptote).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Kaizen PDCA Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PLAN: Identify Problem & Root Cause"] --> B["DO: Pilot Implementation"]
    B --> C["CHECK: Measure Results vs Plan"]
    C --> D["ACT: Standardize if Successful"]
    D --> E["Standard Updated & Sustained"]
    E --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Japanese Quality Methodologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K["KAIZEN UMBRELLA STRATEGY"]
    K --> JIT["JUST IN TIME JIT"]
    K --> TPM["TOTAL PRODUCTIVE MAINTENANCE TPM"]
    K --> QC["QUALITY CIRCLES"]
    K --> FIVE_S["5S WORKPLACE ORGANIZATION"]
    K --> JIDOKA["JIDOKA AUTONOMATION"]
    K --> KAIZEN_EVT["KAIZEN EVENTS BLITZ"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 5S Implementation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["SEIRI SORT Remove unnecessary items"]
    S2["SEITON SET IN ORDER Arrange for easy access"]
    S3["SEISO SHINE Clean workplace"]
    S4["SEIKETSU STANDARDIZE Document norms"]
    S5["SHITSUKE SUSTAIN Train discipline"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S1
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The 7 Wastes (Muda) Elimination Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["KAIZEN WASTE IDENTIFICATION AT GEMBA"]
    W --> W1["Overproduction Excess Inventory"]
    W --> W2["Waiting Idle Time"]
    W --> W3["Transport Unnecessary Movement"]
    W --> W4["Overprocessing Excess Effort"]
    W --> W5["Inventory WIP Buildup"]
    W --> W6["Motion Operator Walking"]
    W --> W7["Defects Rework and Scrap"]
    W1 --> ACT["KAIZEN ACTION Low Cost Removal"]
    W2 --> ACT
    W3 --> ACT
    W4 --> ACT
    W5 --> ACT
    W6 --> ACT
    W7 --> ACT
    ACT --> STD["New Standard Created"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Kaizen Event Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PHASE1["PHASE 1 PREPARATION"] --> PHASE2["PHASE 2 EXECUTION"]
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3["PHASE 3 FOLLOWUP"]
    PHASE1 --> P1A["Select Topic & Metrics"]
    PHASE1 --> P1B["Form Cross Functional Team"]
    PHASE1 --> P1C["Train Team on Tools"]
    PHASE2 --> P2A["Map Current State VSM"]
    PHASE2 --> P2B["Identify Wastes 7 Muda"]
    PHASE2 --> P2C["5 Why Root Cause Analysis"]
    PHASE2 --> P2D["Design Countermeasures"]
    PHASE2 --> P2E["Pilot Test Changes"]
    PHASE3 --> P3A["Measure New Performance"]
    PHASE3 --> P3B["Standardize New SOP"]
    PHASE3 --> P3C["Replicate to Other Areas"]
    PHASE3 --> P3D["Recognize Team Contributions"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents an operational milestone. Arrows indicate sequential dependencies. The Kaizen Event is structured, scientific, and repeatable — not ad-hoc brainstorming.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Kaizen. Mention any two key features.**

**Model Answer:**
Kaizen is a Japanese management philosophy meaning **continuous improvement** (改 善), which involves every employee in small, incremental, low-cost improvements to processes, products, and services.

**Two key features:**
1. **Process-oriented** — Focuses on improving the process, not blaming individuals.
2. **Employee-driven** — Ideas originate from workers at all levels, not only top management.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two features = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the seven wastes (muda) targeted by Kaizen.**

**Model Answer:**
The **seven wastes** are:
1. Overproduction
2. Waiting
3. Transport (unnecessary)
4. Over-processing
5. Inventory (excess)
6. Motion (unnecessary)
7. Defects

*(0.4 mark per waste, rounding up to 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 regulations:** Answer **any one** of the following full 14-mark questions. Each contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**"Kaizen is the engine of Japanese industrial success."**
**(a)** Explain the **five core principles of Kaizen** as proposed by Masaaki Imai. (7 marks)
**(b)** Differentiate between **Kaizen and Innovation** with a suitable real-world example. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Five Core Principles of Kaizen (7 marks):**

| # | Principle | Explanation (1.4 marks each) |
|---|-----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Process-oriented thinking** | Bad results come from bad processes; fix the process to fix the result. Avoid blaming people. |
| 2 | **PDCA cycle as engine** | Every improvement must follow Plan → Do → Check → Act scientifically, using measurable data. |
| 3 | **Gemba — Go to the real place** | Real problems are visible only at the actual workplace; managers must leave offices and observe. |
| 4 | **Eliminate muda (waste)** | Relentlessly remove the 7 wastes: overproduction, waiting, transport, over-processing, inventory, motion, defects. |
| 5 | **Standardization** | Without a standard, there is no baseline; every successful Kaizen ends by raising the standard. |

*(Total: 5 × ~1.4 = 7 marks)*

**(b) Kaizen vs. Innovation (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Kaizen | Innovation |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| Meaning | Small, continuous improvements | Large, breakthrough changes |
| Cost | Low / no capital | High capital investment |
| Risk | Low | High |
| Time | Continuous | Periodic |
| Source | Bottom-up (workers) | Top-down (R&D) |
| Focus | Process | Product/Technology |

**Real-world example:** A hospital uses **Kaizen** to reduce patient waiting time by reorganizing the registration desk (small change, low cost). The same hospital uses **Innovation** by adopting a fully digital AI-based diagnostic system (large change, high cost). Both improvements are valuable but operate on different scales.

*(3 marks for table + 2 marks for explanation + 2 marks for example = 7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied to a Kaizen improvement. (7 marks)
**(b)** A manufacturing unit produces 8,000 units/month at ₹350/unit. After a Kaizen event, the cost dropped to ₹315/unit. Calculate the **(i)** per-unit savings, **(ii)** percentage improvement, and **(iii)** annual monetary savings. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PDCA Cycle Flowchart (7 marks):**

```
        ┌──────────────────┐
        ↓                  │
      PLAN                │
   Identify problem       │
   Analyze root cause     │
   Set targets            │
        ↓                │
       DO                │
   Pilot implementation   │
        ↓                │
      CHECK              │
   Compare results       │
   with planned target   │
        ↓                │
       ACT ──────────────┘
   Standardize if successful
   Else re-plan
```

**Explanations:**
- **Plan:** Define the problem (e.g., high defect rate), analyze root cause using tools like 5-Why, set measurable targets.
- **Do:** Implement the countermeasure on a small, controlled scale.
- **Check:** Compare actual results with the planned target using data.
- **Act:** If target met, update SOP and standardize. If not, return to Plan.

*(2 marks flowchart + 5 marks detailed explanation = 7 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_{\text{before}} = 350$ ₹/unit
- $C_{\text{after}} = 315$ ₹/unit
- $N = 8{,}000$ units/month

**(i) Per-unit savings:**
$$
\Delta C = 350 - 315 = 35 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$
*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Percentage improvement:**
$$
\eta = \left(\frac{35}{350}\right) \times 100 = 10 \%
$$
*(2 marks)*

**(iii) Annual monetary savings:**
$$
S_{\text{monthly}} = 35 \times 8{,}000 = 2{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹/month}
$$
$$
S_{\text{annual}} = 2{,}80{,}000 \times 12 = 33{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹/year} = 33.6 \text{ Lakhs/year}
$$
*(3 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Kaizen with Innovation** — Many students write them as opposites. They are **complementary** strategies.
> 2. **Skipping the numerical units** — Writing "35" without "₹/unit" loses 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting standardization** — In PDCA answers, students often omit the *Act → Standardize* loop. Always end the cycle with an updated SOP.
> 4. **Mis-spelling Gemba / Muda** — KTU examiners deduct marks for inconsistent transliteration. Use "Gemba" and "Muda" consistently.
> 5. **Neglecting the 5S** — When asked about Kaizen tools, students forget to mention 5S, Quality Circles, TPM, and VSM. Always mention at least **3–4 supporting tools**.
> 6. **Ignoring workers' role** — Kaizen is *bottom-up*. Answers stating only management-driven improvements lose marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Kaizen (改善)** = Continuous improvement through small, low-cost, incremental changes.
- **Originator:** Masaaki Imai (1986 book, Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success).
- **Five Principles:** Process-oriented, PDCA cycle, Gemba, eliminate *muda*, standardization.
- **Gemba (現場)** = The real place where work happens; managers must observe directly.
- **Muda (無駄)** = Waste; **7 types** + an 8th (*unused human talent*).
- **PDCA Cycle:** Plan → Do → Check → Act (recurring, scientific improvement loop).
- **5S:** Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke (foundation of Kaizen workplace).
- **Kaizen Event / Blitz:** 3–5 day intensive, cross-functional, focused improvement workshop.
- **Kaizen vs Innovation:** Kaizen = small, low-cost, bottom-up, continuous; Innovation = large, high-cost, top-down, sporadic. **Both are complementary.**
- **Cost Formulas:** $\Delta C = C_{\text{before}} - C_{\text{after}}$ and $\eta = (\Delta C / C_{\text{before}}) \times 100\%$.
- **Tools under Kaizen umbrella:** 5S, JIT, TPM, Jidoka, Quality Circles, VSM, Poka-Yoke.
- **Key Suggestion Metric:** $KPR = $ suggestions accepted per employee per month (target $\geq 1$).
- **Real-world impact example:** ₹86.4 Lakhs annual savings achievable with zero capital investment via disciplined Kaizen events.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Partnership - Partnering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Partnership - Partnering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Supplier Partnership** (also called **Partnering** or **Strategic Supplier Partnership**) is a long-term, cooperative, win-win contractual relationship between a buying organization (customer) and its key suppliers, built on mutual trust, openness, shared risk, and shared rewards, with the explicit objective of achieving superior quality, reduced total cost, continuous improvement, and enhanced competitive advantage for both parties.

In KTU 2024 Scheme TQM parlance, partnering transcends the traditional adversarial *purchase-order transaction* model and evolves into a **strategic alliance** where suppliers are treated as an extension of the buyer's own operating system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exam-Worthy):**
> "Supplier partnering is a structured, continuous improvement-based, long-term relationship between customer and supplier that emphasizes cooperative problem-solving, mutual goal-setting, and joint value creation, replacing the conventional short-term, price-driven, adversarial purchase relationship."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Marriage Analogy 💍:**
Think of the traditional buyer-supplier relationship like a **one-night date** — short, shallow, transactional, focused only on "how cheap can I get it tonight?" There is no emotional investment, no shared future, and the moment a cheaper option appears, both parties walk away.

Now, **Supplier Partnering is like a committed marriage** 💑:
- Both parties know each other deeply (shared information systems).
- They plan children together (joint product development).
- They share finances transparently (open-book costing).
- During tough times (recessions, defects), they stand by each other instead of running to a divorce lawyer.
- They celebrate each other's successes (shared rewards, long-term contracts).

**The Sports Team Analogy ⚽:**
A traditional supplier is like a *mercenary footballer* — paid per match, gives minimal effort, and defects to the highest bidder. A **partnered supplier** is like a *club captain* — invested in the team's long-term success, willing to sacrifice short-term gain for the trophy, and improves his own skills to help the team win.

## 1.3 Why Supplier Partnering is a KTU 2024 High-Priority Topic

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Significance:**
> - Module 2 of PECHT413 (TQM) covers *Supplier Management* and forms **20–25%** of the End Semester Examination (ESE) weightage.
> - Supplier Partnership is a **direct application** of *Juran's Trilogy* (Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement) at the supply chain level.
> - It is the foundational stepping stone to advanced topics like *Supplier Rating*, *SPC in supply chains*, and *ISO 9001:2015 Clause 8.4*.

## 1.4 Transition from Traditional Purchasing to Partnering — The Paradigm Shift

| Dimension | Traditional Purchasing | Supplier Partnering |
|---|---|---|
| **Duration** | Short-term contracts | Long-term (3–10+ years) |
| **Supplier base** | Many suppliers | Few, key suppliers |
| **Focus** | Price only | Total cost, quality, delivery, innovation |
| **Communication** | Adversarial, secretive | Open, transparent (Open-Book Costing) |
| **Risk** | Pushed to supplier | Shared jointly |
| **Rewards** | Supplier's gain only | Shared gains (gainsharing) |
| **Trust level** | Low — based on inspection | High — based on process capability |
| **Conflict resolution** | Legal contracts, lawsuits | Joint problem-solving teams |
| **Quality assurance** | Incoming inspection | Supplier's own quality systems (SPC) |
| **Information sharing** | Minimal, guarded | Extensive, real-time (EDI, ERP) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** *PS-LTO* — **P**rice → **S**trategy, **L**ow trust → **T**otal trust, **O**ne-time → **O**ngoing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Logic — Why Partner? (The "Why" Behind Partnering)

The fundamental reason for partnering is captured by what quality gurus call the **1-10-100 Rule** in supply chains:

$$\text{Cost of Prevention (at supplier)} \ll \text{Cost of Appraisal (incoming inspection)} \ll \text{Cost of Failure (in field)}$$

In simple terms: **Fixing a defect at the supplier costs 1 unit, catching it at incoming inspection costs 10 units, and failing in the customer's hands costs 100+ units.** Partnering shifts investment to the cheapest point — the supplier's process.

### 2.1.1 Pillars of Supplier Partnering (The Four Pillars Framework)

1. **Long-Term Commitment** 🤝
   - Multi-year contracts (typically 3–5 years, renewable).
   - Provides supplier with volume stability → enables investment in better tools, training, capacity.

2. **Mutual Trust & Openness** 🔓
   - Sharing of cost data, forecasts, capacity plans, and quality metrics.
   - Open-Book Costing: supplier reveals true cost structure; buyer understands pricing logic.

3. **Joint Quality Planning & Problem Solving** 🛠️
   - Cross-functional teams (QCCs, Kaizen) with members from both companies.
   - Concurrent Engineering during new product development.

4. **Shared Risk & Reward** ⚖️
   - **Gainsharing:** Cost savings are split (e.g., 50:50) between buyer and supplier.
   - Risk-sharing during demand downturns, raw material price spikes.

## 2.2 Characteristics of a True Partnership (KTU Board Checklist)

A genuine partnership exhibits these **seven diagnostic features**:

- **Fewer suppliers** — typically 1–3 per category (single sourcing or dual sourcing with strategic partners).
- **Supplier involvement from product design stage** — not bolted on at the end.
- **Mutual capability development** — buyer helps supplier upgrade via training, technology transfer.
- **Continuous improvement (Kaizen) targets** — annual cost/quality reduction goals (e.g., 5%/year).
- **Performance-based metrics** — instead of price-only, use TCO (Total Cost of Ownership).
- **Joint problem-solving teams** — cross-company SQDIC (Safety, Quality, Delivery, Inventory, Cost) reviews.
- **Top management involvement** — partnerships are CEO-to-CEO, not just purchasing-to-sales.

## 2.3 The Three Strategic Roles of Suppliers (Kraljic's Matrix Connection)

While not explicitly in KTU syllabus, examiners love connecting partnering to **Kraljic's Purchasing Portfolio Model** (1983). Understanding the *type* of purchased item dictates the *type* of relationship:

| Kraljic Category | Item Type | Recommended Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-critical / Leverage** | Standard, low-risk items | **Arm's-length** (traditional) — multiple suppliers |
| **Bottleneck** | Scarce, single-source items | **Partnership** (deep, survival-based) |
| **Strategic** | High-value, high-risk items | **Strategic Partnership** (closest, most integrated) |
| **Critical** | High-value + high-risk | **Partnership with system lock-in** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question asks "Which items warrant partnering?" — the answer is *Bottleneck* and *Strategic* items. *Non-critical* items should remain on traditional purchasing to maintain competitive tension.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Supplier Partnering is a *qualitative management* topic, the "formulas" are the **decision matrices and rating models** used in supplier evaluation and partnership governance.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula or Definition | Unit / Range | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = P + C_t + C_q + C_d + C_s$ | Currency/unit | True cost comparison of suppliers |
| 2 | **Supplier Rating Index (SRI)** | $SRI = \dfrac{\sum (w_i \times s_i)}{100}$ | 0 to 1 (or %) | Rank suppliers; weight = $w_i$, score = $s_i$ |
| 3 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) at Supplier** | $COPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,fail} + C_{external\,fail}$ | Currency/period | Justifies investment in partnership |
| 4 | **1-10-100 Rule** | $C_{prevention} : C_{appraisal} : C_{failure} = 1 : 10 : 100$ | Ratio | Argument for early supplier involvement |
| 5 | **Number of Suppliers (Ideal)** | $N_{suppliers} = 1 \text{ to } 3$ per category | Integer | Partnering reduces supplier base |
| 6 | **Gainshare Ratio** | $G_{supplier} = \alpha \times \Delta C_{total}$ | Currency | $\alpha$ = 0.3 to 0.5 typically |
| 7 | **Supplier On-Time Delivery (OTD)** | $OTD = \dfrac{N_{on\,time}}{N_{total\,orders}} \times 100$ | % | KPI in partnership SLA |
| 8 | **Defect Rate (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times O}$ | Defects/Million | Quality KPI for partner |
| 9 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good\,units}}{N_{total\,units}} \times 100$ | % | Process capability indicator |
| 10 | **Partnership Maturity Index (PMI)** | $PMI = \dfrac{T+M+O+R+J}{5}$ (1–5 scale each) | 0 to 5 | Self-assessment of partnership depth |

**Where:**
- $P$ = Purchase price
- $C_t$ = Transportation cost
- $C_q$ = Quality cost (inspection, rework, warranty)
- $C_d$ = Downstream disruption cost
- $C_s$ = Social/environmental compliance cost
- $w_i$ = Weight of criterion $i$ (e.g., Quality 40%, Delivery 25%, Cost 20%, Service 15%)
- $s_i$ = Score of supplier on criterion $i$ (0–100)
- $T$ = Trust level, $M$ = Mutual investment, $O$ = Openness, $R$ = Risk-sharing, $J$ = Joint problem-solving

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often quote only the **purchase price** as the cost. KTU examiners deduct marks for ignoring *hidden costs* like inspection, warranty claims, downtime, and rework. **Always use TCO.**

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Management

Supplier partnering is the **backbone of modern manufacturing excellence** programs:
- **Toyota Production System (TPS):** Kyohokai (Association) — tier-1 suppliers as extended family.
- **Boeing & Airbus:** Tier-1 suppliers (Spirit AeroSystems, Safran) co-design wings and engines.
- **Apple & Foxconn:** Strategic partnership for iPhone assembly — Foxconn is a co-investor in capacity.
- **Automotive (Maruti, Tata Motors):** Vendor parks adjacent to assembly plant — joint just-in-time operations.

Without partnering, **Industry 4.0, Lean, and Agile manufacturing** are practically impossible because they require deep, real-time collaboration with suppliers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Models

> [!NOTE]
> Since Supplier Partnering is fundamentally a *qualitative management concept*, the "derivations" here are the **logical decision frameworks, evaluation matrices, and partnership development lifecycles** that KTU examiners test. Each step is laid out exhaustively for full marks.

## 3.1 The Seven-Step Partnering Implementation Roadmap (Model Question Favorite)

This is the **canonical answer structure** for any 14-mark question on "Explain the process of supplier partnering."

### Step 1 — Strategic Sourcing & Segmentation
- Classify all purchased items using **Kraljic's Matrix** (or Pareto ABC analysis).
- Identify items as *Strategic*, *Leverage*, *Bottleneck*, or *Non-critical*.
- **Decision rule:** Only *Strategic* and *Bottleneck* items proceed to full partnership evaluation.

### Step 2 — Supplier Identification & Pre-Qualification
- Build a long-list from market intelligence, trade shows, references.
- Apply **pre-qualification filters**: financial stability, ISO 9001/QMS certification, capacity, geographic risk.
- Short-list to 3–5 potential partners.

### Step 3 — Supplier Evaluation & Selection
- Apply weighted scoring model (see Section 3.2 below).
- Conduct on-site audits of supplier's QMS, process capability (Cpk studies), and financial health.
- **Final selection** — usually 1 (single-sourcing) or 2 (dual-sourcing) partners.

### Step 4 — Partnership Agreement & Contract Structuring
- Move from PO (Purchase Order) to **Master Service Agreement (MSA)**.
- Include clauses for:
  - Quality levels (PPM targets, FPY)
  - Cost reduction (annual 3–5% target)
  - Confidentiality (NDA)
  - Intellectual property (jointly developed designs)
  - Exit clause (with mutual notice period)

### Step 5 — Joint Development & Capability Building
- **Early Supplier Involvement (ESI):** Supplier engineers embedded in buyer's NPD team.
- **Supplier development programs:** Buyer funds training, tooling, software licenses.
- Joint use of **APQP** (Advanced Product Quality Planning) and **FMEA**.

### Step 6 — Performance Monitoring & Continuous Improvement
- Monthly/quarterly **SQDIC reviews** (Safety, Quality, Delivery, Inventory, Cost).
- Annual Kaizen workshops — joint problem-solving.
- Use **Scorecards** with red/amber/green (RAG) status.

### Step 7 — Recognition & Continuous Renewal
- **Supplier recognition awards** (Annual Vendor of the Year).
- **Gainsharing payouts** when targets are exceeded.
- Periodic **partnership health check** (PMI survey — see Section 2.4).
- Renewal or restructuring of contract every 3–5 years.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Weighted Supplier Rating Calculation (7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A manufacturer wants to select a strategic partner for supplying precision-machined gear housings. Three suppliers (A, B, C) have been short-listed. The criteria, weights, and scores (out of 100) are given below. Compute the **Supplier Rating Index (SRI)** for each and recommend the partner.

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Supplier A ($s_i$) | Supplier B ($s_i$) | Supplier C ($s_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (PPM, FPY) | 40% | 85 | 90 | 78 |
| On-Time Delivery | 25% | 80 | 82 | 92 |
| Cost (TCO) | 20% | 75 | 88 | 80 |
| Technical Capability | 15% | 90 | 80 | 85 |
| **Total Weight** | **100%** | — | — | — |

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Formula Recall:**

$$
SRI = \frac{\sum (w_i \times s_i)}{100}
$$

**Step 1 — Calculate weighted scores for Supplier A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRI_A &= \frac{(40 \times 85) + (25 \times 80) + (20 \times 75) + (15 \times 90)}{100} \\
     &= \frac{3400 + 2000 + 1500 + 1350}{100} \\
     &= \frac{8250}{100} \\
     &= 82.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate weighted scores for Supplier B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRI_B &= \frac{(40 \times 90) + (25 \times 82) + (20 \times 88) + (15 \times 80)}{100} \\
     &= \frac{3600 + 2050 + 1760 + 1200}{100} \\
     &= \frac{8610}{100} \\
     &= 86.10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate weighted scores for Supplier C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRI_C &= \frac{(40 \times 78) + (25 \times 92) + (20 \times 80) + (15 \times 85)}{100} \\
     &= \frac{3120 + 2300 + 1600 + 1275}{100} \\
     &= \frac{8295}{100} \\
     &= 82.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Ranking and Recommendation:**

| Rank | Supplier | SRI Score | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1st | **Supplier B** | 86.10% | ✅ **Recommended as Strategic Partner** |
| 🥈 2nd | Supplier C | 82.95% | Reserve / Dual-source backup |
| 🥉 3rd | Supplier A | 82.50% | Improvement needed; not selected |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Always justify your recommendation by linking the *highest-weighted criterion* to the winner's strength. Here, *Quality* (40% weight) is dominant, and Supplier B scored 90 vs. C's 78 — this seals the decision despite C's better delivery score.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Gainsharing Calculation (14-Mark Application)

> **Problem:** A buyer-supplier partnership has identified a joint cost-reduction opportunity. The current annual spend with the supplier is ₹5 Crore. Through joint Kaizen, the supplier proposes to reduce cost by 8% over the next year. The **gainsharing ratio** is 60:40 in favor of the buyer. Compute (a) the buyer's annual saving, (b) the supplier's annual benefit, and (c) the per-unit saving if 50,000 units are purchased annually.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Given Data:**
- Current annual spend: $C_0$ = ₹5,00,00,000
- Cost reduction achieved: $r$ = 8% = 0.08
- Gainshare split: Buyer = 60%, Supplier = 40%
- Annual volume: $Q$ = 50,000 units

**Step (a) — Total Cost Savings (Joint):**

$$
\Delta C_{total} = C_0 \times r = 5{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.08 = 40{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹40 Lakh)}
$$

**Step (b) — Buyer's Net Saving (60% share):**

$$
S_{buyer} = 0.60 \times 40{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹24 Lakh)}
$$

**Step (c) — Supplier's Net Benefit (40% share, post-cost-of-improvement):**

$$
S_{supplier} = 0.40 \times 40{,}00{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹16 Lakh)}
$$

**Step (d) — Per-Unit Saving:**

$$
S_{unit} = \frac{\Delta C_{total}}{Q} = \frac{40{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = 800 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The buyer saves ₹24 Lakh/year, the supplier retains ₹16 Lakh/year, and the *unit price drops by ₹800*. This is the **double dividend** of partnering — both parties are better off than before, validating the *win-win* principle.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]*
**Define Supplier Partnership. List any four characteristics of a true supplier partnership.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Supplier partnership is a long-term, cooperative, and strategic relationship between a buying firm and its key suppliers, based on mutual trust, shared information, joint problem-solving, and shared risks/rewards, aimed at achieving superior quality, lower total cost, and continuous improvement for both parties.

**Four Characteristics (any four):**
1. **Long-term commitment** — multi-year contracts providing stability.
2. **Open communication** — sharing of cost data, forecasts, and capacity plans.
3. **Mutual trust** — replacing incoming inspection with supplier's self-certification.
4. **Joint quality planning** — early supplier involvement (ESI) in new product development.
5. **Shared risk and reward** — gainsharing arrangements and joint cost-reduction programs.
6. **Top management involvement** — CEO-to-CEO relationship anchoring the partnership.

> [!TIP]
> *Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark each for the four characteristics (2 marks).*

---

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]*
**Differentiate between Traditional Purchasing and Supplier Partnership in any six aspects.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Aspect | Traditional Purchasing | Supplier Partnership |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Duration** | Short-term, transactional | Long-term (3–10 years) |
| 2 | **Supplier Base** | Many suppliers (price competition) | Few, key suppliers (1–3) |
| 3 | **Decision Criterion** | Lowest purchase price | Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) |
| 4 | **Trust Level** | Low — verified by inspection | High — verified by process capability |
| 5 | **Information Sharing** | Minimal, secretive | Extensive, open-book costing |
| 6 | **Risk & Reward** | Pushed to supplier | Shared jointly (gainsharing) |
| 7 | **Conflict Resolution** | Contracts, legal action | Joint teams, mutual problem-solving |

> [!TIP]
> *Award 0.5 mark for each correct contrasting point. Six points = 3 marks.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> *Note: KTU 2024 Scheme mandates Module-level internal choice. The two questions below are independent alternatives — answer EITHER OR.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **Seven-Step Process for Developing Supplier Partnership**. Highlight the role of Early Supplier Involvement (ESI).* — **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A manufacturing company sources electronic control units (ECUs) from three vendors. The weighted scoring model uses Quality (40%), Delivery (25%), Cost (20%), and Service (15%). Scores (out of 100) are:*

| Criterion | Vendor X | Vendor Y | Vendor Z |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 88 | 82 | 90 |
| Delivery | 90 | 85 | 80 |
| Cost | 78 | 92 | 85 |
| Service | 80 | 78 | 88 |

*Compute the Supplier Rating Index (SRI) for each vendor and recommend which should be selected as the strategic partner.* — **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Seven-Step Process & Role of ESI (7 marks)**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Supplier partnership is not a single act of signing a contract — it is a structured, multi-stage transformation process. A widely accepted framework consists of **seven sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Strategic Sourcing & Segmentation (1 mark):**
The buyer classifies all purchased items using the **Kraljic Matrix** or **Pareto ABC analysis**. Items are categorized as Strategic, Leverage, Bottleneck, or Non-critical. Only **Strategic** and **Bottleneck** items are candidates for partnership due to their high impact on quality, cost, and risk.

**Step 2 — Supplier Identification & Pre-Qualification (1 mark):**
The buyer identifies potential suppliers through market intelligence, industry referrals, and trade exhibitions. A **pre-qualification questionnaire** filters suppliers on financial health, ISO/QMS certification, capacity, geographical risk, and past performance. The long list is reduced to 3–5 candidates.

**Step 3 — Supplier Evaluation & Selection (1 mark):**
Each shortlisted supplier is evaluated using a **weighted scoring model** (SRI = Σ $w_i s_i$) across quality, delivery, cost, technology, and service. On-site QMS audits and **Cpk process capability** studies are conducted. Top 1–2 suppliers are selected.

**Step 4 — Partnership Agreement (1 mark):**
A **Master Service Agreement (MSA)** is signed, replacing individual POs. Key clauses: quality targets (PPM, FPY), annual cost reduction (3–5%), confidentiality (NDA), IP ownership, dispute resolution, and exit terms. Contract duration is typically **3–5 years**.

**Step 5 — Joint Development & Capability Building (1 mark):**
**Early Supplier Involvement (ESI)** — the cornerstone of partnership — embeds supplier engineers in the buyer's New Product Development (NPD) team from concept stage. Joint use of **APQP, FMEA, DFM, DFA** ensures manufacturability and quality are designed-in, not inspected-in.

**Step 6 — Performance Monitoring (1 mark):**
Monthly/quarterly **SQDIC reviews** (Safety, Quality, Delivery, Inventory, Cost) using RAG scorecards. Annual joint Kaizen workshops drive continuous improvement (Jidoka, Poka-Yoke, 5S).

**Step 7 — Recognition, Gainsharing & Renewal (1 mark):**
Outstanding partners receive **Vendor-of-the-Year** awards. Cost savings are split via **gainsharing** (typically 50:50 to 60:40 in favor of the buyer). The partnership is reviewed every 3–5 years for renewal or restructuring.

**Role of ESI (highlighted in Step 5):**
- **Cuts NPD cycle time** by 30–50%.
- **Reduces engineering change orders (ECOs)** by detecting issues early.
- **Lowers warranty cost** as manufacturability is designed-in.
- **Builds supplier ownership** of the buyer's product success.
- **Enables concurrent engineering** — design, manufacturing, and quality teams work in parallel.

---

#### **Part (b) — SRI Calculation & Recommendation (7 marks)**

**Formula:**

$$
SRI = \frac{\sum (w_i \times s_i)}{100}
$$

**Vendor X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRI_X &= \frac{(40 \times 88) + (25 \times 90) + (20 \times 78) + (15 \times 80)}{100} \\
     &= \frac{3520 + 2250 + 1560 + 1200}{100} \\
     &= \frac{8530}{100} \\
     &= 85.30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Vendor Y:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRI_Y &= \frac{(40 \times 82) + (25 \times 85) + (20 \times 92) + (15 \times 78)}{100} \\
     &= \frac{3280 + 2125 + 1840 + 1170}{100} \\
     &= \frac{8415}{100} \\
     &= 84.15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Vendor Z:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SRI_Z &= \frac{(40 \times 90) + (25 \times 80) + (20 \times 85) + (15 \times 88)}{100} \\
     &= \frac{3600 + 2000 + 1700 + 1320}{100} \\
     &= \frac{8620}{100} \\
     &= 86.20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking:**

| Rank | Vendor | SRI Score |
|---|---|---|
| 🥇 1st | **Vendor Z** | **86.20%** ✅ |
| 🥈 2nd | Vendor X | 85.30% |
| 🥉 3rd | Vendor Y | 84.15% |

**Recommendation (1 mark):**
**Vendor Z should be selected as the strategic partner** for ECU supply. Justification: Vendor Z leads in the highest-weighted criterion (Quality at 40%, scored 90) and has the best overall balanced score. Vendor X is recommended as the **dual-source backup** for supply continuity, and Vendor Y requires development in quality and service before being considered.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]*

**(a)** *Discuss the **benefits and barriers** of supplier partnering. How can these barriers be overcome?* — **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A partnership has identified a cost reduction of 12% on an annual spend of ₹8 Crore. The gainsharing ratio is 55:45 (Buyer : Supplier). If the annual volume is 80,000 units, calculate the buyer's saving, supplier's benefit, and per-unit reduction in cost.* — **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Benefits, Barriers & Overcoming Them (7 marks)**

**Benefits of Supplier Partnership (4 marks, any 4 well-explained):**

1. **Reduced Total Cost of Ownership (TCO):** Joint Kaizen, value engineering, and elimination of inspection lower costs by 10–30% over the partnership life.
2. **Improved Quality:** Supplier process capability (Cpk ≥ 1.33) replaces incoming inspection; PPM defects drop by 50–90%.
3. **Faster Innovation:** Early Supplier Involvement (ESI) accelerates NPD by 30–50% through concurrent engineering.
4. **Supply Chain Resilience:** Long-term contracts and trust ensure supply continuity during disruptions (e.g., COVID-19, semiconductor shortages).
5. **Reduced Administrative Cost:** Fewer POs, fewer suppliers → procurement overhead drops significantly.
6. **Shared Risk & Reward:** Gainsharing aligns both parties' interests, creating a "we're-in-the-same-boat" mindset.
7. **Capacity Assurance:** Buyer gains priority access to supplier capacity during demand peaks.
8. **Joint Learning:** Knowledge transfer (TQM tools, Six Sigma, Lean) uplifts both organizations.

**Barriers of Supplier Partnership (2 marks, any 4):**

1. **Cultural differences** — buyer's and supplier's work cultures may clash (e.g., Japanese Keiretsu style vs. Western adversarial style).
2. **Fear of dependency / loss of control** — buyer fears single-source vulnerability; supplier fears being squeezed on price.
3. **Trust deficit** — historical adversarial relationships make openness difficult.
4. **Information asymmetry concerns** — supplier fears buyer exploiting cost data to squeeze prices (especially with open-book costing).
5. **Resistance to change** — internal procurement teams may resist losing multiple suppliers' competitive leverage.
6. **Geographical & communication barriers** — especially with offshore suppliers.
7. **Lack of top management commitment** — partnerships need C-suite sponsorship, not just purchasing department.

**Overcoming the Barriers (1 mark, brief):**
- **Pilot partnerships** on non-critical items first to build confidence.
- **Formal governance structure** — joint steering committee, regular QBRs (Quarterly Business Reviews).
- **Legal safeguards** — NDAs, IP clauses, mutual gainsharing contracts.
- **Cultural integration programs** — joint training, exchange programs, shared vision workshops.
- **Phased disclosure** of cost data — start with high-level cost buckets, deepen gradually.
- **Top-down leadership** — CEO-level sponsorship signals seriousness to both organizations.

---

#### **Part (b) — Gainsharing Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Annual spend: $C_0$ = ₹8,00,00,000
- Cost reduction: $r$ = 12% = 0.12
- Buyer's gainshare: $\alpha$ = 55% = 0.55
- Supplier's gainshare: $1 - \alpha$ = 45% = 0.45
- Annual volume: $Q$ = 80,000 units

**Step 1 — Total Joint Savings:**

$$
\Delta C_{total} = C_0 \times r = 8{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.12 = 96{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹96 Lakh)}
$$

**Step 2 — Buyer's Net Saving (55% share):**

$$
S_{buyer} = 0.55 \times 96{,}00{,}000 = 52{,}80{,}000 \text{ (₹52.8 Lakh)}
$$

**Step 3 — Supplier's Net Benefit (45% share):**

$$
S_{supplier} = 0.45 \times 96{,}00{,}000 = 43{,}20{,}000 \text{ (₹43.2 Lakh)}
$$

**Step 4 — Per-Unit Cost Reduction:**

$$
\Delta C_{unit} = \frac{\Delta C_{total}}{Q} = \frac{96{,}00{,}000}{80{,}000} = 1200 \text{ ₹/unit}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The partnership generates **₹96 Lakh in total joint savings**, with the buyer pocketing ₹52.8 Lakh and the supplier retaining ₹43.2 Lakh — both parties are financially better off. The per-unit cost drops by **₹1,200**, which can be passed to end customers as a price advantage, creating a **triple-win**: buyer profits, supplier profits, customer saves.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show units** (₹ Lakh / Crore) — examiners deduct 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Reversing gainshare ratio** — read carefully: "55:45 (Buyer:Supplier)" means Buyer = 55%. Many students assign 55% to supplier.
> 3. **Computing only one party's share** — always compute BOTH shares for full marks.
> 4. **Not linking the answer back to the "win-win" principle** — always end with a concluding statement that reinforces partnering benefits.
> 5. **In SRI questions:** Not showing the *final rank table* or *justification* — students often compute scores but skip the recommendation step, losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Supplier Partnership**

### 🔑 **Core Concepts**
- ☐ Supplier partnership = long-term, trust-based, win-win alliance beyond the PO transaction.
- ☐ It is a **paradigm shift** from *adversarial arm's-length* to *cooperative strategic* relationship.
- ☐ It is **NOT** applicable to all items — only **Strategic** and **Bottleneck** items (per Kraljic Matrix).
- ☐ The **4 Pillars** are: Long-term commitment, Mutual trust, Joint quality planning, Shared risk/reward.

### 📊 **Key Numbers & Formulas to Memorize**
- ☐ TCO = Price + Transport + Quality cost + Downtime + Compliance cost.
- ☐ SRI = Σ (weight × score) / 100.
- ☐ **1-10-100 Rule:** Prevention : Appraisal : Failure = 1 : 10 : 100.
- ☐ Gainsharing: Buyer's share = α × ΔC; Supplier's share = (1−α) × ΔC.
- ☐ Ideal supplier count post-partnering: **1 to 3** per category (down from 5–10+).
- ☐ Annual cost reduction target in partnership: **3 to 5% per year**.

### 🏛️ **KTU High-Yield Frameworks**
- ☐ **Seven-Step Process:** Segmentation → Identification → Evaluation → Agreement → Joint Development → Monitoring → Recognition & Renewal.
- ☐ **Kraljic Matrix:** Map items on *Supply Risk × Profit Impact* to choose relationship type.
- ☐ **SQDIC Scorecard:** Safety, Quality, Delivery, Inventory, Cost — the standard review template.
- ☐ **ESI (Early Supplier Involvement):** Embed supplier in NPD from concept stage.
- ☐ **Open-Book Costing:** Supplier shares true cost data; buyer gains transparency.

### ⚠️ **Common Exam Traps**
- ☐ Do **NOT** confuse *Partnering* with *Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)* — VMI is a *subset* of partnering.
- ☐ Do **NOT** say "partnership means lowest price wins" — partnering uses **TCO**, not PO price.
- ☐ Do **NOT** claim partnering eliminates all suppliers — strategic items get partners, non-critical items retain competition.
- ☐ Do **NOT** forget the **win-win conclusion** — partnering must explicitly benefit both parties.

### 🗣️ **Buzzwords Examiners Love**
- ☐ *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)*
- ☐ *Mutual trust and openness*
- ☐ *Win-win relationship*
- ☐ *Cooperative problem-solving*
- ☐ *Continuous improvement (Kaizen)*
- ☐ *Gainsharing arrangement*
- ☐ *Open-book costing*
- ☐ *Long-term strategic alliance*
- ☐ *Concurrent engineering*
- ☐ *Supplier development program*

> 🏁 **End of Module 2 Notes — Supplier Partnership: Partnering** 🏁
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sourcing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sourcing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sourcing** is the strategic business process of identifying, evaluating, selecting, and managing suppliers who can deliver the goods, materials, or services required by an organization to meet its operational and quality objectives at the most optimal total cost of ownership.

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, sourcing extends far beyond a simple purchase transaction. It encompasses the entire upstream quality chain, recognizing the principle of **"Quality is built into the product at the supplier's end."** Under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, sourcing is treated as a **strategic enabler** of quality, cost, delivery, and innovation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECHT413 – Module 2):** The treatment of *Sourcing* under TQM includes its definition, types, the strategic sourcing process, **Make-or-Buy analysis**, supplier selection criteria, supplier rating models, and supplier development partnerships.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Standardized to KTU Lexicon

| Term | Formal KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Sourcing** | The end-to-end process of finding, evaluating, and onboarding suppliers. |
| **Supplier** | An external entity providing inputs (materials, services, information) to the value chain. |
| **Vendor Rating** | A quantitative evaluation of a supplier's performance on Quality, Cost, Delivery, and Service parameters. |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | A data-driven, long-term, collaborative approach to optimize the supplier base. |
| **Single Sourcing** | Procuring a particular item from only one approved supplier. |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Procuring the same item from two or more competing suppliers. |
| **Make-or-Buy Decision** | The strategic choice between in-house manufacture and external procurement. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Cooking a Grand Feast"** 🍽️
> Imagine you are hosting a grand dinner for 50 guests. You can either grow the vegetables in your backyard (**Make**) or visit the market to buy the freshest ingredients (**Buy**). If you choose to buy, will you depend on one trusted vendor who always gives the best tomatoes, or will you split the order across three shops to ensure no single point of failure? How will you rate the vendors — by taste, punctuality, hygiene, or price? **Sourcing in TQM is exactly this decision-making process — only applied to industrial materials, components, and services, with the additional dimensions of quality certification, statistical process control, and long-term partnership.**

**Why does Sourcing matter in TQM?**
- The cost of purchased materials typically constitutes **50%–70%** of a manufactured product's total cost.
- Defects from poor supplier quality propagate downstream and are **7 to 10 times costlier to fix** after assembly.
- Dr. W. Edwards Deming's 14 Points explicitly identify the need to **"end the practice of awarding business on price tag alone"** — a direct call for intelligent sourcing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Strategic Sourcing Process (7-Step KTU Standard Model)

The strategic sourcing process is a structured, repeatable methodology that transforms purchasing from a clerical function into a strategic capability.

**Step 1 — Profile the Category & Spend Analysis**
- Classify all purchased items (Direct vs. Indirect, Critical vs. Non-critical).
- Apply the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** to identify the vital few categories that consume 80% of the budget.

**Step 2 — Develop the Sourcing Strategy**
- Decide between Make-or-Buy, single vs. multiple sourcing, and global vs. local.
- Set objectives for **Quality (Q), Cost (C), Delivery (D), and Service (S)**.

**Step 3 — Identify Potential Suppliers**
- Search via industry directories, trade shows, referrals, and reverse auctions.

**Step 4 — Supplier Evaluation & Selection**
- Apply weighted scoring models, capability audits, and on-site assessments.

**Step 5 — Negotiation & Contracting**
- Finalize **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** including price, freight, inspection, warranty, and risk.

**Step 6 — Implementation & Integration**
- Onboard suppliers, align quality systems (e.g., **ISO 9001**, **PPAP**), and integrate via **EDI / ERP**.

**Step 7 — Performance Monitoring & Continuous Improvement**
- Track KPIs, conduct supplier scorecard reviews, and drive Kaizen with the supplier base.

### 2.2 Types of Sourcing — A Comparative Matrix

| Type of Sourcing | Definition | Advantages | Disadvantages | TQM Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Sourcing** | One supplier for a specific item | Strong partnership, volume discounts, deeper technical collaboration | High dependency risk, supply disruption vulnerability | Demands long-term trust and supplier capability development |
| **Multiple Sourcing** | Several suppliers for the same item | Competition drives lower prices, supply security | Lack of loyalty, split volumes, inconsistent quality | Requires robust vendor rating systems |
| **Dual Sourcing** | Two qualified suppliers | Balanced risk and partnership | Coordination complexity | Ideal compromise for critical items |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | Data-driven, long-term collaborative procurement | Optimized TCO, innovation pipeline, ESG alignment | Time-intensive to set up | **The KTU-recommended TQM approach** |
| **Global Sourcing** | Procuring from international suppliers | Cost arbitrage, access to specialized technology | Currency, logistics, geopolitical risks | Requires rigorous quality audits |
| **Local Sourcing** | Procuring from domestic suppliers | Shorter lead times, easier communication | Higher unit cost, limited technology | Useful for JIT and agile supply chains |

### 2.3 Make-or-Buy Decision — The Core TQM Trade-off

The Make-or-Buy decision is the cornerstone strategic question in sourcing. It is a cost-volume analysis that determines at what production volume it becomes economically advantageous to manufacture in-house.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** The unit selling/transfer price is constant, fixed manufacturing overhead is independent of the buy decision, and only **avoidable (incremental) costs** are considered.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Sourcing — Memorize for ESE & Module Tests**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Make-or-Buy Break-Even Volume** | $V^* = \dfrac{F_{make} - F_{buy}}{V_{buy} - V_{make}}$ | Units/year; if demand > V*, prefer Make |
| 2 | **Total Cost of Make (TCₘ)** | $TC_m = F_{make} + (V_{make} \times Q)$ | Currency (₹ / $) |
| 3 | **Total Cost of Buy (TCᵦ)** | $TC_b = F_{buy} + (V_{buy} \times Q)$ | Currency (₹ / $) |
| 4 | **Differential Cost Savings** | $\Delta TC = TC_b - TC_m$ | Positive ⇒ Make is cheaper |
| 5 | **Vendor Rating — Weighted Score** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | Dimensionless; 0–10 scale |
| 6 | **Normalized Vendor Weight** | $w_j = \dfrac{\text{Criterion importance score}}{\sum \text{All importance scores}}$ | Sum of $w_j$ = 1 |
| 7 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = P + T + I + W + D + S$ | P=Price, T=Transport, I=Inspection, W=Warranty, D=Downtime, S=Stockout |
| 8 | **Pareto Spend Concentration** | $\text{Top X\% items} \approx 80\% \text{ spend}$ | Categorization principle |
| 9 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) at Supplier** | $COPQ = F_{prev} + F_{app} + F_{int} + F_{ext}$ | Failure + Appraisal costs |
| 10 | **Supplier Defect Rate (PPM)** | $PPM = \dfrac{\text{Defective units}}{\text{Total units supplied}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million |

**Where:**
- $F_{make}$, $F_{buy}$ = Fixed (avoidable) costs of making vs. buying
- $V_{make}$, $V_{buy}$ = Variable cost per unit of making vs. buying
- $Q$ = Annual demand quantity (units)
- $S_i$ = Weighted score of supplier *i*
- $w_j$ = Weight assigned to criterion *j*
- $r_{ij}$ = Rating given to supplier *i* on criterion *j*

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Industry

- **Automotive (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors):** Multi-tier supplier sourcing integrated with **Jidoka** and **Just-in-Time** delivery.
- **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** Strategic single/dual sourcing with AS9100-certified suppliers for safety-critical parts.
- **Electronics (Apple, Samsung):** Global sourcing from Foxconn, Luxshare with strict **Incoming Quality Control (IQC)** protocols.
- **Construction:** Tendering, BOQ-based sourcing, and vendor pre-qualification per **IS 9000/ISO 9001**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Make-or-Buy Break-Even Volume

**The Problem:**
An organization must decide whether to manufacture a component in-house ("Make") or purchase it from an external supplier ("Buy"). The fixed costs and variable costs for both options are known, along with the annual demand.

**Let us define the parameters:**

- $F_m$ = Annual avoidable fixed cost of in-house manufacture (₹)
- $V_m$ = Variable cost per unit of in-house manufacture (₹/unit)
- $F_b$ = Annual avoidable fixed cost of buying (₹) — typically includes supplier qualification, incoming inspection setup
- $V_b$ = Variable cost per unit of buying (₹/unit) — includes purchase price, freight, inspection
- $Q$ = Annual demand in units

**Derivation Logic — Each Step Expanded:**

**Step 1:** Write the total cost function for the Make option.
The Make option incurs fixed setup cost $F_m$ plus a variable cost that scales with quantity produced.

$$TC_m(Q) = F_m + V_m \cdot Q$$

**Step 2:** Write the total cost function for the Buy option.
The Buy option incurs a (usually smaller) fixed cost for vendor management plus a variable purchase cost per unit.

$$TC_b(Q) = F_b + V_b \cdot Q$$

**Step 3:** At the break-even point, the two total costs are exactly equal. This is the foundational definition of the indifference point.

$$TC_m(Q^*) = TC_b(Q^*)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2.

$$F_m + V_m \cdot Q^* = F_b + V_b \cdot Q^*$$

**Step 5:** Group all fixed-cost terms on one side and all variable-cost terms on the other.

$$F_m - F_b = V_b \cdot Q^* - V_m \cdot Q^*$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $Q^*$ on the right-hand side.

$$F_m - F_b = (V_b - V_m) \cdot Q^*$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the break-even quantity $Q^*$ by dividing both sides.

$$Q^* = \frac{F_m - F_b}{V_b - V_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule:**
> - If **predicted demand $Q > Q^*$** → Manufacture in-house (**Make** is cheaper).
> - If **predicted demand $Q < Q^*$** → Outsource to supplier (**Buy** is cheaper).
> - If $V_b \le V_m$ → Always Make (Buy is never competitive on variable cost).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Make-or-Buy Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **A KTU-style 14-mark Part B sub-problem. Master the full methodology.**

**Problem Statement:**
*Zenith Engineering Pvt. Ltd. requires **20,000 units/year** of a precision-machined bracket. The company has two options:*

| Parameter | Make In-House | Buy from Vendor |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Fixed Cost (₹) | **6,00,000** | **1,00,000** |
| Variable Cost per Unit (₹) | **180** | **260** |

*Determine whether the company should Make or Buy. Also compute the break-even volume and the cost savings at the actual demand level.*

**Full Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters from the table.

$$F_m = 6{,}00{,}000 \quad ; \quad F_b = 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$V_m = 180 \quad ; \quad V_b = 260 \quad ; \quad Q = 20{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the break-even volume $Q^*$ using the derived formula.

$$Q^* = \frac{F_m - F_b}{V_b - V_m}$$

$$Q^* = \frac{6{,}00{,}000 - 1{,}00{,}000}{260 - 180}$$

$$Q^* = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{80}$$

$$Q^* = 6{,}250 \text{ units/year}$$

**Interpretation:** Below 6,250 units, buying is cheaper; above 6,250 units, making is cheaper.

**Step 3:** Since the actual demand $Q = 20{,}000 > Q^* = 6{,}250$, the company should **Make in-house**.

**Step 4:** Compute the Total Cost under the Make option.

$$TC_m = F_m + V_m \cdot Q$$

$$TC_m = 6{,}00{,}000 + 180 \times 20{,}000$$

$$TC_m = 6{,}00{,}000 + 36{,}00{,}000 = 42{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Compute the Total Cost under the Buy option (for comparison).

$$TC_b = F_b + V_b \cdot Q$$

$$TC_b = 1{,}00{,}000 + 260 \times 20{,}000$$

$$TC_b = 1{,}00{,}000 + 52{,}00{,}000 = 53{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6:** Compute the cost savings (differential).

$$\Delta TC = TC_b - TC_m = 53{,}00{,}000 - 42{,}00{,}000 = 11{,}00{,}000$$

**Conclusion (final line of answer):**
*Since 20,000 > 6,250, Zenith Engineering should **MAKE** the bracket in-house, saving ₹11,00,000 per year compared to buying.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Vendor Rating Worked Example

**Problem:**
Three suppliers (A, B, C) are rated on four criteria with weights as shown. Compute the weighted total score and rank them.

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Supplier A | Supplier B | Supplier C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality (PPM inverse) | 0.40 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| On-Time Delivery | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
| Price Competitiveness | 0.20 | 9 | 7 | 8 |
| Technical Support | 0.15 | 8 | 9 | 7 |

**Verify the weights sum to 1:** $0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✔

**Step 1:** Compute Supplier A's weighted score.

$$S_A = (0.40 \times 8) + (0.25 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 8)$$

$$S_A = 3.20 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 1.20 = 7.95$$

**Step 2:** Compute Supplier B's weighted score.

$$S_B = (0.40 \times 9) + (0.25 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.15 \times 9)$$

$$S_B = 3.60 + 2.00 + 1.40 + 1.35 = 8.35$$

**Step 3:** Compute Supplier C's weighted score.

$$S_C = (0.40 \times 7) + (0.25 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.15 \times 7)$$

$$S_C = 2.80 + 2.25 + 1.60 + 1.05 = 7.70$$

**Step 4:** Rank the suppliers.

| Rank | Supplier | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | **B** | **8.35** |
| 2 | A | 7.95 |
| 3 | C | 7.70 |

**Conclusion:** *Supplier B should be selected based on the highest weighted score, provided the score exceeds the company's threshold (typically 7.5/10 for critical parts).*

---

### 3.4 Comparative Cost Analysis Table

| Production Volume (Q) | TC Make (₹) | TC Buy (₹) | Cheaper Option | Savings (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2,000 | 9,60,000 | 6,20,000 | **Buy** | 3,40,000 |
| 5,000 | 15,00,000 | 14,00,000 | **Buy** | 1,00,000 |
| **6,250 (Break-even)** | **17,25,000** | **17,25,000** | Indifferent | 0 |
| 10,000 | 24,00,000 | 27,00,000 | **Make** | 3,00,000 |
| 20,000 | 42,00,000 | 53,00,000 | **Make** | 11,00,000 |

This sensitivity table is a **mandatory exhibit** for KTU full-mark answers on Make-or-Buy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The 7-Step Strategic Sourcing Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Spend Analysis and Category Profiling] --> B[Step2 Develop Sourcing Strategy]
    B --> C[Step3 Identify Potential Suppliers]
    C --> D[Step4 Supplier Evaluation and Selection]
    D --> E[Step5 Negotiation and Contracting]
    E --> F[Step6 Implementation and Integration]
    F --> G[Step7 Performance Monitoring and Improvement]
    G -->|Feedback Loop| A
```

### 4.2 Make-or-Buy Decision Logic Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Identify Component and Annual Demand Q] --> P2[Gather Cost Data Fm Vm Fb Vb]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Break Even Volume Q star]
    P3 --> P4{Is Q greater than Q star?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Decision MAKE In House]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Decision BUY from Supplier]
    P5 --> P7[Monitor Quality Cost Delivery]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Annual Review and Recompute]
    P8 --> P2
```

### 4.3 Vendor Rating Weighted Score Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Quality 40 percent] --> R[Weighted Total Score Si]
    C2[Delivery 25 percent] --> R
    C3[Price 20 percent] --> R
    C4[Service 15 percent] --> R
    R --> D1{Threshold 7.5}
    D1 -->|Pass| P1[Approved Vendor]
    D1 -->|Fail| P2[Reject or Develop Supplier]
```

### 4.4 TQM Sourcing Ecosystem — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph upstream [Upstream Sourcing Layer]
        U1[Strategic Planning] --> U2[Supplier Identification]
        U2 --> U3[Supplier Qualification ISO 9001 PPAP]
        U3 --> U4[Contract and SLA Setup]
    end
    subgraph midstream [Operational Sourcing Layer]
        M1[Purchase Order Release] --> M2[Incoming Quality Control IQC]
        M2 --> M3[Statistical Process Control SPC at Supplier]
        M3 --> M4[Vendor Scorecard Monthly]
    end
    subgraph downstream [Strategic Review Layer]
        D1[Supplier Development Kaizen] --> D2[Long Term Partnership]
        D2 --> D3[Innovation and Co Creation]
        D3 --> D1
    end
    U4 --> M1
    M4 --> D1
```

### 4.5 Sourcing Decision Tree — Single vs. Multiple vs. Strategic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Identify Item Criticality] --> Q{Is Item Critical to Quality}
    Q -->|Yes| C1{Is Volume High}
    Q -->|No| C2[Use Multiple Sourcing]
    C1 -->|Yes| C3[Strategic Sourcing with 2 to 3 qualified vendors]
    C1 -->|No| C4[Single Sourcing with tight SLA]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2 marks definition + 1 mark key point pattern]**

**Q1. Define sourcing and list any four types of sourcing used in TQM.** `[KTU University Exam – Sample]`

**Model Answer:**
*Sourcing is the strategic process of identifying, evaluating, and selecting suppliers who can provide the required materials, components, or services to meet an organization's quality, cost, and delivery objectives.*
*Four types of sourcing are:*
*1. Single Sourcing*
*2. Multiple Sourcing*
*3. Dual Sourcing*
*4. Strategic Sourcing*

---

**Q2. What is a Make-or-Buy decision? State the break-even formula.** `[KTU University Exam – Sample]`

**Model Answer:**
*The Make-or-Buy decision is a strategic sourcing choice that determines whether a component should be manufactured in-house or purchased from an external supplier based on cost-volume analysis.*
*The break-even volume formula is:*

$$Q^* = \frac{F_m - F_b}{V_b - V_m}$$

*If the actual demand exceeds $Q^*$, the Make option is more economical; otherwise, Buy is preferred.*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Model ESE Question — 14 Marks, Module 2 Choice Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the strategic sourcing process in detail. Discuss the role of vendor rating in supplier selection.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The strategic sourcing process is a structured, data-driven approach to optimize the supply base. The 7 steps are:

1. **Spend Analysis & Category Profiling:** Apply the Pareto Principle to identify high-spend, high-risk categories.
2. **Strategy Development:** Decide Make-or-Buy, sourcing type, and supplier count.
3. **Supplier Identification:** Search industry directories, trade shows, and pre-qualify candidates.
4. **Evaluation & Selection:** Use weighted scoring on Q-C-D-S parameters; conduct capability audits.
5. **Negotiation & Contracting:** Negotiate on TCO, not just unit price.
6. **Implementation:** Align quality systems, integrate via EDI/ERP, and pilot supplies.
7. **Monitoring & Improvement:** Monthly scorecards, Kaizen events, and annual reviews.

**Role of Vendor Rating:**
Vendor rating converts qualitative supplier performance into a quantitative score. By assigning weights $w_j$ to criteria (Quality, Cost, Delivery, Service) and rating each supplier on a 0–10 scale, the organization gets an objective, repeatable selection mechanism aligned with TQM's evidence-based decision-making.

**(b) A company is evaluating three suppliers for a critical component. The criteria and ratings are given below. Compute the weighted score for each supplier and recommend the best vendor.** **[7 Marks]**

| Criterion | Weight | Supplier X | Supplier Y | Supplier Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.50 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
| Delivery | 0.30 | 8 | 9 | 8 |
| Price | 0.20 | 7 | 8 | 9 |

**Solution:**

*Verification of weights: $0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✔*

**Supplier X:**

$$S_X = (0.50 \times 9) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 7) = 4.50 + 2.40 + 1.40 = 8.30$$

**Supplier Y:**

$$S_Y = (0.50 \times 8) + (0.30 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) = 4.00 + 2.70 + 1.60 = 8.30$$

**Supplier Z:**

$$S_Z = (0.50 \times 7) + (0.30 \times 8) + (0.20 \times 9) = 3.50 + 2.40 + 1.80 = 7.70$$

**Recommendation:** *Suppliers X and Y are tied at 8.30. In case of a tie, the organization may break the tie using secondary criteria such as technical capability, financial stability, or past performance. If quality alone is the decisive factor, Supplier X is preferred (raw quality score = 9); if delivery reliability is critical, Supplier Y is preferred (raw delivery score = 9).*

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – Module 2 Alternative Choice — 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the Make-or-Buy decision. Discuss the factors that influence this decision.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The Make-or-Buy decision is a strategic sourcing question that determines whether to manufacture a component in-house or procure it from an external supplier. It is a long-term commitment that affects cost structure, quality control, capacity utilization, and competitive advantage.

**Factors Influencing the Decision:**

1. **Cost:** Comparative analysis of fixed and variable costs.
2. **Quality & Technology:** Proprietary processes may require in-house manufacture.
3. **Capacity:** Available plant capacity and utilization levels.
4. **Reliability of Supply:** Single vs. multiple source risk.
5. **Strategic Importance:** Core competency items are usually Made.
6. **Lead Time & Flexibility:** Sourcing provides agility; making provides control.
7. **Confidentiality & IP Protection:** Sensitive designs are kept in-house.
8. **Government Regulations & Taxes:** Tariffs, duties, MSME preferences.
9. **Availability of Skilled Labor.**
10. **Long-term Vendor Relationship & Volume Discounts.**

**(b) ABC Manufacturing requires 15,000 components per year. The cost data is as follows: Make: Fixed Cost = ₹4,00,000, Variable Cost = ₹120/unit. Buy: Fixed Cost = ₹50,000, Variable Cost = ₹180/unit. Determine the best decision and the cost savings at the actual demand.** **[7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

*Given: $F_m = 4{,}00{,}000$ ; $V_m = 120$ ; $F_b = 50{,}000$ ; $V_b = 180$ ; $Q = 15{,}000$*

**Step 1:** Compute break-even volume.

$$Q^* = \frac{F_m - F_b}{V_b - V_m} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{180 - 120} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{60} = 5{,}833.33 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2:** Compare actual demand with break-even.

$$Q = 15{,}000 > Q^* = 5{,}833.33 \implies \text{Make is the better option.}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Total Cost under Make.

$$TC_m = 4{,}00{,}000 + (120 \times 15{,}000) = 4{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = 22{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 4:** Compute Total Cost under Buy.

$$TC_b = 50{,}000 + (180 \times 15{,}000) = 50{,}000 + 27{,}00{,}000 = 27{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 5:** Compute cost savings.

$$\Delta TC = TC_b - TC_m = 27{,}50{,}000 - 22{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000$$

**Conclusion:** *ABC Manufacturing should **MAKE** the component, resulting in an annual savings of ₹5,50,000.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the break-even calculation first:** Jumping to a decision without computing $Q^*$ is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Confusing Fixed and Variable costs:** Only **avoidable** fixed costs should be considered. Allocated overheads are irrelevant.
