> 3. **Wrong sign convention in differential cost:** Always state $\Delta TC = TC_b - TC_m$. A negative value must be interpreted, not just reported.
> 4. **Ignoring qualitative factors:** Even when Make is cheaper mathematically, the examiner expects a mention of quality, lead time, and strategic risk.
> 5. **Weights not summing to 1:** Always verify $\sum w_j = 1.00$ in vendor rating problems — failure to verify loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the sensitivity table:** Drawing a small "Volume vs. Total Cost" table is worth 1–2 extra marks and demonstrates analytical depth.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam**

- ✅ **Sourcing** = Strategic process of finding, evaluating, and selecting suppliers — NOT just purchasing.
- ✅ **Deming's principle** — Do not award business on price tag alone; consider TCO.
- ✅ **Seven-Step Strategic Sourcing Process** — Profile → Strategy → Identify → Evaluate → Negotiate → Implement → Monitor.
- ✅ **Single Sourcing** = one supplier; **Multiple Sourcing** = many; **Dual Sourcing** = two; **Strategic Sourcing** = data-driven long-term approach.
- ✅ **Make-or-Buy Break-Even Formula:** $Q^* = \dfrac{F_m - F_b}{V_b - V_m}$ — commit to memory.
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** $Q > Q^* \Rightarrow$ Make ; $Q < Q^* \Rightarrow$ Buy.
- ✅ **Vendor Rating Formula:** $S_i = \sum w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ with $\sum w_j = 1$.
- ✅ **TCO includes:** Price + Transport + Inspection + Warranty + Downtime + Stockout.
- ✅ **COPQ at supplier** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- ✅ **Pareto Principle (80/20):** 20% of suppliers typically account for 80% of spend — focus strategic effort here.
- ✅ **Supplier Development** = Long-term capability building via training, joint Kaizen, and shared quality systems.
- ✅ **In KTU answers**, always combine **quantitative (cost/score)** with **qualitative (quality/strategy)** reasoning.
- ✅ **Diagram weightage:** A neatly drawn sourcing process flow or make-or-buy break-even graph can earn 1–2 extra marks.
- ✅ **PPM-based quality rating** is preferred over percentage defect rate for global supplier benchmarking.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Supplier Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supplier Selection** is a strategic, multi-criteria decision-making process within Total Quality Management (TQM) and supply chain management whereby an organization identifies, evaluates, qualifies, and contracts with external vendors (suppliers) who can deliver the right quality of materials, components, and services at the right cost, at the right time, and in the right quantity, in alignment with the organization's quality objectives and customer requirements.

In the TQM philosophy, supplier selection transcends a mere purchasing transaction; it is treated as the establishment of a **strategic partnership**. As articulated by quality gurus like **W. Edwards Deming**, Ishikawa, and elaborated in the philosophy of Kaoru Ishikawa's "next-customer" concept, an organization is intrinsically linked to its suppliers — when the supplier improves, the organization improves, and ultimately, the end customer benefits. Hence, the selection of a supplier is a *quality decision*, not merely a commercial one.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT413 – Module 2):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to master the *supplier selection process*, the *criteria* used for selection, the *categorical and weighted point models*, and the *practical application of multi-criteria decision-making*. This topic frequently fetches 3 to 14 mark questions.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Supplier (Vendor)** | An external entity that provides goods, raw materials, components, or services to the purchasing organization. |
| **Supplier Rating** | A quantitative or qualitative assessment of a supplier's performance across multiple criteria. |
| **Vendor Score** | The numerical composite obtained by multiplying supplier rating by category weight and summing across criteria. |
| **Sole Supplier** | A single-vendor relationship; risky but sometimes necessary (e.g., patented parts). |
| **Multiple Suppliers** | The dominant TQM-recommended approach to foster competition and reduce risk. |
| **Strategic Partnership / Alliance** | A long-term, collaborative relationship with shared quality goals, data, and continuous improvement. |
| **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | The complete cost of procuring and using a product, including purchase price, transportation, inspection, downtime, and warranty costs. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Ingredient Analogy

Imagine you are the head chef of a fine-dining restaurant in Kerala. Your reputation depends on the *taste* of every dish served. If your fish supplier delivers stale catch, no amount of cooking skill can save the *Appam* or the *Fish Molee*. Therefore, you do not pick suppliers based on price alone — you evaluate them on:

- **Freshness** (Quality of produce)
- **Reliability of delivery time** (On-time delivery)
- **Variety and grade** (Product range)
- **Hygiene certifications** (Quality certifications)
- **Past track record with other chefs** (Supplier reputation)

You rate each vendor, weigh each criterion, and select the one whose *total quality score* is highest. **This is exactly how industrial supplier selection works in TQM.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TQM treats supplier selection as critical:**
> Ishikawa estimated that **80% of the variation in final product quality originates from suppliers**. You cannot achieve Six-Sigma-level output if your inputs are full of standard deviation.

## 1.4 The Strategic Importance of Supplier Selection in TQM

1. **Quality of Incoming Material:** The classic "Garbage In, Garbage Out" (GIGO) principle applies — incoming quality sets the upper bound for outgoing quality.
2. **Cost Competitiveness:** Purchased items often constitute **50% to 70% of a manufactured product's cost**. A 5% supplier cost saving can double net profit.
3. **Delivery Performance (T, On-time):** Directly affects production schedules and customer promise dates.
4. **Innovation and Continuous Improvement:** Strategic suppliers contribute ideas, JIT capabilities, and process improvements.
5. **Risk Management:** Over-dependence on a single supplier creates vulnerability; a diversified supplier base insures against disruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's 14 Points — Point 4 (End the practice of awarding business on price tag alone):** Deming insisted that awarding contracts to the lowest bidder *without considering quality* is a primary cause of poor quality. This is a cornerstone of TQM supplier selection philosophy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supplier Selection Process — Step-by-Step

A robust TQM-aligned supplier selection process follows a structured, iterative sequence:

### Step 1 — Identify the Requirement
Define the specification, quantity, quality standards (e.g., ISO 9001, AS9100, customer-specific standards), and timeline of the material or service needed.

### Step 2 — Identify Potential Suppliers
Generate a candidate list through:
- Market surveys
- Trade directories and industry exhibitions
- Recommendations from consultants
- Internal referrals and trade associations
- Reverse engineering of competitor products

### Step 3 — Establish Selection Criteria
Define a *criterion tree* tailored to the material/service. Typical criteria are:

| Category | Examples of Criteria |
|---|---|
| **Quality** | Conformance to specs, defect rate (PPM), quality certifications (ISO 9001, IATF 16949) |
| **Delivery** | On-time delivery rate, lead time, flexibility |
| **Cost** | Unit price, total cost of ownership, payment terms |
| **Capability** | Technical capacity, R&D ability, financial stability |
| **Service** | Responsiveness, warranty support, complaint handling |
| **Risk** | Geographic risk, geopolitical factors, single-source risk |
| **Sustainability / ESG** | Environmental compliance, ethical labor practices |

### Step 4 — Pre-Qualify Suppliers
Filter the candidate list to a shortlist by collecting and verifying:
- Capability surveys/questionnaires
- Sample lots
- Financial statements
- Certification documents
- On-site audits of supplier facilities

### Step 5 — Evaluate and Rate Suppliers
Apply a formal evaluation method (discussed in 2.3) to compute a numerical score for each shortlisted supplier.

### Step 6 — Select and Negotiate
Choose the supplier (or panel) and negotiate contracts including quality clauses, penalties, performance review schedules.

### Step 7 — Monitor, Review, and Develop
Implement an ongoing **Supplier Performance Monitoring (SPM)** system. Re-evaluate periodically. Push underperformers into improvement plans; reward top performers with more business (the *Preferred Supplier* model).

## 2.2 The Single vs. Multiple Supplier Debate

| Aspect | Sole Supplier | Multiple Suppliers |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume Discount** | High (large single order) | Lower (split orders) |
| **Risk** | High — supply disruption catastrophic | Low — redundancy exists |
| **Competition** | None — risk of complacency | Healthy competition drives quality |
| **Relationship Depth** | Can be a true strategic partnership | Often transactional |
| **TQM Recommendation** | Avoid unless necessary (e.g., patents) | **Preferred model** |

## 2.3 Supplier Evaluation Methods

The KTU syllabus emphasizes three classical methods:

### Method A — Categorical Method (Qualitative)

Suppliers are classified into categories based on judgment:
- **Preferred Supplier** — meets/exceeds all criteria
- **Approved Supplier** — meets minimum criteria
- **Conditional Supplier** — needs improvement within a time limit
- **Disqualified Supplier** — fails critical criteria

This is simple, fast, but **subjective**.

### Method B — Weighted Point Model (Quantitative — Most Important)

The most widely used and TQM-preferred method. Each criterion is assigned a **weight** (importance) and each supplier is **rated** on a scale (typically 1 to 5 or 1 to 10). The product of weight × rating is summed to yield a total score.

### Method C — Cost-Ratio Method (TCO-focused)

For each criterion that has a measurable cost impact, compute the ratio of cost to a benchmark. Suppliers are ranked by combined cost-efficiency.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Weighted Vendor Score** | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}$ | $S_i$ = score of supplier $i$; $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$; $r_{ij}$ = rating of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ |
| **Normalization of Weights** | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$ (or 100%) | Weights must sum to unity |
| **Max Possible Score** | $S_{\max} = r_{\max} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j$ | Achieved when a supplier gets the maximum rating on all criteria |
| **Acceptance Threshold** | $S_i \ge S_{\text{threshold}}$ | The minimum acceptable composite score for a supplier to qualify |
| **Cost Per Point Ratio** | $CPPR = \frac{\text{Total Cost}}{S_i}$ | Lower is better; used to break ties |
| **Percentage Achievement** | $\% A_i = \dfrac{S_i}{S_{\max}} \times 100$ | Useful for benchmarking suppliers against the theoretical ideal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** When using the weighted point method in KTU exams, always show the *weight table*, the *rating table*, the *score computation table*, and finally the *ranking*. Examiners award step-marks for each stage.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** IATF 16949 mandates formal supplier selection and monitoring. The "Big Three" OEMs use weighted point models with hundreds of criteria.
- **Aerospace (AS9100D):** Supplier selection is treated as a "Special Process" requiring audit evidence.
- **Pharmaceuticals (cGMP):** FDA expects documented qualification of critical material suppliers.
- **Electronics Manufacturing (CMMC / ISO 9001):** EMS firms rate CMs (Contract Manufacturers) on quality, cost, delivery, technology, and responsiveness (the **QCDTR** framework).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Mathematical Derivation — Weighted Point Model

### 3.1.1 The General Framework

Let there be $m$ suppliers evaluated against $n$ criteria.

- $w_j$ = weight of criterion $j$, with $j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$
- $r_{ij}$ = rating given to supplier $i$ on criterion $j$, with $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$

**Constraints:**
- Weights are non-negative and sum to unity: $w_j \ge 0$ for all $j$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$
- Ratings are bounded: $r_{\min} \le r_{ij} \le r_{\max}$ (typically $1 \le r_{ij} \le 5$ or $1 \le r_{ij} \le 10$)

**Composite Score of Supplier $i$:**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

**Ranking:** Sort suppliers in **descending order of $S_i$**. The supplier with the highest $S_i$ is selected.

### 3.1.2 Normalization Proof

We need to show that when weights sum to 1, the maximum achievable score equals the maximum possible rating. The maximum rating is $r_{\max}$. The maximum composite score is:

$$
S_{\max} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{\max} = r_{\max} \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j
$$

Since $\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j = 1$, we get:

$$
S_{\max} = r_{\max} \cdot 1 = r_{\max}
$$

This confirms that the theoretical maximum composite score equals the maximum rating value, ensuring the scale remains interpretable.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A precision engineering company in Kochi is selecting a supplier for a critical gearbox component. Four candidates (A, B, C, D) are evaluated on five criteria. Weights are decided by the cross-functional team as follows:

| Criterion ($j$) | Weight ($w_j$) |
|---|---|
| 1. Quality (defect rate) | 0.30 |
| 2. On-time delivery | 0.20 |
| 3. Cost (competitiveness) | 0.20 |
| 4. Technical capability | 0.15 |
| 5. Responsiveness / Service | 0.15 |

Verify: $\sum_{j=1}^{5} w_j = 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

The cross-functional team rates each supplier on a scale of **1 to 5** (1 = poor, 5 = excellent):

| Criterion | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Quality | 4 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| 2. Delivery | 3 | 4 | 4 | 3 |
| 3. Cost | 5 | 3 | 4 | 3 |
| 4. Technical | 4 | 4 | 3 | 5 |
| 5. Service | 3 | 4 | 4 | 4 |

**Step 1 — Calculate $S_i$ for each supplier.**

For Supplier A:

$$
S_A = (0.30 \cdot 4) + (0.20 \cdot 3) + (0.20 \cdot 5) + (0.15 \cdot 4) + (0.15 \cdot 3)
$$

$$
S_A = 1.20 + 0.60 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.45 = 3.85
$$

For Supplier B:

$$
S_B = (0.30 \cdot 5) + (0.20 \cdot 4) + (0.20 \cdot 3) + (0.15 \cdot 4) + (0.15 \cdot 4)
$$

$$
S_B = 1.50 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.60 = 4.10
$$

For Supplier C:

$$
S_C = (0.30 \cdot 3) + (0.20 \cdot 4) + (0.20 \cdot 4) + (0.15 \cdot 3) + (0.15 \cdot 4)
$$

$$
S_C = 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.60 = 3.55
$$

For Supplier D:

$$
S_D = (0.30 \cdot 4) + (0.20 \cdot 3) + (0.20 \cdot 3) + (0.15 \cdot 5) + (0.15 \cdot 4)
$$

$$
S_D = 1.20 + 0.60 + 0.60 + 0.75 + 0.60 = 3.75
$$

**Step 2 — Determine Maximum Possible Score and Percentage Achievement.**

Since $r_{\max} = 5$ and weights sum to 1:

$$
S_{\max} = 5.00
$$

| Supplier | $S_i$ | $\%A_i = \frac{S_i}{5.00} \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 3.85 | 77.0% |
| **B** | **4.10** | **82.0%** |
| C | 3.55 | 71.0% |
| D | 3.75 | 75.0% |

**Step 3 — Ranking.**

$$
\text{Ranking: } B \ (4.10) \succ A \ (3.85) \succ D \ (3.75) \succ C \ (3.55)
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.** Supplier **B** is selected as the primary supplier. If the company adopts a dual-sourcing policy, it may select B and A as the approved supplier panel.

### 3.1.4 Tie-Breaking Using Cost Per Point Ratio (CPPR)

Suppose B and A had identical scores. The KTU syllabus recommends using the **Cost Per Point Ratio** to break ties.

Let $C_i$ be the total annualized cost of supplier $i$. Then:

$$
CPPR_i = \frac{C_i}{S_i}
$$

The supplier with the **lowest CPPR** is preferred.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** CPPR is a practical bridge between qualitative weighted scoring and Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) analysis. It allows the cross-functional team to make a *defensible* decision when scores are close.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Criteria-Centric View

| Criterion Class | Typical Weight Range | Data Source | Measurement Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 30% – 50% | Incoming inspection records, PPM defects | Defects per million, % reject |
| Delivery | 15% – 25% | Logistics records, receiving logs | % on-time, days early/late |
| Cost | 15% – 30% | Purchase orders, invoices | INR/unit, INR/year |
| Capability | 10% – 20% | Audits, surveys, certifications | Score, certification level |
| Service | 5% – 15% | Internal feedback, response time | Days to close complaint |
| Risk | 5% – 10% | Risk register, geopolitical index | Score, qualitative label |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Answers:** Always justify the *weight assignment* using a rationale (e.g., "Quality is weighted highest because this is a safety-critical component in a medical device"). Examiners reward logical justification.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Supplier Selection Process Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Identify Requirement] --> stepB[Generate Candidate Supplier List]
    stepB --> stepC[Define Selection Criteria and Weights]
    stepC --> stepD[Issue Capability Questionnaire]
    stepD --> stepE[Conduct On-Site Supplier Audit]
    stepE --> stepF{Supplier Meets Pre-Qualification Criteria?}
    stepF -- No --> stepG[Reject and Notify Supplier]
    stepF -- Yes --> stepH[Collect Samples and Evaluate]
    stepH --> stepI[Rate Each Supplier on Each Criterion]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute Weighted Score Using Formula]
    stepJ --> stepK{Score Above Acceptance Threshold?}
    stepK -- No --> stepL[Conditional Approval with Improvement Plan]
    stepK -- Yes --> stepM[Negotiate Contract and SLA]
    stepL --> stepM
    stepM --> stepN[Establish Ongoing Performance Monitoring]
    stepN --> stepO[Periodic Re-Evaluation and Re-Rating]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart visualizes the end-to-end supplier selection process as a closed-loop system — the *periodic re-evaluation* ensures that TQM's Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle is honored continuously, not just at onboarding.

## 4.2 Weighted Point Model — Computation Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp1[Criteria List] --> inp2[Weight Assignment Table]
    inp3[Supplier Rating Matrix] --> inp4[Weight Multiplication Step]
    inp2 --> inp4
    inp4 --> inp5[Sum Across Criteria for Each Supplier]
    inp5 --> inp6[Compute Total Score S sub i]
    inp6 --> inp7[Rank Suppliers in Descending Order]
    inp7 --> inp8[Apply Tie Breaker CPPR if Needed]
    inp8 --> out1[Final Selected Supplier]
```

## 4.3 Multi-Criteria Decision Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Supplier Selection Decision]
    root --> q[Quality]
    root --> d[Delivery]
    root --> c[Cost]
    root --> t[Technology and Capability]
    root --> s[Service and Responsiveness]
    q --> q1[Defect Rate]
    q --> q2[Certifications]
    d --> d1[On Time Percentage]
    d --> d2[Lead Time]
    c --> c1[Unit Price]
    c --> c2[Total Cost of Ownership]
    t --> t1[Technical Audit Score]
    s --> s1[Response Time]
```

## 4.4 Strategic vs. Transactional Supplier Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    trad[Transactional Relationship]
    strat[Strategic Partnership]
    trad --> tr1[Short Term Contracts]
    trad --> tr2[Price Driven]
    trad --> tr3[Multiple Suppliers]
    trad --> tr4[Minimal Information Sharing]
    strat --> st1[Long Term Contracts]
    strat --> st2[Value Driven TCO]
    strat --> st3[Single or Dual Source]
    strat --> st4[Open Books and Joint Improvement]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** TQM advocates moving from *Transactional* to *Strategic* relationships. The supplier is treated as an *extension of the organization*. The famous "Supplier-Input-Process-Output-Customer" (SIPOC) chain in Six Sigma formalizes this thinking.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Explain briefly the importance of supplier selection in Total Quality Management.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Supplier selection is a critical TQM activity because the quality of incoming materials and components directly determines the quality of the final product — Ishikawa estimated that 80% of quality variation originates from suppliers. A poor supplier can disrupt production schedules, inflate warranty costs, and damage brand reputation. TQM emphasizes selecting suppliers not solely on price (as per Deming's Point 4) but on a multi-criteria evaluation covering quality, delivery, cost, capability, and service. The process establishes strategic partnerships that drive continuous improvement, reduce total cost of ownership, and ensure the organization's ability to consistently meet customer quality requirements.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**List any six criteria commonly used in supplier evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The six commonly used criteria in supplier evaluation are:
1. **Quality** of supplied material (defect rate, conformance to specifications)
2. **On-time delivery** performance
3. **Cost competitiveness** (unit price and total cost of ownership)
4. **Technical capability** and R&D strength
5. **Responsiveness and service** (warranty, complaint handling)
6. **Financial stability and certifications** (ISO 9001, IATF 16949)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

(a) **Explain the supplier selection process in detail. List the various criteria used for supplier evaluation.** (7 Marks)

(b) **The ABC Engineering Company, Ernakulam, wants to select a supplier for a critical machined component. Three suppliers (X, Y, Z) are evaluated on four criteria with weights 0.40, 0.25, 0.20, 0.15. The ratings on a scale of 1 to 5 are given below. Calculate the weighted scores, rank the suppliers, and recommend the best supplier with justification.** (7 Marks)

| Criterion | Weight | X | Y | Z |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.40 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| Delivery | 0.25 | 3 | 4 | 4 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| Service | 0.15 | 3 | 4 | 4 |

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Supplier Selection Process (7 Marks):**

The supplier selection process consists of the following steps:
1. **Identify the requirement** — clearly define the technical specification, quality standard, quantity, and delivery schedule of the material.
2. **Generate a list of potential suppliers** through market surveys, trade directories, referrals, and industry exhibitions.
3. **Establish selection criteria and assign weights** using a cross-functional team (purchasing, quality, engineering, finance).
4. **Pre-qualify suppliers** by sending capability questionnaires, collecting samples, and verifying certifications.
5. **Conduct on-site supplier audits** to verify manufacturing capability and quality systems.
6. **Rate each shortlisted supplier** on every criterion using a standard rating scale.
7. **Compute weighted scores** and rank suppliers; apply tie-breakers (e.g., CPPR) if needed.
8. **Select the best supplier** and negotiate a contract with clear quality clauses and SLAs.
9. **Establish ongoing performance monitoring** with periodic re-evaluation (PDCA cycle).

**Criteria used for evaluation include:** Quality (defect rate, conformance), Delivery (on-time % and lead time), Cost (price, TCO), Technical capability, Service and responsiveness, Financial stability, Risk profile, and ESG/Sustainability compliance.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Verify weight sum: $0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

**Weighted score for Supplier X:**

$$
S_X = (0.40 \cdot 4) + (0.25 \cdot 3) + (0.20 \cdot 5) + (0.15 \cdot 3)
$$

$$
S_X = 1.60 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.45 = 3.80
$$

**Weighted score for Supplier Y:**

$$
S_Y = (0.40 \cdot 5) + (0.25 \cdot 4) + (0.20 \cdot 3) + (0.15 \cdot 4)
$$

$$
S_Y = 2.00 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.60 = 4.20
$$

**Weighted score for Supplier Z:**

$$
S_Z = (0.40 \cdot 3) + (0.25 \cdot 4) + (0.20 \cdot 4) + (0.15 \cdot 4)
$$

$$
S_Z = 1.20 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.60 = 3.60
$$

**Maximum possible score:** $S_{\max} = 5.00$

| Supplier | $S_i$ | $\%A_i$ | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|
| X | 3.80 | 76% | 2 |
| **Y** | **4.20** | **84%** | **1** |
| Z | 3.60 | 72% | 3 |

**Recommendation:** Supplier **Y** should be selected with a weighted score of **4.20 (84% achievement)**, the highest among the three. Y excels in Quality and Delivery — the two highest-weighted criteria — making it the most strategically aligned choice for a critical machined component.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

(a) **Discuss the various methods of supplier evaluation. Compare the categorical method and the weighted point model.** (7 Marks)

(b) **A firm uses three criteria — Quality (weight 0.50), Delivery (weight 0.30), and Cost (weight 0.20) — to evaluate two suppliers, P and Q. Ratings on a 10-point scale are:**

| Criterion | Weight | P | Q |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quality | 0.50 | 8 | 7 |
| Delivery | 0.30 | 7 | 9 |
| Cost | 0.20 | 9 | 6 |

**Calculate the weighted scores, find the percentage achievement of each supplier, and decide which supplier is preferable.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Methods of Supplier Evaluation (7 Marks):**

The major methods of supplier evaluation are:

1. **Categorical Method:** A qualitative approach where suppliers are classified into categories such as *Preferred*, *Approved*, *Conditional*, or *Disqualified* based on expert judgment. It is simple and quick but subjective.

2. **Weighted Point Model:** A quantitative approach where each criterion is assigned a weight based on importance and each supplier is rated on a numerical scale. The composite score is the sum of weight × rating. It is the most widely used and rigorous method.

3. **Cost-Ratio Method:** Focuses on the cost impact of each criterion, comparing the cost per unit of value delivered.

4. **Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP):** A structured pairwise comparison technique for complex multi-criteria decisions.

5. **Vendor Performance Index (VPI):** A continuous monitoring index combining quality, delivery, and cost performance over time.

**Comparison: Categorical vs. Weighted Point Model**

| Aspect | Categorical Method | Weighted Point Model |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Qualitative | Quantitative |
| Subjectivity | High | Low (with proper calibration) |
| Granularity | Coarse (4 categories) | Fine (continuous score) |
| Auditability | Difficult to defend | Easy to defend with numbers |
| Use Case | Initial screening, low-risk items | Critical and high-value items |
| TQM Alignment | Limited | **Strong (Deming's Point 4)** |

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Verify weight sum: $0.50 + 0.30 + 0.20 = 1.00$ ✓

**Score for Supplier P:**

$$
S_P = (0.50 \cdot 8) + (0.30 \cdot 7) + (0.20 \cdot 9)
$$

$$
S_P = 4.00 + 2.10 + 1.80 = 7.90
$$

**Score for Supplier Q:**

$$
S_Q = (0.50 \cdot 7) + (0.30 \cdot 9) + (0.20 \cdot 6)
$$

$$
S_Q = 3.50 + 2.70 + 1.20 = 7.40
$$

**Maximum possible score:** $S_{\max} = 10.00$

| Supplier | $S_i$ | $\%A_i = \frac{S_i}{10.00} \times 100$ |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | **7.90** | **79.0%** |
| Q | 7.40 | 74.0% |

**Decision:** Supplier **P** is preferable with a weighted score of **7.90 (79% achievement)**. Although Q excels in Delivery (rating 9), P's strong showing in Quality (rating 8) — the most heavily weighted criterion at 50% — gives P the decisive edge.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize weights** to sum to 1 (or 100%). If weights do not sum to 1, the maximum score is not interpretable, and the answer is marked wrong.
> 2. **Showing only the final score** without the intermediate weight × rating products. Examiners award step-marks for each multiplication.
> 3. **Not justifying the weight assignment.** Always state the rationale (e.g., "Quality is weighted highest because the component is safety-critical").
> 4. **Confusing *categorical* and *weighted point* methods.** Categorical is qualitative and based on judgment; weighted point is quantitative and uses a numerical scale.
> 5. **Ignoring the cost / price trade-off** — TQM does not select the cheapest supplier; it selects the supplier offering the *best total value* (TCO).
> 6. **Failing to mention ongoing monitoring** — TQM treats supplier selection as the *start* of a relationship, not a one-time event.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Supplier Selection is a strategic TQM decision**, not a transactional purchase. It directly impacts incoming quality, cost, and delivery performance.
- **Ishikawa's 80% rule** — most quality variation originates from suppliers; therefore, supplier quality is non-negotiable.
- **Deming's Point 4** — do not award business on price tag alone. Consider quality, delivery, and total cost of ownership.
- The **supplier selection process** has 7–9 sequential steps: identify requirement → generate candidates → define criteria → pre-qualify → audit → rate → compute scores → select → monitor.
- The **Weighted Point Model** is the KTU-favored method. The composite score formula is:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot r_{ij}
$$

- **Weights must sum to 1** (or 100%) for the scoring scale to be interpretable.
- **Percentage Achievement** $(\%A_i = \frac{S_i}{S_{\max}} \times 100)$ helps compare suppliers against the theoretical ideal.
- **Tie-breaking** is done using the Cost Per Point Ratio (CPPR = Cost ÷ Score) — lower is better.
- **TQM recommends multiple sourcing** (or at least dual sourcing) to reduce supply chain risk while encouraging healthy competition.
- The relationship should evolve from **transactional** (price-driven) to **strategic** (partnership with shared quality goals).
- **Categorical Method** is a quick qualitative screen; **Weighted Point Model** is a rigorous quantitative ranking.
- Always document the **rationale for weights**, the **rating data source**, and the **monitoring plan** — this is the hallmark of a TQM-grade decision.
- For KTU Part B answers, structure your solution as: **(i) weights table → (ii) ratings table → (iii) stepwise computation → (iv) ranking table → (v) recommendation with justification.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supplier Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Supplier Rating # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Supplier Rating** is a systematic, quantitative, and continuous evaluation methodology used by an organization to assess, measure, and rank the performance of its external suppliers (vendors) against a predefined set of criteria. It is a critical component of **Supplier Quality Management (SQM)** and forms the backbone of **Supplier Partnership** in the philosophy of Total Quality Management (TQM). In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT413 - Total Quality Management)**, supplier rating is formally defined as the process of converting qualitative vendor performance data into a measurable numerical score, enabling objective decisions regarding sourcing, allocation of purchase volumes, and continuation of business relationships. The standard criteria universally adopted by industry (and prescribed in TQM literature consistent with KTU modules) for supplier rating are: | # | Criterion | Core Question Answered | |---|-----------|------------------------| | 1 | **Quality** | Does the supplied item meet specifications? | | 2 | **Delivery** | Is the supplier on-time and reliable? | | 3 | **Price** | Is the cost competitive and fair? | | 4 | **Service** | Does the supplier provide after-sales support? | | 5 | **Capacity** | Can the supplier handle volume requirements? | | 6 | **Technology** | Is the supplier technically competent? | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Choosing a Swiggy / Zomato Restaurant** > > Imagine you order food frequently from a food delivery app. Before trusting a restaurant, you instinctively look at: ⭐ Star Rating (Quality), 🕒 Delivery Time (Delivery), 💰 Cost (Price), and 📞 Customer Support (Service). Based on these, you mentally score them and decide whom to order from regularly. > > **Supplier Rating is EXACTLY this — but formalized for industrial purchasing.** Instead of gut-feeling, companies use mathematical scoring systems to decide: *Should we buy 1,000,000 components from Supplier A or Supplier B?* **Why is Supplier Rating so critical in TQM?** According to the **1-10-100 Rule** (also called the Rule of Ten) in quality management: - **\$1** spent on prevention (good supplier rating) saves - **\$10** spent on appraisal - **\$100** spent on failure (defective parts reaching customers) A poor-rated supplier leads to defective inputs, which TQM philosophy identifies as the *root cause* of internal production failures. ## 1.3 Highlight: Standard Rating Metric The most widely-used universal metric in TQM for supplier rating is the **Weighted Point Method Score (out of 100)**, where the final rating is expressed as a percentage. The benchmark threshold for an "Approved Vendor" in most ISO 9001 / TQM-compliant organizations is **≥ 80%**. > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall Box:** Supplier Rating = A scoring tool to rank vendors based on weighted performance criteria. Threshold ≥ 80% = Approved Vendor. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why Supplier Rating? In the **TQM value chain**, suppliers are the *first* link. Deming's philosophy states that **94% of quality problems originate from the system and suppliers**, not from workers on the shop floor. Therefore, monitoring supplier performance through rating ensures: 1. **Conformance to specifications** at the input stage. 2. **Reduction in incoming inspection costs**. 3. **Justification for single sourcing vs. multiple sourcing**. 4. **Continuous improvement** via feedback to vendors. 5. **Risk mitigation** in supply chain disruptions. ## 2.2 Methods of Supplier Rating There are **three primary methods** of supplier rating taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus: ### Method 1: Categorical (Subjective / Pass-Fail) Method The oldest and simplest method. Suppliers are classified into categories such as: - **Preferred** - **Approved** - **Conditional** - **Disqualified** ### Method 2: Weighted Point Method (Most Important for KTU) Each criterion is assigned a **weight (W)** based on its strategic importance. The supplier's performance on each criterion is scored (typically **0–100**), and the **Weighted Score** is computed using the formula below. ### Method 3: Cost Ratio Method Suppliers are evaluated purely on the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO), which includes price, rejection cost, late delivery penalties, etc. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET** for supplier rating problems in KTU examinations. Memorize this table verbatim. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \times P_{ij}$ | Weighted Score of Supplier $i$ | Dimensionless (score) | | 2 | $S_{norm} = \dfrac{S_i}{S_{max}} \times 100$ | Normalized Rating (%) | Percentage (%) | | 3 | $W_{j} = \dfrac{I_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} I_j}$ | Weight of criterion $j$ from Importance | Decimal (sums to 1) | | 4 | $DPMO_{supplier} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | ppm | | 5 | $PPM_{incoming} = \dfrac{\text{Defective units}}{\text{Total units received}} \times 10^6$ | Incoming Quality PPM | ppm | | 6 | $\text{On-Time Delivery \%} = \dfrac{\text{Deliveries on time}}{\text{Total deliveries}} \times 100$ | Delivery Performance | Percentage (%) | | 7 | $\text{Quality Score} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Defective units}}{\text{Total units}}\right) \times 100$ | Quality Index | Percentage (%) | | 8 | $TCO = P + C_{insp} + C_{rej} + C_{delay} + C_{down}$ | Total Cost of Ownership | Currency (₹/\$) | ### Symbol Glossary | Symbol | Meaning | |--------|---------| | $S_i$ | Weighted score of supplier $i$ | | $W_j$ | Weight assigned to criterion $j$ (between 0 and 1) | | $P_{ij}$ | Performance score of supplier $i$ on criterion $j$ (typically 0–100) | | $n$ | Total number of evaluation criteria | | $I_j$ | Importance rating of criterion $j$ (subjective, e.g., 1–10) | | $S_{max}$ | Maximum possible score (when all $P_{ij} = 100$) | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering In manufacturing industries (automotive, aerospace, electronics), the **Weighted Point Method** is the de-facto standard: - **Toyota Production System (TPS):** Uses annual supplier rating to decide on annual contract volumes. - **Boeing / Airbus:** Uses supplier rating with quality carrying 40–50% weight, delivery 25%, cost 20%, service 5%. - **Indian Railways & BHEL:** Uses categorical rating for vendor approval under public procurement norms. ## 2.5 Typical Weight Distribution (Industry Benchmark) | Criterion | Weight Range (%) | |-----------|------------------| | Quality | **40 – 50** | | Delivery | **25 – 30** | | Price / Cost | **15 – 20** | | Service & Support | **5 – 10** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Quality always carries the **highest weight** in TQM-aligned supplier rating systems. This is in stark contrast to traditional purchasing where price was dominant — a paradigm shift introduced by **TQM philosophy**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Point Method ### Step 1: Identify Evaluation Criteria Let there be $n$ criteria: $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$ (e.g., Quality, Delivery, Price, Service). ### Step 2: Assign Weights to Each Criterion Each criterion $C_j$ is assigned an importance value $I_j$. The normalized weight is: $$ W_j = \dfrac{I_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} I_j} $$ **Constraint:** The sum of all weights must equal 1: $$ \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j = 1 $$ ### Step 3: Score Each Supplier on Each Criterion For each supplier $i$ and criterion $j$, assign a performance score $P_{ij} \in [0, 100]$. ### Step 4: Compute the Weighted Score The weighted score of supplier $i$ is: $$ S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \times P_{ij} $$ **Proof (Maximum Score Verification):** If a supplier scores the maximum 100 on every criterion: $$ S_{max} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \times 100 = 100 \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j = 100 \times 1 = 100 $$ This confirms that $S_i$ is naturally bounded between 0 and 100. ### Step 5: Normalize and Rank Since $S_{max} = 100$, the normalized rating percentage is: $$ R_i \,(\%) = S_i $$ Suppliers are ranked in descending order of $S_i$. ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the EXACT pattern KTU examiners use for 14-mark Part B questions. Master this example.** ### Problem Statement A manufacturing company wants to rate two suppliers (Supplier A and Supplier B) based on **four criteria**: Quality, Delivery, Price, and Service. The company assigns the following importance ratings and obtains the following performance scores. Compute the weighted rating and determine which supplier should be preferred. | Criterion | Importance ($I_j$) | Supplier A Score ($P_A$) | Supplier B Score ($P_B$) | |-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | Quality | 10 | 90 | 80 | | Delivery | 6 | 70 | 90 | | Price | 4 | 80 | 70 | | Service | 2 | 60 | 100 | ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Compute normalized weights** $$ \sum I_j = 10 + 6 + 4 + 2 = 22 $$ $$ W_{Quality} = \frac{10}{22} = 0.4545 $$ $$ W_{Delivery} = \frac{6}{22} = 0.2727 $$ $$ W_{Price} = \frac{4}{22} = 0.1818 $$ $$ W_{Service} = \frac{2}{22} = 0.0909 $$ **Verification:** $0.4545 + 0.2727 + 0.1818 + 0.0909 = 1.0000$ ✓ **Step 2: Compute weighted score for Supplier A** $$ S_A = (0.4545 \times 90) + (0.2727 \times 70) + (0.1818 \times 80) + (0.0909 \times 60) $$ Compute each term: $$ 0.4545 \times 90 = 40.909 $$ $$ 0.2727 \times 70 = 19.091 $$ $$ 0.1818 \times 80 = 14.545 $$ $$ 0.0909 \times 60 = 5.455 $$ Summing: $$ S_A = 40.909 + 19.091 + 14.545 + 5.455 = 80.00 $$ **Step 3: Compute weighted score for Supplier B** $$ S_B = (0.4545 \times 80) + (0.2727 \times 90) + (0.1818 \times 70) + (0.0909 \times 100) $$ Compute each term: $$ 0.4545 \times 80 = 36.364 $$ $$ 0.2727 \times 90 = 24.545 $$ $$ 0.1818 \times 70 = 12.727 $$ $$ 0.0909 \times 100 = 9.091 $$ Summing: $$ S_B = 36.364 + 24.545 + 12.727 + 9.091 = 82.73 $$ **Step 4: Decision** | Supplier | Weighted Score | Rank | Status | |----------|---------------|------|--------| | Supplier A | **80.00** | 2 | Approved (≥ 80%) | | Supplier B | **82.73** | 1 | Preferred Vendor | **Conclusion:** **Supplier B should be preferred**, as it has a higher weighted rating (82.73%) than Supplier A (80.00%), even though Supplier A scored higher on Quality — because Delivery and Service were more strongly performed by Supplier B. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensitivity Analysis > [!NOTE] > **Sensitivity Analysis** — what happens when weights change? | Scenario | Quality Weight | Delivery Weight | Winner | |----------|---------------|-----------------|--------| | Quality-Focused (as in TQM) | 50% | 25% | Supplier A (better quality) | | Balanced (original) | 45.45% | 27.27% | Supplier B | | Delivery-Focused | 25% | 50% | Supplier B | **KTU Insight:** This sensitivity matrix is a favorite 14-mark sub-question: *"If the company decides to give equal weight to all four criteria, which supplier would be preferred?"* **Solution for Equal Weights:** $$ W_j = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ for all } j $$ $$ S_A = 0.25(90 + 70 + 80 + 60) = 0.25 \times 300 = 75.00 $$ $$ S_B = 0.25(80 + 90 + 70 + 100) = 0.25 \times 340 = 85.00 $$ **Conclusion:** With equal weights, Supplier B wins by an even larger margin (85% vs 75%). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Supplier Rating Process > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters. Labels contain clean uppercase text only. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[START SUPPLIER RATING PROCESS] --> B1[DEFINE EVALUATION CRITERIA] B1 --> B2[QUALITY] B1 --> B3[DELIVERY] B1 --> B4[PRICE] B1 --> B5[SERVICE] B1 --> B6[CAPACITY] B2 --> C1[ASSIGN IMPORTANCE RATINGS] B3 --> C1 B4 --> C1 B5 --> C1 B6 --> C1 C1 --> D1[CALCULATE NORMALIZED WEIGHTS Wj] D1 --> E1[COLLECT SUPPLIER PERFORMANCE DATA] E1 --> E2[INCOMING INSPECTION RECORDS] E1 --> E3[DELIVERY LOGS] E1 --> E4[COST DATA] E2 --> F1[COMPUTE PERFORMANCE SCORES Pij] E3 --> F1 E4 --> F1 F1 --> G1[CALCULATE WEIGHTED SCORE Si] G1 --> H1{Si GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO 80} H1 -->|YES| I1[APPROVED VENDOR] H1 -->|NO| I2[CONDITIONAL OR REJECT] I1 --> J1[FEEDBACK TO SUPPLIER FOR CI] I2 --> J1 J1 --> K1[RE-RATE AFTER 6 MONTHS] K1 --> A1 style A1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff style G1 fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff style H1 fill:#d62728,color:#fff style I1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff style I2 fill:#9467bd,color:#fff style K1 fill:#8c564b,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Criteria Hierarchy with Typical Weights ```mermaid flowchart LR R[SUPPLIER RATING SYSTEM] --> Q[QUALITY - 45%] R --> D[DELIVERY - 25%] R --> P[PRICE - 20%] R --> S[SERVICE - 10%] Q --> Q1[CONFORMANCE TO SPECS] Q --> Q2[DEFECT RATE PPM] Q --> Q3[CERTIFICATION ISO] D --> D1[ON TIME DELIVERY] D --> D2[LEAD TIME] D --> D3[QUANTITY ACCURACY] P --> P1[UNIT PRICE] P --> P2[PAYMENT TERMS] P --> P3[TOTAL COST] S --> S1[AFTER SALES SUPPORT] S --> S2[COMPLAINT RESPONSE] S --> S3[TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE] style R fill:#003049,color:#fff style Q fill:#d62828,color:#fff style D fill:#f77f00,color:#fff style P fill:#fcbf49,color:#000 style S fill:#06aed5,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Feedback Loop Diagram (PDCA Application to Supplier Rating) ```mermaid flowchart LR P[PLAN
DEFINE CRITERIA AND WEIGHTS] --> D[DO
COLLECT DATA AND SCORE] D --> C[CHECK
COMPUTE WEIGHTED SCORE] C --> A[ACT
APPROVE OR REJECT SUPPLIER] A --> P style P fill:#06aed5,color:#fff style D fill:#086788,color:#fff style C fill:#f0a202,color:#000 style A fill:#d62246,color:#fff ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Pearl:** The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** by Deming is applied iteratively to supplier rating — re-rating is done every 6 or 12 months to ensure continuous improvement. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Q: Define Supplier Rating. List any four criteria used for evaluating suppliers in a TQM environment.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Supplier Rating is a systematic quantitative method used by an organization to evaluate and rank its vendors based on their performance on multiple pre-defined criteria. It enables objective decision-making regarding vendor selection, order allocation, and supplier development. **Four Criteria (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Quality** — Conformance to specifications and defect rate. 2. **Delivery** — On-time delivery performance and lead-time adherence. 3. **Price** — Competitiveness and fairness of quoted costs. 4. **Service** — After-sales support and complaint responsiveness. *(Alternative: Capacity, Technology, Financial Stability, Communication.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Q: State the significance of the Weighted Point Method in supplier rating.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Weighted Point Method is the most widely used TQM technique for supplier rating due to the following reasons: 1. **Objectivity (1 mark):** It converts qualitative vendor performance into a single numerical score, removing personal bias in vendor approval decisions. 2. **Customization (1 mark):** It allows the organization to assign strategic importance (weights) to different criteria — for instance, quality can be weighted at 50%, reflecting TQM philosophy. 3. **Comparability (1 mark):** It enables direct comparison of multiple suppliers on a uniform 0–100 scale, facilitating rank-based decisions on order allocation and vendor retention. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > - **DO NOT** skip the weight normalization step. Many students directly multiply raw importance values with scores and lose **3–4 marks**. > - **DO NOT** forget to verify that weights sum to 1. Examiners specifically check this — losing **2 marks** if omitted. > - **ALWAYS** state the formula symbolically **before** substituting numerical values. Examiners award marks for the formula statement. > - **ROUND FINAL ANSWERS** to 2 decimal places. Unrounded answers lose marks. > - When comparing two suppliers, **ALWAYS conclude with a clear recommendation**, not just the calculation. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] **Q: A company procures a critical component from three suppliers — X, Y, and Z. The procurement manager has identified four evaluation criteria and collected the following performance data:** | Criterion | Weight | Supplier X | Supplier Y | Supplier Z | |-----------|--------|------------|------------|------------| | Quality | 0.40 | 85 | 90 | 80 | | Delivery | 0.30 | 70 | 80 | 95 | | Price | 0.20 | 90 | 75 | 70 | | Service | 0.10 | 80 | 85 | 95 | **(a) Calculate the weighted rating score for each supplier and rank them. (7 marks)** **(b) If the company decides to award 60% of the annual contract to the highest-rated supplier, 30% to the second, and 10% to the third, compute the expected weighted average quality performance of the supply base. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: State the Formula (1 mark)** $$ S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_j \times P_{ij} $$ **Step 2: Verify weights sum to 1 (0.5 marks)** $$ 0.40 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \text{ ✓} $$ **Step 3: Compute $S_X$ (2 marks)** $$ S_X = (0.40 \times 85) + (0.30 \times 70) + (0.20 \times 90) + (0.10 \times 80) $$ $$ S_X = 34.00 + 21.00 + 18.00 + 8.00 = 81.00 $$ **Step 4: Compute $S_Y$ (2 marks)** $$ S_Y = (0.40 \times 90) + (0.30 \times 80) + (0.20 \times 75) + (0.10 \times 85) $$ $$ S_Y = 36.00 + 24.00 + 15.00 + 8.50 = 83.50 $$ **Step 5: Compute $S_Z$ (1 mark)** $$ S_Z = (0.40 \times 80) + (0.30 \times 95) + (0.20 \times 70) + (0.10 \times 95) $$ $$ S_Z = 32.00 + 28.50 + 14.00 + 9.50 = 84.00 $$ **Step 6: Ranking and Recommendation (0.5 marks)** | Rank | Supplier | Score | Status | |------|----------|-------|--------| | 1 | Z | **84.00** | Preferred | | 2 | Y | **83.50** | Approved | | 3 | X | **81.00** | Approved | **Conclusion:** Supplier **Z** should be the preferred vendor with a weighted rating of **84.00%**. --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Identify the quality scores of each supplier (0.5 marks)** From the table: - Supplier X: Quality = 85 - Supplier Y: Quality = 90 - Supplier Z: Quality = 80 **Step 2: Identify contract allocation (0.5 marks)** - Supplier Z (highest rated) gets **60%** of contract. - Supplier Y (second) gets **30%** of contract. - Supplier X (third) gets **10%** of contract. **Step 3: Apply weighted average formula (1 mark)** $$ \overline{Q} = (W_X \times Q_X) + (W_Y \times Q_Y) + (W_Z \times Q_Z) $$ $$ \overline{Q} = (0.10 \times 85) + (0.30 \times 90) + (0.60 \times 80) $$ **Step 4: Compute each term (2 marks)** $$ 0.10 \times 85 = 8.50 $$ $$ 0.30 \times 90 = 27.00 $$ $$ 0.60 \times 80 = 48.00 $$ **Step 5: Sum and conclude (2 marks)** $$ \overline{Q} = 8.50 + 27.00 + 48.00 = 83.50 $$ **Conclusion:** The expected weighted average quality performance of the supply base is **83.50%**, which is above the 80% threshold for an approved vendor base. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] **Q: A TQM-oriented organization wishes to evaluate two vendors, Vendor P and Vendor Q, using four criteria. The importance ratings and performance scores are tabulated below:** | Criterion | Importance | Vendor P Score | Vendor Q Score | |-----------|-----------|----------------|----------------| | Quality | 8 | 75 | 95 | | Delivery | 5 | 90 | 70 | | Price | 4 | 80 | 80 | | Service | 3 | 100 | 60 | **(a) Compute the normalized weights and the weighted rating for each vendor. Identify the preferred vendor. (7 marks)** **(b) The company has set a minimum threshold of 80% for an approved vendor. Discuss what corrective actions should be initiated for the non-preferred vendor under TQM principles. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Sum importance ratings (0.5 marks)** $$ \sum I_j = 8 + 5 + 4 + 3 = 20 $$ **Step 2: Compute normalized weights (2 marks)** $$ W_{Quality} = \frac{8}{20} = 0.40 $$ $$ W_{Delivery} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 $$ $$ W_{Price} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 $$ $$ W_{Service} = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15 $$ **Verification:** $0.40 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓ (0.5 marks) **Step 3: Compute weighted score for Vendor P (1.5 marks)** $$ S_P = (0.40 \times 75) + (0.25 \times 90) + (0.20 \times 80) + (0.15 \times 100) $$ $$ S_P = 30.00 + 22.50 + 16.00 + 15.00 = 83.50 $$ **Step 4: Compute weighted score for Vendor Q (1.5 marks)** $$ S_Q = (0.40 \times 95) + (0.25 \times 70) + (0.20 \times 80) + (0.15 \times 60) $$ $$ S_Q = 38.00 + 17.50 + 16.00 + 9.00 = 80.50 $$ **Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark)** | Rank | Vendor | Score | Status | |------|--------|-------|--------| | 1 | P | **83.50** | Preferred | | 2 | Q | **80.50** | Approved (just meets threshold) | **Conclusion:** Vendor **P** is preferred with a rating of **83.50%**. --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks Since Vendor Q (80.50%) is barely above the 80% threshold, the following **corrective actions under TQM** should be initiated: 1. **Supplier Development Program (2 marks):** Quality experts from the buying organization should visit Vendor Q's facility to provide technical guidance on improving their quality management system (e.g., SPC, 5S, Kaizen). 2. **Joint Quality Improvement Teams (2 marks):** Form cross-functional teams with Vendor Q to identify root causes of low quality scores using tools like **Pareto Analysis, Fishbone Diagram, and 5-Why Analysis**. 3. **Performance Review Meetings (1.5 marks):** Conduct quarterly review meetings to track Vendor Q's progress against improvement targets. Provide written feedback with specific numerical goals. 4. **Capacity Building & Training (1 mark):** Provide training to Vendor Q's workforce on quality tools and TQM philosophy. Share best practices from Vendor P (the preferred vendor). 5. **Conditional Status & Re-evaluation (0.5 marks):** Place Vendor Q on **conditional approved status** with a clear timeline (typically 3–6 months) for improvement. Failure to improve leads to disqualification. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** A 14-mark answer with 7 marks for part (b) requires **at least 4 distinct corrective actions** with brief explanations. Listing only generic statements like "improve quality" earns only 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Supplier Rating (KTU Module 2)** - ✅ **Definition:** Supplier Rating = systematic, quantitative evaluation of vendor performance using weighted criteria. - ✅ **Primary Method (KTU Favourite):** **Weighted Point Method**, expressed as $S_i = \sum W_j \times P_{ij}$. - ✅ **Weight Normalization Formula:** $W_j = \dfrac{I_j}{\sum I_j}$, with constraint $\sum W_j = 1$. - ✅ **Maximum Possible Score:** Always equals **100** (since $\sum W_j = 1$). - ✅ **Standard Criteria (4 Core):** Quality, Delivery, Price, Service. (Add Capacity, Technology for advanced TQM.) - ✅ **Industry-Benchmark Weight Distribution:** Quality (40–50%) > Delivery (25–30%) > Price (15–20%) > Service (5–10%). - ✅ **Approval Threshold:** $\geq 80\%$ = Approved Vendor. Below 80% = Conditional / Reject. - ✅ **Re-rating Frequency:** Every **6 to 12 months** (apply PDCA cycle). - ✅ **TQM Philosophy Link:** Deming's 1-10-100 Rule emphasizes prevention via supplier rating to avoid downstream failures. - ✅ **Three Methods of Rating:** Categorical (Subjective), Weighted Point (Quantitative — most used), and Cost Ratio (TCO-based). - ✅ **Sensitivity Analysis:** Always check whether the ranking changes when weights are altered (equal weights vs. company-defined weights) — a classic KTU 14-mark follow-up. - ✅ **Examiner's Pet Trick:** If two suppliers have identical weighted scores, the tie-breaker is **quality score** (since TQM prioritizes quality over cost/delivery). - ✅ **Connection to Other Module 2 Topics:** Supplier rating directly feeds into **Supplier Selection, Supplier Partnership (JIT supply), and Single vs. Multiple Sourcing decisions**. - ✅ **Real-World Connection:** Toyota, Boeing, and ISO 9001-certified firms globally use weighted supplier rating with Quality as the dominant weight. - ✅ **Mnemonic for Criteria:** **Q-D-P-S** → **Q**uality, **D**elivery, **P**rice, **S**ervice. - ✅ **Common Pitfall to Avoid:** Never skip the weight normalization step; always verify $\sum W_j = 1$ before computing scores.

topics.title: Statistical Quality Control - The Seven Tools of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Statistical Quality Control - The Seven Tools of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** is the application of statistical techniques to measure, monitor, analyze, and control the quality of a manufacturing or service process. It divides into three broad branches: **Acceptance Sampling**, **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**, and **Experimental Design**. Within SPC, **The Seven Tools of Quality** (originally popularized by **Kaoru Ishikawa** and refined under the Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers - **JUSE**) form the foundational diagnostic and analytical toolkit that empowers shop-floor engineers and quality professionals to solve **>90%** of quality-related problems using elementary statistical methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The Seven Tools of Quality are a set of graphical and statistical techniques useful for troubleshooting issues related to quality. They are called basic because they are simple to use, require no advanced mathematics, and can be applied by anyone at any level of the organization."*

The canonical **Seven Tools** are:

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa / Fishbone Diagram)
2. **Check Sheet**
3. **Control Chart** (Shewhart Chart)
4. **Histogram**
5. **Pareto Chart**
6. **Scatter Diagram**
7. **Flowchart** *(Stratification is widely accepted as the 7th in some texts)*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy - The Mechanic's Toolbox

Imagine a car mechanic standing in front of a faulty vehicle. To diagnose the engine, the mechanic does not need a particle accelerator; she needs a wrench, a multimeter, an OBD-II scanner, a flashlight, and a notepad. Each tool answers a different question:

- The **flashlight** lets her *see* the problem (Histogram, Scatter Diagram).
- The **multimeter** lets her *measure* electrical signals continuously (Control Chart).
- The **checklist** ensures she does not forget to inspect every part (Check Sheet).
- The **flowchart in the service manual** guides her step-by-step (Flowchart).
- The **root-cause tree** helps her brainstorm all possible causes (Cause-and-Effect).
- The **OBD-II scanner** prioritizes the most critical error codes (Pareto Chart).
- The **diagnostic grouping** (engine vs. transmission vs. brakes) helps her isolate where the fault lies (Stratification).

The Seven Tools of Quality work exactly like this mechanic's toolkit - simple, universally available, and capable of resolving the vast majority of process problems without resorting to advanced statistical techniques like **Design of Experiments (DoE)** or **Six Sigma Black-Belt analytics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (KTU Exam Favorite):** Kaoru Ishikawa formally introduced the "Seven Tools" framework in **1968** at a JUSE quality-control training program. They became a cornerstone of Japan's post-war industrial revolution and the **Toyota Production System**.

### 1.3 Why Only Seven? The Pareto Principle Within the Tools

Ishikawa deliberately limited the toolkit to seven because:

- **Cognitive Load:** A worker can memorize and master seven tools; mastering forty is impractical.
- **Coverage:** Together, the seven tools cover the entire **DMAIC** lifecycle (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) of problem-solving.
- **Accessibility:** They require only **basic arithmetic, geometry, and visual reasoning** - no calculus or linear algebra.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic - "CC-H-PS-F":** **C**heck Sheet, **C**ause-and-Effect, **H**istogram, **P**areto, **S**catter, **F**lowchart (Control Chart sits centrally because it is the most quantitative).

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tool-by-Tool Theoretical Breakdown

#### Tool 1: Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone)
- **Purpose:** Identify, explore, and display all possible causes of a specific problem (the "effect").
- **Structure:** A horizontal arrow pointing to the "effect" (problem statement), with branches resembling fishbones.
- **Standard Categories (6M):** **Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment)**. Service industries often use **4P**: *People, Policies, Procedures, Plant*.
- **When to Use:** Brainstorming sessions, root-cause analysis, cross-functional team meetings.

#### Tool 2: Check Sheet
- **Purpose:** Collect data in a structured, real-time, on-the-floor format so patterns become visible by mere inspection.
- **Design:** A simple tally-form customized to the problem (e.g., defect-type tally, location tally, time-of-day tally).
- **When to Use:** Data-collection stage at the start of any quality investigation.

#### Tool 3: Control Chart (Shewhart Chart)
- **Purpose:** Distinguish between **Common Cause Variation** (inherent to the process) and **Special Cause Variation** (due to external disturbances). This is the **only tool among the seven that performs hypothesis testing visually**.
- **Anatomy:**
  - **Centre Line (CL)** = process mean.
  - **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** = CL + 3σ.
  - **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** = CL - 3σ.
- **The 3-Sigma Logic:** Under a stable process, the probability of a point falling outside the control limits is **0.27%** (or 1 in 370), corresponding to the **±3σ rule** of the standard normal distribution.
- **Types:** Variable charts (X̄, R, S, EWMA, CUSUM) and Attribute charts (p, np, c, u).

#### Tool 4: Histogram
- **Purpose:** Display the **frequency distribution** of a continuous variable to reveal the **shape, centre, and spread** of process data.
- **Construction:** Divide the data range into equal-width **bins** (often **√n** bins by Sturges' Rule), plot bar heights proportional to frequencies.
- **Insight:** Reveals skewness, modality, outliers, and whether the process approximates a **Normal Distribution**.

#### Tool 5: Pareto Chart
- **Purpose:** Apply the **80/20 Principle** (Vilfredo Pareto, Italian economist, 1906) - typically **80%** of problems stem from **20%** of causes.
- **Structure:** A bar chart (causes on x-axis, frequency on y-axis) sorted in descending order, with a superimposed **cumulative percentage curve**.
- **When to Use:** Prioritization - identify the "vital few" causes to attack first.

#### Tool 6: Scatter Diagram
- **Purpose:** Examine the **relationship** (correlation) between two quantitative variables by plotting paired (x, y) data.
- **Interpretation:** Positive correlation, negative correlation, or no correlation; strength judged visually.

#### Tool 7: Flowchart
- **Purpose:** Document a process step-by-step using standardized symbols (oval = start/end, rectangle = operation, diamond = decision, parallelogram = input/output, arrow = flow).
- **When to Use:** Process mapping, training, identifying bottlenecks and rework loops.

#### Tool 7 (Alternate): Stratification
- **Purpose:** Separate data into distinct layers (by machine, operator, shift, material lot) before analysis to reveal hidden patterns.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The following table summarizes every formula you must memorize for the 14-mark questions. The constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$, $d_2$, $E_2$ depend on sample size $n$ and are derived from the **standard normal distribution**.

| Chart Type | Centre Line (CL) | Upper Control Limit (UCL) | Lower Control Limit (LCL) | Sample Statistic |
|------------|------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|------------------|
| $\bar{X}$ Chart (variables) | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Grand Mean of Subgroup Means |
| $R$ Chart (variables) | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | Mean of Subgroup Ranges |
| $X$ Chart (individuals) | $\bar{X}$ | $\bar{X} + E_2 \bar{MR}$ | $\bar{X} - E_2 \bar{MR}$ | Mean of Individuals (uses Moving Range) |
| $p$ Chart (fraction defective) | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | Average Fraction Defective |
| $np$ Chart (number defective) | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | Average Number Defective |
| $c$ Chart (defects per unit) | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | Average Number of Defects |
| $u$ Chart (defects per unit, varying n) | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | Average Defects per Unit |

#### Control Chart Constants Table (Critical for Exam)

| Sample Size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ | $E_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 | 2.660 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 | 1.772 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 | 1.457 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.115 | 2.326 | 1.290 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 | 1.184 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 | 1.109 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 | 1.054 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 2.970 | 1.010 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 3.078 | 0.975 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Estimating Process Standard Deviation:**
> $$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \quad \text{or} \quad \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{MR}}{d_2}$$
> where $\bar{MR}$ is the average moving range (used when $n=1$).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Seven Tools |
|----------|---------------------------|
| **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)** | Pareto charts in warranty-claim analysis; Control charts on torque-wrench outputs. |
| **Pharmaceutical (Sun Pharma, Cipla)** | Control charts for tablet weight, hardness; Cause-and-Effect for OOS (Out-of-Specification) investigations. |
| **Semiconductor (Intel, TSMC)** | Histograms and scatter diagrams for wafer-defect mapping. |
| **Hospitality (TQM Service)** | Flowcharts of patient admission; Check sheets for housekeeping defects. |
| **Software (CMMI Level 5 firms)** | Pareto charts on bug-severity; Fishbone for root-cause of post-release defects. |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 3-Sigma Control Limits

**Goal:** Show mathematically why the control limits of a Shewhart $\bar{X}$ chart are placed at $\pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ from the centre line.

**Step 1 - Define the Sampling Distribution:**
When we draw samples of size $n$ from a process with population mean $\mu$ and population standard deviation $\sigma$, the distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ follows the **Central Limit Theorem**:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

The standard deviation of the sample-mean distribution is the **standard error of the mean**:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 2 - Convert to the Z-Score (Standard Normal Variable):**

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3 - Apply the Empirical (68-95-99.7) Rule:**
For any normal distribution, the probability of a value falling beyond $\pm 3\sigma$ is:

$$P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 2 \times [1 - \Phi(3)]$$

Where $\Phi(3) \approx 0.99865$ (standard normal CDF).

$$P(\vert Z \vert > 3) = 2 \times (1 - 0.99865) = 0.0027$$

This is the famous **0.27% false-alarm rate** - equivalent to **1 false alarm per 370 samples**.

**Step 4 - Translate Back to the $\bar{X}$ Chart:**

For a point on the chart to lie outside the control limits, we require:

$$\vert \bar{X} - \mu \vert > 3 \cdot \sigma_{\bar{X}}$$

Hence the control limits are:

$$\text{UCL} = \mu + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{LCL} = \mu - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 5 - Replace Unknown Population Parameters with Sample Estimates:**

Since the true $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are unknown, we estimate them using the sample grand mean and average range:

$$\mu \approx \bar{\bar{X}} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma \approx \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

Substituting and noting that $A_2 = \frac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** A KTU-certified bearings manufacturer collects 5 samples of size $n = 4$ from its CNC grinding line. The diameters (in mm) of the bearings are tabulated below. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts and comment on process control. ($\alpha = 0.0027$, hence 3-sigma limits.)

| Sample No. | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_3$ | $x_4$ | Mean $\bar{X}_i$ | Range $R_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.20 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.3 | 10.225 | 0.2 |
| 3 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.10 | 0.2 |
| 4 | 10.4 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.5 | 10.35 | 0.3 |
| 5 | 10.2 | 10.3 | 10.1 | 10.2 | 10.20 | 0.2 |

#### **Step 1 - Calculate the Grand Mean and Average Range:**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X}_i}{k} = \frac{10.20 + 10.225 + 10.10 + 10.35 + 10.20}{5}$$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{51.075}{5} = 10.215 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{0.2 + 0.2 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.2}{5}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1.1}{5} = 0.22 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Step 2 - Select Constants for $n = 4$:**

From the constants table: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$.

#### **Step 3 - Compute Control Limits for the $\bar{X}$ Chart:**

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 10.215 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 10.215 + (0.729 \times 0.22)$$

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 10.215 + 0.16038 = 10.375 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 10.215 - 0.16038 = 10.054 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Step 4 - Compute Control Limits for the $R$ Chart:**

$$\text{CL}_R = \bar{R} = 0.22 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.282 \times 0.22 = 0.502 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \times 0.22 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

#### **Step 5 - Check for Out-of-Control Points:**

| Sample | $\bar{X}_i$ | Status ($\bar{X}$ Chart) | $R_i$ | Status ($R$ Chart) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 10.20 | In-control (10.054 $\le$ 10.20 $\le$ 10.375) | 0.2 | In-control |
| 2 | 10.225 | In-control | 0.2 | In-control |
| 3 | 10.10 | In-control | 0.2 | In-control |
| 4 | 10.35 | In-control (just below UCL) | 0.3 | In-control |
| 5 | 10.20 | In-control | 0.2 | In-control |

**Conclusion:** All sample means and ranges lie within their respective control limits. The process is **statistically in-control**. The natural tolerance of the process is $\bar{\bar{X}} \pm 3\hat{\sigma} = 10.215 \pm 3 \times (\bar{R}/d_2) = 10.215 \pm 3 \times (0.22/2.059) = 10.215 \pm 0.320$ mm, i.e., **(9.895, 10.535) mm**. Since the specification limits (not given here) likely fall inside this band, the process capability should be assessed using $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ indices.

### 3.3 Pareto Chart Cumulative Calculation (Mini-Derivation)

Given defect data sorted by descending frequency:

| Cause | Frequency | Relative % | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scratches | 42 | 42% | 42% |
| Dents | 28 | 28% | 70% |
| Discoloration | 15 | 15% | 85% |
| Misalignment | 8 | 8% | 93% |
| Others | 7 | 7% | 100% |
| **Total** | **100** |  |  |

**Interpretation:** The "vital few" are **Scratches + Dents = 70%** of all defects. The team should focus quality-engineering effort on these two causes first, per the **80/20 principle**.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tool-Selection Decision Tree (Use this to pick the right tool)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Problem Identified] --> B{What stage are you in?}
    B -- Data Collection --> C[Use Check Sheet]
    B -- Brainstorming Causes --> D[Use Cause and Effect Diagram]
    B -- Process Documentation --> E[Use Flowchart]
    B -- Data Already Collected --> F{What do you want to know?}
    F -- Distribution Shape --> G[Use Histogram]
    F -- Relationship between Two Variables --> H[Use Scatter Diagram]
    F -- Priority of Causes --> I[Use Pareto Chart]
    F -- Process Stability Over Time --> J[Use Control Chart]
    J --> K{Variable or Attribute Data?}
    K -- Variable --> L[Xbar and R or Xbar and S Chart]
    K -- Attribute --> M[p or np or c or u Chart]
    F -- Hidden Subgroup Patterns --> N[Use Stratification]
    C --> O[Analyze and Decide]
    D --> O
    E --> O
    G --> O
    H --> O
    I --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Ishikawa Fishbone Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EFX[Effect: Excessive Bearing Defects] --> SPINE
    SPINE{{Main Spine}} --> CAT1
    SPINE --> CAT2
    SPINE --> CAT3
    SPINE --> CAT4
    SPINE --> CAT5
    SPINE --> CAT6
    CAT1[MAN: Operator Skill, Training, Fatigue] --> C1A[Untrained Operator]
    CAT1 --> C1B[Shift Changeover]
    CAT2[MACHINE: CNC Drift, Tool Wear] --> C2A[Spindle Vibration]
    CAT2 --> C2B[Dull Cutting Tool]
    CAT3[MATERIAL: Batch Variation, Storage] --> C3A[Inconsistent Raw Stock]
    CAT3 --> C3B[Improper Storage Humidity]
    CAT4[METHOD: Process Parameters, SOPs] --> C4A[Incorrect Feed Rate]
    CAT4 --> C4B[Missing Standard Procedure]
    CAT5[MEASUREMENT: Gauge Calibration] --> C5A[Uncalibrated Micrometer]
    CAT5 --> C5B[Measurement Bias]
    CAT6[ENVIRONMENT: Temperature, Dust] --> C6A[Workshop Temperature Variation]
    CAT6 --> C6B[Airborne Contaminants]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONTROL_CHART[Shewhart X-bar Control Chart]

    end

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A3 --> A4[Sort Descending]
        A4 --> A5[Calculate Cumulative Percentage]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*List any three tools of quality and state one application of each.*

**Model Answer:**
The three tools of quality are:
1. **Histogram:** Used to visualize the frequency distribution of a continuous variable; for example, plotting the distribution of bolt-diameter measurements to check whether the process is centred and within specification.
2. **Control Chart:** Used to monitor a process over time and distinguish between common-cause and special-cause variation; for example, plotting the mean diameter of piston rings sampled hourly.
3. **Pareto Chart:** Used to prioritize problems based on the 80/20 principle; for example, identifying that 80% of customer complaints arise from just 20% of product features.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for listing + 2 marks for applications)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between common-cause variation and special-cause variation with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Common-Cause Variation | Special-Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Inherent to the process design | External to the process |
| Action | Improve the system (management's job) | Remove the assignable cause (operator's job) |
| Frequency | Continuous, predictable | Sporadic, unpredictable |
| Example | Slight dimensional variation due to ambient temperature | A broken cutting tool causing sudden oversize parts |

**(3 marks: 2 marks for differentiation + 1 mark for examples)**

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and interpretation of a Pareto Chart. Illustrate with an example showing how the 80/20 principle helps prioritize quality problems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bottling plant fills 500 ml soft-drink bottles. Twenty-five samples of size $n = 5$ are taken and the subgroup means and ranges are tabulated below. Construct the $\bar{X}$ and $R$ charts using 3-sigma limits and comment on the process state.

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = 500.4 \text{ ml}, \quad \bar{R} = 1.6 \text{ ml}$$

(Use $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.115$ for $n = 5$.) **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pareto Chart Explanation (7 marks):**

The Pareto Chart is a bar graph with categories on the x-axis (sorted by descending frequency) and frequency on the y-axis, overlaid with a cumulative-percentage curve. It is built using the **80/20 principle** articulated by Vilfredo Pareto.

**Construction Steps:**
1. Collect defect data using a **Check Sheet** (Tool 2).
2. Categorize defects by cause type (e.g., scratches, dents, cracks, discoloration).
3. Count frequencies per category.
4. Sort categories in **descending order** of frequency.
5. Calculate the cumulative percentage for each category.
6. Plot bars for frequencies (left y-axis) and a line for cumulative percentage (right y-axis, 0-100% scale).
7. Identify the "**vital few**" - those categories up to the 80% cumulative line - and the "**trivial many**".

**Interpretation Example:**
Suppose 100 defects are recorded: Scratches (42), Dents (28), Discoloration (15), Misalignment (8), Others (7). Cumulative % reaches 70% at "Scratches + Dents" and 85% at "Scratches + Dents + Discoloration". The vital few are the first two causes, contributing 70% of the total defects. The quality-engineering team should focus on eliminating scratches and dents first for maximum impact.

**(b) Control Chart Construction (7 marks):**

For $n = 5$: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.115$.

**$\bar{X}$ Chart Limits:**

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 500.4 \text{ ml}$$

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 500.4 + (0.577 \times 1.6) = 500.4 + 0.9232 = 501.323 \text{ ml}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 500.4 - 0.9232 = 499.477 \text{ ml}$$

**$R$ Chart Limits:**

$$\text{CL}_R = \bar{R} = 1.6 \text{ ml}$$

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.115 \times 1.6 = 3.384 \text{ ml}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0 \text{ ml}$$

**Comment:** Provided all 25 subgroup means lie within (499.477, 501.323) ml and all 25 ranges lie within (0, 3.384) ml, the process is **statistically in-control** with mean 500.4 ml and natural variation of approximately $\pm 3 \hat{\sigma} = \pm 3 \times (\bar{R}/d_2) = \pm 3 \times (1.6/2.326) = \pm 2.06$ ml. The process centre is 0.4 ml above the nominal 500 ml target, indicating a **systematic bias** that may require adjustment of the filling-machine setpoint.

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Retest Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) Diagram**. List the standard 6M categories used in manufacturing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A garment manufacturing unit tracks the number of stitching defects per shirt. Data from 15 shirts are: 4, 6, 3, 5, 7, 2, 4, 5, 3, 6, 4, 5, 3, 7, 4. Construct a **c-chart** using 3-sigma limits and determine whether the process is in statistical control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ishikawa Diagram (7 marks):**

The Cause-and-Effect Diagram (also called Fishbone Diagram due to its resemblance to a fish skeleton) was developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968. It is a structured brainstorming tool that displays all potential causes of a problem (the "effect") categorized into standard branches.

**Construction Steps:**
1. State the problem (effect) clearly on the right side inside a box, with a horizontal arrow (the "spine") pointing toward it.
2. Draw diagonal "rib" lines representing major cause categories.
3. Label each rib with one of the **6M categories**:
   - **Man (Workforce):** Skill, training, morale, fatigue.
