From the standard normal CDF table, $\Phi(6.0) \approx 0.999999999$, so the one-sided tail is essentially **1 part per billion**.

**Step 3 — Account for the 1.5σ Drift (Long-Term):**

Motorola's empirical observation: real processes drift. After time, the mean shifts by up to **1.5σ**.

$$\text{Effective Z (long-term)} = 6.0 - 1.5 = 4.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute Long-Term Tail Probability:**

$$P(Z > 4.5) = 1 - \Phi(4.5) \approx 3.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to DPMO (Two-Sided Specs):**

$$DPMO = 2 \times 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6} = 6.8 \approx 3.4 \text{ DPMO}$$

> The **3.4 DPMO** value (technically 6.8 for perfectly symmetric specs, but 3.4 when only one side drift is applied per Motorola's convention) is the **defining metric of Six Sigma quality**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply Which Tool

| DMAIC Phase | Statistical Tool | Engineering Tool | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define | VOC matrix, CTQ tree | SIPOC, Project Charter | Problem statement |
| Measure | MSA, Gauge R&R | Process mapping, Gemba walk | Baseline sigma |
| Analyze | Hypothesis testing, regression | Fishbone, 5-Why, FMEA | Root causes |
| Improve | DOE, ANOVA | Poka-Yoke, Pugh matrix | Optimized process |
| Control | SPC, Control charts | SOPs, Visual management | Sustained gains |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Six Sigma. Briefly explain the concept of 1.5σ shift.**

**Model Answer:**

Six Sigma is a data-driven quality management methodology that aims to reduce process defects to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It uses statistical tools (DMAIC) and disciplined project management to achieve near-perfect process capability.

The **1.5σ shift** is Motorola's empirical correction factor. It accounts for the real-world observation that **process means drift over time** due to tool wear, environmental changes, supplier variability, and operator differences. In the short term, a process operates at its plotted sigma level; in the long term, the mean may shift by up to **1.5σ** toward the nearest specification limit. Hence, a true 6σ short-term capability (Z = 6.0 from spec) corresponds to a long-term Z of 4.5, which statistically yields **3.4 DPMO**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between DMAIC and DMADV.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DMAIC | DMADV (DFSS) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Improve existing process | Design new process/product |
| Full form | Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control | Define, Measure, Analyze, Design, Verify |
| Phase emphasis | Improvement and Control | Design and Verification |
| When used | Defect rates too high; process exists | New product launch; process does not exist |
| Tools | FMEA, SPC, DOE | QFD, Axiomatic Design, Robust Design |
| Outcome | Sustained reduction in defects | Six Sigma capable design from day one |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Students are offered an **internal choice** between two full 14-mark questions from the same module. **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives.

---

### QUESTION A — Case-Study Driven `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(14 Marks)**

A large automotive parts manufacturer produces **gearboxes**. Each gearbox has **8 CTQ features**. In a recent audit over **one month (25 working days)**, the company produced **10,000 gearboxes** and identified **800 defects** in total.

**(a)** (7 Marks) Compute the **DPMO**, the **process yield**, and determine the **approximate sigma level** of the process.

**(b)** (7 Marks) Suggest a **DMAIC roadmap** that the company should follow. For each phase, list **two tools** that would be most relevant for this scenario.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

**(a) DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- $U = 10{,}000$ gearboxes
- $O = 8$ opportunities per unit
- $D = 800$ defects

**Step 2:** Apply DPMO formula:

$$DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{800 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 8}$$

$$DPMO = \frac{800{,}000{,}000}{80{,}000} = 10{,}000 \text{ DPMO}$$

**Step 3:** Compute Process Yield:

$$\text{Yield} = \left(1 - \frac{10{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) \times 100 = 99.0\%$$

**Step 4:** Sigma Level Lookup:

10,000 DPMO corresponds to approximately **3.8σ (long-term)**. The process is currently between **3σ and 4σ**, far from the Six Sigma goal.

$$\boxed{DPMO = 10{,}000; \quad \text{Yield} = 99.0\%; \quad \sigma \approx 3.8}$$

---

**(b) DMAIC Roadmap for Gearbox Manufacturer:**

**DEFINE Phase:**
- Tool 1: **VOC/CTQ Tree** — capture what "quality" means to automotive OEM customers (e.g., gear-meshing noise ≤ 55 dB, surface roughness ≤ 0.8 μm).
- Tool 2: **SIPOC Diagram** — map suppliers (steel vendors) → inputs (raw blanks) → process (machining, hobbing, grinding, heat treatment) → outputs (finished gearboxes) → customers (OEM assembly line).

**MEASURE Phase:**
- Tool 1: **Gage R&R (Gauge Repeatability & Reproducibility)** — confirm that the Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) is measuring reliably.
- Tool 2: **Pareto Chart on Defect Types** — identify the "vital few" defects (e.g., 70% of defects might be tooth-profile errors).

**ANALYZE Phase:**
- Tool 1: **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** with 6 Ms (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature) to brainstorm root causes.
- Tool 2: **Hypothesis Testing (2-sample t-test)** to compare defect rates between two shifts or two machine suppliers.

**IMPROVE Phase:**
- Tool 1: **Design of Experiments (2k Factorial)** to optimize CNC cutting speed, feed rate, and depth of cut.
- Tool 2: **Poka-Yoke (Error-Proofing)** such as automated torque-limit fixtures on the assembly line.

**CONTROL Phase:**
- Tool 1: **X-bar and R Control Charts** on critical dimensions (bore diameter, tooth thickness) to detect out-of-control conditions in real time.
- Tool 2: **Updated SOPs and Visual Workplaces** with poka-yoke counters and red-bin systems for defect segregation.

---

### QUESTION B — Conceptual + Numerical `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** (7 Marks) Explain in detail the **roles and responsibilities** of the various **Six Sigma belt holders**. Discuss why Six Sigma is considered a **financially-driven methodology**.

**(b)** (7 Marks) A chemical plant fills detergent bottles. The **target fill** is **1000 ml**, with **USL = 1010 ml** and **LSL = 990 ml**. A sample of 50 bottles gives $\bar{x} = 1003$ ml and $s = 2.5$ ml. Compute **$C_p$** and **$C_{pk}$**, and comment on whether the process is capable.

---

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

**(a) Belt Holders and Financial Drive:**

**Belt Hierarchy (in descending order of expertise):**

1. **Executive Leaders** — Set the strategic vision, allocate capital budgets for Six Sigma projects, and tie rewards to project outcomes. They guarantee organisational alignment between quality and business goals.

2. **Champions** — Senior managers who select projects, approve project charters, and remove organisational barriers. They act as the bridge between the executive suite and the technical belts.

3. **Master Black Belts (MBB)** — The most technically proficient statisticians. They mentor Black Belts, ensure statistical correctness, and lead organisation-wide deployments of Six Sigma.

4. **Black Belts (BB)** — Full-time project leaders, typically with 4–5 years of experience. They execute DMAIC end-to-end, train Green Belts, and are expected to deliver **$100,000–$1,000,000 in savings per project**.

5. **Green Belts (GB)** — Part-time project leaders who run smaller DMAIC projects under Black Belt mentorship while continuing their normal duties.

6. **Yellow/White Belts** — Team members with introductory Six Sigma awareness who support data collection and execution.

**Why Six Sigma is Financially Driven:**

- Every project begins with a **financial baseline (COPQ)** and ends with **hard-dollar savings**.
- GE reported **$12 billion** in cumulative Six Sigma savings between 1995 and 2005.
- Projects are prioritized by **NPV, ROI, and payback period**, not just statistical elegance.
- Every DMAIC phase has a **financial gate review**, ensuring accountability at each step.

---

**(b) Process Capability Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Given values: $USL = 1010$, $LSL = 990$, $\mu = 1003$, $\sigma = 2.5$.

**Step 2:** Compute $C_p$:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{1010 - 990}{6 \times 2.5} = \frac{20}{15} = 1.333$$

**Step 3:** Compute $C_{pk}$ terms:

$$\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{1010 - 1003}{3 \times 2.5} = \frac{7}{7.5} = 0.933$$

$$\frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{1003 - 990}{3 \times 2.5} = \frac{13}{7.5} = 1.733$$

**Step 4:** Take the minimum:

$$C_{pk} = \min\{0.933, 1.733\} = 0.933$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:

> $C_p = 1.333$ indicates the process **spread** is acceptable. However, $C_{pk} = 0.933$ is **below 1.00**, meaning the process is **not capable** because the **mean (1003 ml)** is shifted **toward the upper specification limit (1010 ml)**. There is a real risk of overfilling. Recommended action: **re-center the filling machine** so that $\mu$ shifts back toward 1000 ml, after which $C_{pk}$ will improve dramatically.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $C_{pk} = C_p$. Always remember $C_{pk} \leq C_p$ in real life; equality holds only when the process is perfectly centred. Many students lose 1–2 marks by ignoring the centring aspect.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to divide by **$3\sigma$** (not $6\sigma$) in the $C_{pk}$ numerator. The full specification width is **6σ** for $C_p$, but only **one-sided** for $C_{pk}$.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Not commenting on **process centring direction**. The examiner expects students to say whether the mean is drifting toward USL or LSL.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Defining DPMO incorrectly. It is **defects per million OPPORTUNITIES**, not per million units or per million dollars.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Six Sigma Essentials**

- **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.9997% yield = 4.5σ long-term from spec (with 1.5σ drift).**
- **Origin:** Motorola (1986, Bill Smith); popularized by GE (1995, Jack Welch).
- **DMAIC:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control (improves existing processes).
- **DMADV / DFSS:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Design → Verify (creates new processes).
- **DPMO Formula:** $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ — *memorize this cold.*
- **Process Yield:** $\left(1 - \dfrac{DPMO}{10^{6}}\right) \times 100$.
- **$C_p$** measures spread; **$C_{pk}$** measures spread **and** centring.
- **Hierarchy (top-down):** Executive → Champion → Master Black Belt → Black Belt → Green Belt → Yellow/White Belt.
- **Six Sigma requires statistical rigour + financial accountability + customer focus.**
- **Real-world average:** Most companies operate at **3σ–4σ**. Reaching 6σ is a 3–5 year journey.
- **Tools to recall:** Fishbone, Pareto, Control Charts, FMEA, DOE, SIPOC, Gauge R&R, Poka-Yoke.
- **DMAIC = existing process improvement; DMADV = new process design.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Seven Management Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** New Seven Management Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **New Seven Management Tools (N7 Tools / M7 Tools)** are a set of qualitative, planning-oriented graphical techniques formally identified and codified by the **Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)** in **1976**. They are intended to complement the traditional Seven QC Tools and are predominantly applied during the **planning phase of the PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act) cycle** rather than purely during statistical problem-solving.

The seven tools are:
1. **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)**
2. **Interrelationship Diagram (Relations Diagram)**
3. **Tree Diagram (Systematic Diagram)**
4. **Matrix Diagram**
5. **Prioritization Matrix (Matrix Data Analysis)**
6. **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)**
7. **Arrow Diagram (Activity Network / PERT–CPM)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** The original 7 QC Tools are **statistical/numerical** and are used mostly during the **'Check' (analysis)** phase. The New 7 Management Tools are **qualitative/planning** tools and are used mostly during the **'Plan'** phase. This contrast is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a **wedding**:
- The **original 7 QC Tools** are like the *guest list and budget spreadsheet* — they **measure and verify** that the wedding stays on track (numerical control).
- The **New Seven Management Tools** are like the *mind-maps, timelines, and contingency plans* you draw on the **whiteboard** before the wedding — they help you **organize ideas, prioritize tasks, and anticipate problems** before they occur (planning and thinking tools).

In simple words: **QC Tools = "Solve problems that already exist."** **N7 Tools = "Plan properly so problems don't occur."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

While the N7 tools are mostly qualitative, certain standardized parameters are commonly used in practice:

- **Affinity Diagram card limit:** Typically **40–60 ideas** per session.
- **Tree Diagram hierarchy depth:** Usually **3–5 levels** for executive review.
- **PDPC branching factor:** Contingency branches limited to **3–5 alternatives** per node.
- **Arrow Diagram network:** Uses **CPM Critical Path** (deterministic) and **PERT expected time** $T_E = (O + 4M + P)/6$, where **O = Optimistic, M = Most likely, P = Pessimistic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in KTU exams:** A direct 14-mark question on "Explain the New Seven Management Tools" or "Differentiate between the 7 QC Tools and the New 7 Tools" appears almost every semester in Module 3. Mastery of the **purpose + a simple sketch** of each tool is mandatory.

---

## 1.4 Comparative Snapshot — Old vs New Tools

| Aspect | Original 7 QC Tools | New 7 Management Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Identified by Kaoru Ishikawa (1968) | Identified by JUSE (1976) |
| **Nature** | Quantitative / Statistical | Qualitative / Planning |
| **Phase of PDCA** | Mainly **Check** (Analysis) | Mainly **Plan** |
| **Users** | Shop-floor / QC engineers | Middle & Senior management |
| **Output type** | Numerical data, charts | Words, relationships, diagrams |
| **Primary goal** | Detect & solve existing problems | Prevent problems; organize thinking |
| **Example tools** | Pareto, Fishbone, Histogram | Affinity, Tree, Arrow diagram |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tool-by-Tool Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)

- **Developer:** **Kawakita Jiro (1964)** — hence the alternate name *KJ Method*.
- **Purpose:** Organize a large volume of **unstructured, language-based data** (ideas, opinions, complaints) into natural, meaningful groupings.
- **When to use:** When the problem is vague, the team has too many ideas to handle, or stakeholder input is divergent.
- **Step-by-step logic:**
  1. **State the issue / problem** clearly.
  2. **Brainstorm** silently — every member writes ideas on **sticky notes / cards** (one idea per card).
  3. **Spread the cards** on a wall randomly.
  4. **Silently cluster** related cards into natural groups.
  5. **Label each group** with a header card (the affinity).
  6. **Discuss** the groupings with the team and refine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of it as *throwing all your clothes onto the bed and then sorting them into "shirts", "pants", "underwear" piles* — but for ideas.

- **Engineering utility:** Used in **QFD (Quality Function Deployment)** to convert the *Voice of Customer* (VOC) into affinity groups before constructing the House of Quality.

---

### 2.1.2 Interrelationship Diagram (Relations Diagram)

- **Purpose:** Untangle **complex, intertwined cause-and-effect relationships** among many factors when a problem has multiple interacting causes.
- **Visual form:** Boxes (factors) connected by **arrows** showing direction of influence.
- **Three structural variants:**
  1. **One-Way (L-Type)** — arrows flow in a single direction.
  2. **Central (Y-Type)** — one key driver in the center.
  3. **Both-Way (C-Type)** — mutually influencing factors.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. List all factors affecting the problem.
2. Place each factor in a box.
3. Draw arrows: **"Factor A influences Factor B"**.
4. Count arrows in and out for each factor.
5. The factor with the **most outgoing arrows** = the **root cause / driver**.
6. The factor with the **most incoming arrows** = the **key result / effect**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Classic:** Draw an interrelationship diagram for a quality problem (e.g., *defects in welding*) and identify the root cause — this is a guaranteed question pattern.

---

### 2.1.3 Tree Diagram (Systematic Diagram)

- **Purpose:** Hierarchically break down a **broad goal** into **increasingly detailed sub-goals / tasks / means**, until actionable items are reached.
- **Structure:** A **single root** at the top, branches splitting downward — like an inverted tree.
- **Use cases:**
  - Convert customer needs (from QFD) into engineering specifications.
  - Break a company mission into department-level objectives.
  - Convert an affinity group header into a work breakdown structure (WBS).

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. State the **objective / goal** at the top.
2. Ask: *What are the main means to achieve it?* — generate **Level 1** branches.
3. For each Level 1, ask: *What are the sub-means?* — generate **Level 2**.
4. Continue until **actionable tasks** appear at the leaf nodes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** It is exactly the **"Means–Ends" tree** — top = Ends (Goal), bottom = Means (Actions). Often paired with an **Affinity Diagram above** (provides the headers) and a **Matrix Diagram below** (relates the leaves).

---

### 2.1.4 Matrix Diagram

- **Purpose:** Identify and visualize the **strength of relationships** between **two or more sets of items** (e.g., customer requirements vs. engineering specs, departments vs. functions).
- **Matrix cells** are filled with symbols denoting relationship strength:
  - ⊙ **Strong**
  - ○ **Moderate**
  - △ **Weak**

**Six structural variants:**

| Type | Shape | Number of Groups | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L-Shaped** | L | 2 groups | A vs. B (most common) |
| **T-Shaped** | T | 3 groups (A vs. B, A vs. C) | Customer req. vs. Specs vs. Tasks |
| **Y-Shaped** | Y | 3 groups in a cycle | 3 elements mutually related |
| **X-Shaped** | X | 4 groups, all-vs-all | Complex 4-factor analysis |
| **C-Shaped** | 3D | 3 groups | 3-factor intersection |
| **Roof-Shaped** | Triangle | 1 group vs. itself | Correlation within one set |

- **Engineering utility:** Backbone of **QFD's Roof Matrix** (which correlates engineering specs against each other).

---

### 2.1.5 Prioritization Matrix (Matrix Data Analysis)

- **Purpose:** Apply **numerical weighting** to a **Matrix Diagram** to **rank alternatives** objectively when multiple criteria exist.
- **Two common forms:**
  1. **Weighted-Score Matrix** — qualitative scoring of alternatives against weighted criteria.
  2. **Matrix Data Analysis Chart** — uses **statistical tools (regression, AHP)** inside the matrix cells.

**Step-by-step logic (Weighted-Score form):**
1. List alternatives (e.g., Supplier A, B, C).
2. List criteria (e.g., Cost, Quality, Delivery).
3. Assign **weight** to each criterion: $\sum w_i = 1$ (or 100%).
4. Score each alternative against each criterion (e.g., 1–10).
5. Compute **Weighted Total Score** = $\sum w_i \cdot s_{ij}$.
6. Rank alternatives by descending total.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) connection:** Saaty's AHP is the most rigorous extension — uses pairwise comparison and **eigenvector weighting** instead of arbitrary weights.

---

### 2.1.6 Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

- **Purpose:** **Anticipate and plan contingencies** during implementation of a plan. Maps out **what could go wrong** at each step and the **counter-measures**.
- **Visual form:** Starts as a **Tree Diagram** of the plan; at each node, **"if-then" contingency branches** are added.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Draw the planned sequence of events (like a tree).
2. At each step, brainstorm: *"What could go wrong here?"*
3. Add a **contingency branch** with a counter-measure.
4. Continue iteratively as new risks emerge.
5. After execution, **mark** which contingencies actually occurred — feed back to refine.

- **Engineering utility:** Used heavily in **new product launch**, **FMEA**, **disaster response planning**.

---

### 2.1.7 Arrow Diagram (Activity Network / PERT–CPM)

- **Purpose:** Plan, schedule, and control **complex projects** with many interdependent activities. Identify the **Critical Path** and **bottlenecks**.
- **Components:**
  - **Nodes (circles):** Events / milestones.
  - **Arrows:** Activities (with duration).
  - **Dummy arrows:** Logical dependencies without duration.

**Two main flavors:**
- **CPM (Critical Path Method):** Deterministic single-time estimates.
- **PERT (Program Evaluation & Review Technique):** Three-time estimates using $\beta$-distribution.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are fair game for KTU Part A (2-mark) and Part B (14-mark) numerical questions on the Arrow Diagram tool.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PERT Expected Time** | $T_E = \dfrac{O + 4M + P}{6}$ | O, M, P in days/weeks |
| 2 | **PERT Variance** | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{P - O}{6}\right)^2$ | Used for probability of completion |
| 3 | **PERT Std. Deviation** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P - O}{6}$ | Days |
| 4 | **Slack / Float** | $\text{Slack} = LS - ES = LF - EF$ | Higher slack = not on critical path |
| 5 | **Crash Cost Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | ₹ per day |
| 6 | **Weighted Score** | $S_i = \sum_{j} w_j \cdot s_{ij}$ | Where $\sum w_j = 1$ |
| 7 | **Project Completion Probability** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{CP}}$ | Standard normal Z-value |
| 8 | **AHP Pairwise Scale** | Saaty 1–9 scale | 1 = equal, 9 = extreme |
| 9 | **Critical Path Length** | Sum of $T_E$ of activities on CP | Equals minimum project duration |

**Example KTU-type numerical:** A project has 5 activities A, B, C, D, E with O, M, P estimates. Compute $T_E$, $\sigma^2$, identify critical path, find probability of finishing in 30 days if $T_s = 30$ days.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Most-used N7 Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive (Toyota, Tata)** | Affinity + Tree + Arrow | QFD-based product development |
| **Software (ISO 9001 firms)** | PDPC + Prioritization | Risk planning in agile sprints |
| **Construction (L&T, NCC)** | Arrow Diagram (PERT/CPM) | Scheduling multi-storey projects |
| **Pharma (Cipla, Sun)** | Matrix + Interrelationship | Cross-functional CAPA tracking |
| **Manufacturing SMEs** | Affinity Diagram | Sorting customer complaints |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: PERT Expected Time Formula

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Basis

PERT assumes activity durations follow a **Beta distribution** with three input parameters:
- **O** = Optimistic time (best case)
- **M** = Most likely time (modal value)
- **P** = Pessimistic time (worst case)

The mean and variance of a Beta distribution cannot be written in closed form using only O, M, P, so **Dr. D.G. Malcolm and the PERT team (1958)** approximated it using a **weighted average** of the three estimates.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of $T_E$

We approximate the Beta mean by a linear combination:

$$
T_E = \alpha \cdot O + \beta \cdot M + \gamma \cdot P
$$

Where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (weights sum to 1).

Empirically, PERT assigns **heavier weight to M** (most likely) because in real projects the modal time is the strongest predictor. The historically accepted weights are:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{1}{6}, \quad \beta = \dfrac{4}{6}, \quad \gamma = \dfrac{1}{6}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_E &= \frac{1}{6} \cdot O + \frac{4}{6} \cdot M + \frac{1}{6} \cdot P \\
&= \frac{1 \cdot O + 4 \cdot M + 1 \cdot P}{6} \\
T_E &= \frac{O + 4M + P}{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** Memorize as **"O + 4M + P, all divided by 6"** — the **4-times weight on M** is the heart of PERT and the most-frequently tested sub-part.

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of PERT Variance $\sigma^2$

For a Beta distribution, the variance approximation is:

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2
$$

**Reasoning (range-based estimator):**
- The total range is $(P - O)$.
- In a normal distribution, ~99.7% of values lie within $\pm 3\sigma$ — i.e., a total spread of $6\sigma$.
- For a Beta distribution skewed by M, the **6-sigma rule is also empirically used** (PERT approximation).

Therefore:

$$
6\sigma \approx (P - O) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = \frac{P - O}{6}
$$

Squaring both sides:

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2
$$

### 3.1.4 Project Completion Probability Derivation

For a scheduled target time $T_s$:

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{critical path}}}
$$

Where $\sigma_{\text{CP}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma^2_{i \in \text{CP}}}$.

The probability of finishing in $T_s$ days = $\Phi(Z)$ from the **Standard Normal Table**.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Numerical:** This exact pattern appears frequently in the 14-mark Part B question on Arrow Diagrams.

**Problem:** A small project has 4 activities with the following estimates (in days):

| Activity | Predecessor | O | M | P |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| B | A | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| C | A | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| D | B, C | 4 | 6 | 14 |

**Find:** (a) Expected time & variance of each activity. (b) Critical path & project duration. (c) Probability of completing in **18 days**.

### 3.2.1 Part (a) — Expected Time & Variance

**Activity A:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EA} &= \frac{O + 4M + P}{6} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_A^2 &= \left(\frac{P - O}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{36} = 0.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activity B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EB} &= \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_B^2 &= \left(\frac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activity C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{EC} &= \frac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_C^2 &= \left(\frac{5 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activity D:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{ED} &= \frac{4 + 4(6) + 14}{6} = \frac{42}{6} = 7 \text{ days} \\
\sigma_D^2 &= \left(\frac{14 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{100}{36} = 2.778
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Activity | $T_E$ (days) | $\sigma^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 4 | 0.444 |
| B | 5 | 0.444 |
| C | 3 | 0.444 |
| D | 7 | 2.778 |

### 3.2.2 Part (b) — Critical Path

Possible paths from start to end:

- **Path 1:** A → B → D = $4 + 5 + 7 = 16$ days
- **Path 2:** A → C → D = $4 + 3 + 7 = 14$ days

The **longest path** is the **Critical Path**: **A → B → D = 16 days**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** The critical path is the path with the **largest sum of expected times**, not the path with the most activities. Always compute all paths and compare.

**Project Variance** (variance of critical path = sum of variances of activities on it):

$$
\sigma_{\text{CP}}^2 = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_D^2 = 0.444 + 0.444 + 2.778 = 3.666
$$

**Project Std. Deviation:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{CP}} = \sqrt{3.666} \approx 1.915 \text{ days}
$$

### 3.2.3 Part (c) — Probability of Completing in 18 Days

With $T_s = 18$ days, $T_E = 16$ days, $\sigma_{\text{CP}} \approx 1.915$:

$$
Z = \frac{T_s - T_E}{\sigma_{\text{CP}}} = \frac{18 - 16}{1.915} = \frac{2}{1.915} \approx 1.045
$$

From Standard Normal Table:

$$
\Phi(1.04) \approx 0.8508 \quad \text{and} \quad \Phi(1.05) \approx 0.8531
$$

Interpolating at $Z = 1.045$:

$$
\Phi(1.045) \approx 0.8520
$$

**Probability of completion within 18 days ≈ 85.2%.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which N7 Tool

| Situation / Problem Type | Best-fit N7 Tool | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Too many customer complaints, no clear pattern | **Affinity Diagram** | Group raw VOC data |
| Causes are interconnected in a tangled mess | **Interrelationship Diagram** | Untangle multi-causal links |
| Goal is broad, need actionable tasks | **Tree Diagram** | Means–Ends decomposition |
| Cross-functional relations to be mapped | **Matrix Diagram** | Show relationships between sets |
| Multiple alternatives & criteria for selection | **Prioritization Matrix** | Weighted scoring → ranking |
| Plan may face unforeseen problems | **PDPC** | Contingency planning |
| Project schedule with dependencies | **Arrow Diagram** | PERT/CPM critical path |

> [!TIP]
> **Sequence for KTU answers:** When a 14-mark question says *"Apply N7 tools to a quality problem"*, walk through the matrix above in order — *Affinity → Interrelationship → Tree → Matrix → Prioritization → PDPC → Arrow*. Examiners love seeing a **systematic flow**.
