> **Block-level interpretation for KTU answers:** When physical drawings are impractical in exam scripts, this Mermaid-style block-flow is an *acceptable substitute* if redrawn as labelled boxes connected by arrows. It conveys the temporal sequence of benchmarking decisions effectively.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*Define benchmarking. Differentiate between internal and competitive benchmarking in three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Benchmarking is the systematic and continuous process of measuring an organization's products, services, and processes against those of recognized best-in-class performers, for the purpose of identifying performance gaps and implementing improvements that lead to superior performance.

**Differentiation (2 marks — three crisp points):**

| Aspect | Internal Benchmarking | Competitive Benchmarking |
|---|---|---|
| Reference | Other units *within* the same organization | *Direct competitors* in the same industry |
| Data accessibility | Easy, internal records | Difficult, often confidential |
| Strategic value | Limited by internal ceiling | Industry-relevant, high transferability |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*List the five phases of the benchmarking process in the correct order.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.6 marks per phase):**

1. **Planning** — Identify the subject and benchmarking partner.
2. **Data Collection** — Gather quantitative and qualitative information.
3. **Analysis** — Compute performance gap and identify root causes.
4. **Action / Implementation** — Adapt and deploy improved practices.
5. **Review / Maturity** — Monitor outcomes and recalibrate.

---

### 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five phases of the benchmarking process** with a suitable engineering example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit records its current defect rate at **4.5%**, while the industry leader operates at **1.8%**. The unit targets closing **65% of the gap** in one cycle.
   Compute: (i) the **Performance Gap (%)**, (ii) the **Target Defect Rate**, and (iii) the **Relative Competitive Position** of the unit. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Seven Marks Model Answer

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Benchmarking, formalized by Robert C. Camp, is a structured quality management discipline comprising five sequential phases.

**Phase 1 — Planning (1.5 marks):**
The organization identifies *what* to benchmark (a specific process — e.g., welding defect rate in a fabrication shop) and *whom* to benchmark against (e.g., a Japanese fabricator with world-class weld quality). Baseline current performance is documented.

**Phase 2 — Data Collection (1.5 marks):**
Quantitative metrics (defects per million opportunities, weld strength variance) and qualitative practices (operator training, equipment calibration) are gathered via site visits, interviews, and benchmark reports.

**Phase 3 — Analysis (1.5 marks):**
The performance gap is computed numerically, and root causes are investigated using quality tools (fishbone, Pareto). For instance, the gap analysis might reveal that **70% of weld defects** originate from inadequate preheat procedure.

**Phase 4 — Action / Implementation (1.5 marks):**
An action plan is developed to introduce preheat standardization, operator certification, and inline temperature sensors. The benchmark's practices are *adapted*, not copied, to suit the local equipment and workforce.

**Phase 5 — Review / Maturity (1 mark):**
Post-implementation, the new defect rate is measured and compared against the target. The benchmarking cycle is then re-entered with recalibrated standards, embodying continuous improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently write "benchmarking means copying best practices." This **loses 1–2 marks**. Always emphasize *adaptation*, not imitation. Also, do **not** list only four phases — the fifth (Review/Maturity) is frequently tested.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

- $\text{Current Defect Rate} = 4.5\%$
- $\text{Benchmark Defect Rate} = 1.8\%$
- $\text{Adoption Rate} = 65\% = 0.65$

#### Sub-part (i) — Performance Gap (3 marks)

$$\text{Performance Gap} = \frac{\text{Benchmark} - \text{Current}}{\text{Benchmark}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Performance Gap} = \frac{1.8 - 4.5}{1.8} \times 100$$

> Note: For "lower is better" metrics like defect rate, we use benchmark as the *target*, so we compute shortfall relative to the benchmark value.

$$\text{Performance Gap} = \frac{-2.7}{1.8} \times 100$$

$$\text{Performance Gap} = -150\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnitude interpretation:** The unit's defect rate is **150% higher** than the benchmark. In KTU notation, present the magnitude as 150% excess or simply 150 percentage points of relative shortfall.

**Equivalent presentation (using current as reference):**

$$\text{Relative Excess} = \frac{4.5 - 1.8}{1.8} \times 100 = 150\%$$

#### Sub-part (ii) — Target Defect Rate (2 marks)

$$\text{Improvement Required} = \text{Current Gap Magnitude} \times \text{Adoption Rate}$$

$$\text{Improvement} = 2.7\% \times 0.65 = 1.755\%$$

$$\text{Target Defect Rate} = \text{Current} - \text{Improvement} = 4.5\% - 1.755\%$$

$$\text{Target Defect Rate} = 2.745\%$$

#### Sub-part (iii) — Relative Competitive Position (2 marks)

$$\text{Relative Competitive Position} = \frac{\text{Organization's Rate}}{\text{Benchmark Rate}} \times 100$$

For defect rate (lower is better), we express as the ratio inverse:

$$\text{Relative Quality Position} = \frac{1.8}{4.5} \times 100 = 40\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The unit delivers only **40%** of the benchmark's quality level in defect performance — meaning there is significant room for improvement and a strong case for adopting the benchmark's preheat procedure.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **four types of benchmarking** with one industry example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **role of benchmarking in Total Quality Management (TQM)** and outline **three major limitations** of the benchmarking process. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Four Types of Benchmarking (7 marks)

**Type 1 — Internal Benchmarking (1.75 marks):**
Comparing similar functions across units within the same organization. *Example:* Infosys comparing average ticket resolution time across its Bengaluru, Mysuru, and Thiruvananthapuram development centres to identify the best internal process for global rollout.

**Type 2 — Competitive Benchmarking (1.75 marks):**
Direct comparison with industry rivals. *Example:* Asian Paints benchmarking its dealer-distribution efficiency against Berger Paints' network metrics.

**Type 3 — Functional / Generic Benchmarking (1.75 marks):**
Cross-industry comparison against the best-in-class performer of a specific function. *Example:* A Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC) depot studying Indian Railways' reservation system architecture for passenger flow optimization.

**Type 4 — Strategic Benchmarking (1.75 marks):**
Long-term, broad strategic comparison. *Example:* Tata Motors studying Toyota's Production System strategy to inform its global manufacturing expansion roadmap.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse *functional* with *competitive* benchmarking. Functional benchmarking is **cross-industry**; competitive is **same-industry**. Mixing examples loses 1–2 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — Role in TQM + Limitations (7 marks)

**Role of Benchmarking in TQM (4 marks):**

1. **Customer Focus Reinforcement (1 mark):** Benchmarking translates customer expectations into measurable standards by referencing best-in-class customer experiences.
2. **Continuous Improvement Engine (1 mark):** It feeds Deming's PDCA cycle with external evidence, preventing internal complacency.
3. **Objective Target Setting (1 mark):** Replaces guesswork in goal-setting with quantified, defensible targets.
4. **Best Practice Adoption (1 mark):** Accelerates quality maturity by leveraging proven external methods rather than trial-and-error.

**Three Major Limitations (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Adaptation Difficulty:** Practices that excel in one organizational culture may fail when transplanted to another without contextual modification.
2. **Data Asymmetry:** Competitor and partner data are often incomplete, outdated, or selectively disclosed, undermining analytical validity.
3. **Resource Intensity:** A rigorous benchmarking project demands significant time, money, and skilled manpower — sometimes exceeding the value of insights gained.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students writing "benchmarking has no limitations" lose marks. **Always state at least three limitations** — this is a recurring KTU valuation pattern.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- **Benchmarking** = systematic comparison with best-in-class for superior performance.
- It is **learning and adapting**, **not copying**.
- Origin: **Robert C. Camp**, Xerox, 1970s–80s.
- **Four Types:** Internal, Competitive, Functional/Generic, Strategic.
- **Five Phases:** Planning → Data Collection → Analysis → Action → Review (cyclic).
- **Performance Gap Formula:**
$$\text{Gap (\%)} = \frac{\text{Benchmark} - \text{Current}}{\text{Benchmark}} \times 100$$
- **Lower-is-better metrics** (defect rate, response time): invert the formula or compute magnitude.
- **Adoption Rate** multiplies the gap to set realistic targets.
- Benchmarking **supports PDCA** by providing external evidence for the Check stage.
- **Always state limitations** — copying-without-adaptation, data asymmetry, resource intensity.
- Benchmarking is **continuous**, not a one-time project — the cycle restarts with recalibrated standards.
- For KTU numerical answers, **show every algebraic step**; partial steps lose partial marks.

---

**End of Module 4 — Benchmarking Process Notes**
**Course:** PECHT413 — Total Quality Management
**KTU 2024 Scheme | B.Tech | Outcome-Based Education**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Function Deployment (QFD) - House of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) - House of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven planning and product development methodology that systematically translates the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** into actionable technical and engineering requirements throughout the stages of product design, engineering, and production. The term *deployment* refers to the cascading flow-down of customer requirements into subsystems, components, processes, and ultimately production specifications.

QFD was originally developed in **Japan by Yoji Akao** in 1966 and first implemented at the Mitsubishi Heavy Industries shipyard in Kobe, Japan. The methodology was subsequently refined and popularized in the United States by Bob King and Glenn Mazur of the American Supplier Institute (ASI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *QFD is a planning tool that translates customer needs (the "Whats") into appropriate technical company requirements (the "Hows") for each stage of product development and production, ensuring that customer satisfaction drives the engineering and manufacturing decisions from concept to final product.*

The central visual artefact of QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a matrix diagram whose shape resembles a house, hence the name. It serves as the primary vehicle to map and correlate the relationship between customer desires and engineering characteristics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a custom home for a client. Before laying a single brick, the architect must fully understand what the homeowner wants (more natural light, a large kitchen, child-safe staircases, a quiet study room). However, the homeowner speaks in lifestyle terms (e.g., "I want it to feel open and airy"), while the builder speaks in technical terms (window-to-wall ratio, cubic-feet-per-minute ventilation, R-value insulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analogy:** QFD is the **"universal translator"** between the homeowner and the construction team. The House of Quality is the **blueprint-meeting document** where the architect writes down every wish of the homeowner in plain language on one wall, and converts each wish into a specific engineering instruction (window type, HVAC capacity, wall thickness) on the adjacent wall. The strength of the link between the wish and the instruction is graded, and at the roof, the architect notes which engineering decisions help or hurt each other (e.g., bigger windows reduce wall strength).

In manufacturing terms:
- **Customer Requirements (CRs)** = *What the customer wants* (e.g., long battery life, sleek design, affordable price)
- **Technical Requirements (TRs)** = *How engineering will deliver it* (e.g., mAh capacity, chassis dimensions, bill-of-materials cost)
- The **HOQ** is the document that ensures **no customer wish is left un-engineered**, and **no engineering decision is made without a customer justification**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following KTU-grade definitions and metrics must be memorized verbatim:

- **Voice of Customer (VOC):** The raw, unfiltered expectations, preferences, and aversions of the end user, typically gathered through focus groups, interviews, surveys, complaint logs, and ethnographic observation.
- **Customer Requirements (CRs):** Refined and prioritized VOC data expressed as distinct, measurable attributes (the *Whats*).
- **Technical Requirements (TRs):** The engineering characteristics, design parameters, and specifications that satisfy the CRs (the *Hows*).
- **House of Quality (HOQ):** A single matrix that combines customer requirements, technical requirements, their relationships, and competitive intelligence into one planning tool.
- **Importance Rating:** A numerical weight, usually on a **1–5** scale, assigned to each CR reflecting its relative priority to the customer.
- **Relationship Matrix:** The central grid evaluating how strongly each TR contributes to each CR (typically **9 = strong, 3 = medium, 1 = weak, 0 = none**).
- **Roof (Correlation Matrix):** The triangular top of the house showing interrelationships between technical requirements.
- **Absolute Weight (Raw Score):** The sum of (Customer Importance) × (Relationship Score) for each technical requirement.
- **Relative Weight (%):** Normalized weights calculated by dividing each absolute weight by the total of all absolute weights, multiplied by 100.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** The HOQ is always read in three directions: **Top-Down** (priorities), **Left-Right** (Whats to Hows), and **Roof** (How-to-How trade-offs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic "Why" Behind QFD

QFD exists because of three persistent problems in product development:

1. **The Customer-Engineering Disconnect:** Marketing, design, and production speak different languages. Customer wishes are often lost or misinterpreted as they cascade through departments.
2. **Late Discovery of Failure:** Traditional product development discovers that the customer dislikes the product only after launch. QFD catches mismatches *before* tooling and tooling-related investments are made.
3. **Reactive Decision-Making:** Without a structured tool, engineering decisions are made on intuition, experience, or internal politics rather than data-driven customer priorities.

QFD solves these by making **customer requirements the *driver*** of every downstream engineering and process decision.

## 2.2 The "Four Phases" of QFD (Cascading Houses)

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, QFD is described as a sequence of four interconnected matrices, often called the **"Four Phases"** or **"Aspects"**. Each phase deploys information from the previous one:

| Phase | House Name | Focus | Translates |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Phase 1** | House of Quality (Product Planning) | What product features satisfy the customer? | Customer Requirements → Technical Requirements |
| **Phase 2** | House of Quality (Parts Deployment) | Which parts deliver the technical requirements? | Technical Requirements → Component/Part Characteristics |
| **Phase 3** | House of Quality (Process Planning) | Which manufacturing processes deliver the parts? | Part Characteristics → Process Parameters |
| **Phase 4** | House of Quality (Production Planning) | What production controls deliver the process? | Process Parameters → Production/Quality Controls |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *most-examined* phase is **Phase 1 — the original House of Quality**. The "Whats" of one phase become the "Hows" of the next phase, creating a cascade.

## 2.3 Structural Anatomy of the House of Quality (Phase 1)

The HOQ contains **eight discrete sections**, each performing a specific information-processing function. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to label and describe each section by name.

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats):** Listed in the leftmost column, prioritized by importance rating.
2. **Planning Matrix (Customer Importance):** A column of weights (1–5) beside the customer requirements.
3. **Technical Requirements (Hows):** Listed across the top row as engineering descriptors.
4. **Relationship Matrix (Center):** The heart of the house — symbols (●, ○, △) or numbers (9, 3, 1) indicating how strongly each TR satisfies each CR.
5. **Technical Correlation / Roof Matrix (Top Triangle):** Symbols (++, +, −, −−, or blank) showing whether technical requirements help, hurt, or are independent of each other.
6. **Competitive Assessment — Customer:** Two or more rows on the left of the planning matrix rating our product and competitors against each customer requirement (1–5 scale).
7. **Competitive Assessment — Technical:** Rows above the technical requirements rating our product and competitors against each technical characteristic.
8. **Target Values / Engineering Targets:** The right-most column setting measurable goals for each technical requirement.
9. **Importance Ratings (Bottom):** Raw and relative weights derived from the relationship matrix and customer importance.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Construction Logic

The HOQ is **always built in a specific sequential order**. Deviating from the order produces an internally inconsistent matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 9-Step KTU-Standard HOQ Construction Procedure:**
> 1. List the **Customer Requirements (CRs)** in the leftmost column using VOC data.
> 2. Assign a **Customer Importance Rating (1–5)** for each CR based on VOC analysis.
> 3. List the **Technical Requirements (TRs)** across the top row.
> 4. Fill the **Relationship Matrix** at the center, scoring each TR–CR pair (typically 1, 3, 9 or Weak, Moderate, Strong).
> 5. Fill the **Roof / Technical Correlation Matrix** to identify synergies and conflicts among TRs.
> 6. Fill the **Customer Competitive Assessment** by benchmarking our product and at least one competitor against each CR.
> 7. Fill the **Technical Competitive Assessment** by benchmarking against each TR.
> 8. Determine **Target Values** for each TR (measurable engineering goals).
> 9. Calculate **Absolute Weights** and **Relative Weights (%)** for each TR to prioritize engineering effort.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The mathematical core of the House of Quality is the weight calculation. The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for KTU 2024 examination problems.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Absolute Weight of a TR** | $W_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{j} \cdot R_{ij}$ | $W_i$ = raw score of TR $i$, $I_j$ = importance of CR $j$, $R_{ij}$ = relationship between TR $i$ and CR $j$, $n$ = number of CRs | Dimensionless score |
| **Total Absolute Weight** | $W_{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} W_{i}$ | $W_T$ = sum of all absolute weights, $m$ = number of TRs | Dimensionless score |
| **Relative Weight (%)** | $RW_{i} = \dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{T}} \times 100$ | $RW_i$ = percentage priority of TR $i$ | **Percent (%)** |
| **Rank** | $Rank = \text{sort}(RW_{i}, \text{descending})$ | Engineering priority order (1 = highest priority) | Integer |
| **Customer Satisfaction Score** | $CS_{j} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{p=1}^{k} S_{pj}$ | $CS_j$ = average score of our product on CR $j$ across $k$ customers/respondents | Rating (1–5) |
| **Improvement Ratio** | $IR_{j} = \dfrac{T_{j}}{S_{j,\text{us}}}$ | $T_j$ = target satisfaction, $S_{j,\text{us}}$ = our current score on CR $j$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Sales Point (Optional)** | $SP_{j} = I_{j} \cdot IR_{j}$ | $SP_j$ = strategic priority of CR $j$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** Do not confuse **Customer Importance Rating** ($I_j$, given by the customer) with **Absolute Weight** ($W_i$, calculated for each technical requirement). They are multiplied together but are not the same value.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

QFD is extensively used in the following KTU-relevant engineering sectors:

- **Automotive Industry** (Toyota's hallmark — *the "Big M" matrix* at Mazda and Mitsubishi): Designing vehicle platforms where customer priorities (fuel efficiency, safety rating, infotainment) drive chassis, engine, and electronics specifications.
- **Consumer Electronics** (Asian OEM manufacturers): Translating smartphone user demands (camera quality, battery life, screen brightness) into component specs for SoC selection, optics, and battery cells.
- **Software Product Management** (Agile QFD): Mapping user stories to technical backlog items and acceptance criteria.
- **Healthcare and Medical Devices** (FDA design-control compliance): The Design History File of an FDA-regulated device is conceptually a series of cascading HOQs.
- **Civil Engineering**: Translating client briefs (aesthetic, functional, sustainability) into architectural specifications, structural load tolerances, and HVAC capacities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** QFD is recognized as a Tier-1 tool in **Six Sigma (DMAIC Define Phase)**, **Design for Six Sigma (DFSS IDOV)**, and **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)** workflows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Absolute Weight Derivation — Full Algebraic Walkthrough

The Absolute Weight is the central computed value in the House of Quality. Below is the full KTU-board-quality derivation, written out step by step with no skipped transitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A manufacturer of portable Bluetooth speakers wants to determine which engineering specification to prioritize. Three customer requirements (CR1, CR2, CR3) and three technical requirements (TR1, TR2, TR3) have been identified from VOC analysis. Customer importance ratings and the relationship matrix are provided.

**Given Data:**

| | CR1 (Long Battery Life) | CR2 (Loud Volume) | CR3 (Lightweight) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Customer Importance ($I_j$)** | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| **TR1 (Battery Capacity, mAh)** | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| **TR2 (Driver Wattage, W)** | 1 | 9 | 0 |
| **TR3 (Chassis Mass, g)** | 0 | 1 | 9 |

**Step 1 — Define the Absolute Weight formula for a single TR.**

For technical requirement $i$, the absolute weight is the sum of products of customer importance ($I_j$) and the relationship strength ($R_{ij}$) across all customer requirements.

$$W_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{j} \cdot R_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR1 (Battery Capacity).**

$$W_{1} = I_{1} \cdot R_{1,1} + I_{2} \cdot R_{1,2} + I_{3} \cdot R_{1,3}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values into the expression.**

The values $I_1 = 5$, $I_2 = 4$, $I_3 = 3$ are the customer importance ratings. The values $R_{1,1} = 9$, $R_{1,2} = 1$, $R_{1,3} = 0$ are the relationship scores between TR1 and each customer requirement.

$$W_{1} = (5)(9) + (4)(1) + (3)(0)$$

**Step 4 — Perform the multiplication of each term.**

The first term is the product of customer importance of CR1 (5) and the strong relationship between TR1 and CR1 (9). The second term is the product of customer importance of CR2 (4) and the weak relationship between TR1 and CR2 (1). The third term is zero because there is no relationship.

$$W_{1} = 45 + 4 + 0$$

**Step 5 — Sum the three products to obtain the absolute weight of TR1.**

$$W_{1} = 49$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR2 (Driver Wattage).**

$$W_{2} = I_{1} \cdot R_{2,1} + I_{2} \cdot R_{2,2} + I_{3} \cdot R_{2,3}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the numerical values for TR2.**

$$W_{2} = (5)(1) + (4)(9) + (3)(0)$$

**Step 8 — Compute each term and sum.**

$$W_{2} = 5 + 36 + 0 = 41$$

**Step 9 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR3 (Chassis Mass).**

$$W_{3} = I_{1} \cdot R_{3,1} + I_{2} \cdot R_{3,2} + I_{3} \cdot R_{3,3}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute the numerical values for TR3.**

$$W_{3} = (5)(0) + (4)(1) + (3)(9)$$

**Step 11 — Compute each term and sum.**

$$W_{3} = 0 + 4 + 27 = 31$$

**Step 12 — Calculate the Total Absolute Weight across all technical requirements.**

The total absolute weight is the sum of the individual absolute weights of all technical requirements. It serves as the denominator for normalization.

$$W_{T} = W_{1} + W_{2} + W_{3}$$

**Step 13 — Substitute the computed values.**

$$W_{T} = 49 + 41 + 31$$

**Step 14 — Sum the three absolute weights.**

$$W_{T} = 121$$

**Step 15 — Compute the Relative Weight (in percent) for each technical requirement.**

The relative weight expresses each technical requirement's share of the total engineering priority. It is calculated by dividing the absolute weight of the TR by the total absolute weight, then multiplying by 100 to convert to a percentage.

$$RW_{i} = \frac{W_{i}}{W_{T}} \times 100$$

**Step 16 — Substitute values for TR1.**

$$RW_{1} = \frac{49}{121} \times 100$$

**Step 17 — Compute the decimal value.**

$$RW_{1} \approx 0.40496 \times 100$$

**Step 18 — Convert to percentage.**

$$RW_{1} \approx 40.50\%$$

**Step 19 — Compute Relative Weight for TR2.**

$$RW_{2} = \frac{41}{121} \times 100 \approx 33.88\%$$

**Step 20 — Compute Relative Weight for TR3.**

$$RW_{3} = \frac{31}{121} \times 100 \approx 25.62\%$$

**Step 21 — Verify that the three relative weights sum to 100%.**

$$40.50 + 33.88 + 25.62 = 100.00\%$$

The total is exactly 100% (allowing for rounding), confirming the calculation is correct.

**Step 22 — Final Engineering Interpretation.**

| Technical Requirement | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight | Rank |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **TR1: Battery Capacity** | 49 | **40.50%** | **1** |
| **TR2: Driver Wattage** | 41 | 33.88% | 2 |
| **TR3: Chassis Mass** | 31 | 25.62% | 3 |

The engineering team should allocate the largest share of its R&D effort to **battery capacity**, followed by driver wattage, and finally chassis mass minimization.

## 3.2 Complete House of Quality — Schematic Construction

The following table represents a fully populated House of Quality for the above example, which students should be able to reproduce in the examination.

| Section | Position in HOQ | Content |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Customer Requirements (CRs)** | Left column | CR1, CR2, CR3 |
| **Customer Importance** | Left column (adjacent) | 5, 4, 3 |
| **Competitive Assessment — Customer (Our Brand)** | Far left | e.g., 4, 3, 4 |
| **Competitive Assessment — Customer (Competitor A)** | Far left | e.g., 3, 4, 3 |
| **Technical Requirements (TRs)** | Top row | TR1, TR2, TR3 |
| **Relationship Matrix** | Center | 9, 1, 0 / 1, 9, 0 / 0, 1, 9 |
| **Technical Correlation (Roof)** | Top triangle | Trade-offs between TR1–TR2, TR1–TR3, TR2–TR3 |
| **Competitive Assessment — Technical** | Top right | Our/Competitor TR scores |
| **Target Values** | Right column | 4000 mAh, 20 W, 250 g |
| **Importance Ratings (Absolute / Relative)** | Bottom | 49 / 40.50%, 41 / 33.88%, 31 / 25.62% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
