# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Mention the origin of QFD.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Definition:** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a structured, customer-driven planning methodology that translates the Voice of the Customer (VOC) into specific technical and engineering requirements at each stage of product design, development, and production.
- (1 Mark) **Origin:** QFD was originally developed in **Japan by Yoji Akao** in **1966** while working at the **Mitsubishi Heavy Industries** shipyard in Kobe, Japan.
- (1 Mark) **Tool:** The primary tool of QFD is the **House of Quality (HOQ)**, a matrix diagram that maps the relationship between customer requirements ("Whats") and technical requirements ("Hows").

### Question 2: What is the House of Quality? List any four sections of the HOQ.
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 2]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- (1 Mark) **Definition:** The House of Quality (HOQ) is a matrix-based planning diagram used in QFD to translate customer requirements into technical requirements, benchmark against competitors, and prioritize engineering effort.
- (1 Mark) **Four Sections** (any four of the following, ¼ Mark each):
  1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — left column
  2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — top row
  3. **Relationship Matrix** — central grid
  4. **Technical Correlation / Roof** — top triangle
  5. **Competitive Assessment** — left and top margins
  6. **Importance Ratings (Absolute and Relative Weights)** — bottom
  7. **Target Values** — right column

## 5.2 Part B — Essay-Level Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-question 1: QFD Conceptual & Structural**

**(a)** Define Quality Function Deployment. Explain the concept of the House of Quality (HOQ) with a neat block diagram. List the **eight sections** of the HOQ and explain the function of the **Relationship Matrix** and the **Roof (Technical Correlation Matrix)**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company developing a new electric kettle has identified the following customer requirements (CRs) and technical requirements (TRs). Construct the House of Quality weight calculations and identify the highest-priority technical requirement.

| | CR1 (Fast Boiling) | CR2 (Energy Efficient) | CR3 (Safe to Touch) | CR4 (Quiet Operation) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Customer Importance** | 5 | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| **TR1 (Heater Wattage)** | 9 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **TR2 (Insulation Thickness)** | 0 | 3 | 9 | 1 |
| **TR3 (Vibration Dampener)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 9 |

**(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Quality Function Deployment (QFD) is a customer-focused product development methodology that systematically translates customer requirements (Whats) into technical and engineering specifications (Hows) at each stage of product design, engineering, and production. The central matrix tool is the **House of Quality (HOQ)**.

> **Block Diagram (2 Marks):** A schematic HOQ is shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. The shape resembles a house — the **Customer Requirements** form the left wall, the **Technical Requirements** form the roof beams, the **Relationship Matrix** is the central living area, and the **Roof** is the triangular peak.

> **Eight Sections (2 Marks):**
> 1. Customer Requirements (Whats)
> 2. Planning Matrix (Customer Importance Ratings)
> 3. Technical Requirements (Hows)
> 4. Relationship Matrix (Center)
> 5. Technical Correlation (Roof)
> 6. Customer Competitive Assessment
> 7. Technical Competitive Assessment
> 8. Importance Ratings and Target Values

> **Relationship Matrix Function (1 Mark):** The Relationship Matrix is the central grid that scores how strongly each Technical Requirement contributes to each Customer Requirement, typically using the symbols ● (Strong, 9), ○ (Moderate, 3), △ (Weak, 1), and blank (None, 0). It provides the raw data for computing the absolute and relative weights of each technical requirement.

> **Roof Function (1 Mark):** The Roof (Technical Correlation Matrix) is the triangular top of the house that captures the **interrelationships between technical requirements themselves**. Strong positive correlations (++) indicate synergies (improving one TR also improves another); strong negative correlations (−−) indicate trade-offs (improving one TR worsens another). This warns the design team of engineering conflicts early in the cycle.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the Absolute Weight formula.**

$$W_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{j} \cdot R_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR1 (Heater Wattage).**

$$W_{1} = (5)(9) + (4)(1) + (5)(0) + (3)(0) = 45 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 49$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR2 (Insulation Thickness).**

$$W_{2} = (5)(0) + (4)(3) + (5)(9) + (3)(1) = 0 + 12 + 45 + 3 = 60$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR3 (Vibration Dampener).**

$$W_{3} = (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (5)(0) + (3)(9) = 0 + 0 + 0 + 27 = 27$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Total Absolute Weight.**

$$W_{T} = 49 + 60 + 27 = 136$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Relative Weights.**

$$RW_{1} = \frac{49}{136} \times 100 \approx 36.03\%$$

$$RW_{2} = \frac{60}{136} \times 100 \approx 44.12\%$$

$$RW_{3} = \frac{27}{136} \times 100 \approx 19.85\%$$

**Step 7 — Verify the Sum.**

$$36.03 + 44.12 + 19.85 = 100.00\%$$

**Step 8 — Rank the Technical Requirements.**

| TR | Description | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight | Rank |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **TR2** | Insulation Thickness | **60** | **44.12%** | **1** |
| **TR1** | Heater Wattage | 49 | 36.03% | 2 |
| **TR3** | Vibration Dampener | 27 | 19.85% | 3 |

> **Conclusion (1 Mark):** The highest-priority technical requirement is **TR2 — Insulation Thickness**, with a relative weight of **44.12%**. The engineering team should allocate the largest R&D investment to developing superior insulation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Sub-question 2: HOQ Construction and Application**

**(a)** Explain the **four phases (four houses)** of QFD. Describe how the *Whats* of one phase become the *Hows* of the next phase. Mention the **types of conflicts** that the Roof of the House of Quality is designed to reveal. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A design team is developing a new laptop. The following data has been compiled:

| | CR1 (Lightweight) | CR2 (Long Battery) | CR3 (Bright Display) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Customer Importance** | 4 | 5 | 4 |
| **TR1 (Chassis Material)** | 9 | 1 | 0 |
| **TR2 (Battery Cell Capacity)** | 1 | 9 | 0 |
| **TR3 (Backlight Nits)** | 0 | 1 | 9 |

Calculate the absolute weight, relative weight, and rank of each technical requirement. Identify the **single most critical TR** and explain how the result guides the engineering team's resource allocation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

> **Four Phases of QFD (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Phase 1 — Product Planning (HOQ):** Translates **Customer Requirements (Whats)** into **Technical Requirements (Hows)** for the product.
> 2. **Phase 2 — Parts Deployment:** The TRs from Phase 1 become the *Whats* of Phase 2, which are then translated into **Component and Part Characteristics (Hows)**.
> 3. **Phase 3 — Process Planning:** The component characteristics become the *Whats* of Phase 3, which are translated into **Manufacturing Process Parameters (Hows)**.
> 4. **Phase 4 — Production Planning:** The process parameters become the *Whats* of Phase 4, which are translated into **Production and Quality Control Instructions (Hows)**.

> **Cascading Logic (2 Marks):** The "Whats" of one phase become the "Hows" of the next phase because the output of one QFD house is a refined engineering requirement that must be further decomposed into a more detailed deliverable. This cascading pattern ensures that no customer wish is lost in translation across the multi-stage product development cycle.

> **Conflicts Revealed by the Roof (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Trade-offs (Negative Correlations, −−):** Improving one TR worsens another (e.g., increasing battery capacity increases chassis weight).
> 2. **Synergies (Positive Correlations, ++):** Improving one TR also improves another (e.g., better thermal design also improves display brightness stability).
> 3. **Independence (No Mark):** Two TRs are unrelated and can be optimized separately.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the Absolute Weight formula.**

$$W_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{j} \cdot R_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR1 (Chassis Material).**

$$W_{1} = (4)(9) + (5)(1) + (4)(0) = 36 + 5 + 0 = 41$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR2 (Battery Cell Capacity).**

$$W_{2} = (4)(1) + (5)(9) + (4)(0) = 4 + 45 + 0 = 49$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Absolute Weight for TR3 (Backlight Nits).**

$$W_{3} = (4)(0) + (5)(1) + (4)(9) = 0 + 5 + 36 = 41$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the Total Absolute Weight.**

$$W_{T} = 41 + 49 + 41 = 131$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Relative Weights.**

$$RW_{1} = \frac{41}{131} \times 100 \approx 31.30\%$$

$$RW_{2} = \frac{49}{131} \times 100 \approx 37.40\%$$

$$RW_{3} = \frac{41}{131} \times 100 \approx 31.30\%$$

**Step 7 — Rank the Technical Requirements.**

| TR | Description | Absolute Weight | Relative Weight | Rank |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **TR2** | Battery Cell Capacity | **49** | **37.40%** | **1** |
| **TR1** | Chassis Material | 41 | 31.30% | 2 (tied) |
| **TR3** | Backlight Nits | 41 | 31.30% | 2 (tied) |

> **Critical TR and Resource Allocation Guidance (2 Marks):**
> The single most critical technical requirement is **TR2 — Battery Cell Capacity**, with a relative weight of **37.40%**. This result guides the engineering team to:
> 1. **Allocate the largest share of R&D budget** to battery cell selection, energy density optimization, and battery management system (BMS) design.
> 2. **Prioritize vendor negotiations** with high-energy-density cell suppliers (e.g., lithium-polymer and silicon-anode technology partners).
> 3. **Conduct trade-off analysis with TR1 (Chassis Material):** Increasing battery capacity will increase mass, so the team should plan a paired engineering investigation into lightweight chassis materials (magnesium alloy, carbon-fiber composite) to neutralize the negative correlation revealed in the Roof.
> 4. **Set measurable target values** (e.g., ≥ 90 Whr capacity, ≥ 15 hours of real-world use) for the Battery Cell Capacity TR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Customer Importance Rating ($I_j$) with Absolute Weight ($W_i$):** The customer importance rating is **given** (1–5). The absolute weight is **calculated**. They are not interchangeable.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify the sum of relative weights:** Always confirm that the sum equals 100% (or 1.00). Examiners award partial credit for verification.
> 3. **Ignoring the Roof:** The Roof (Technical Correlation) is a separate grading axis from the Relationship Matrix. Do not skip it.
> 4. **Mislabeling the axes of the HOQ:** Students frequently write "Whats" on the top and "Hows" on the side. The correct orientation is **Whats on the LEFT** and **Hows on the TOP**.
> 5. **Single-House QFD:** Examiners expect the answer to mention the **four-phase cascade** for full marks on QFD definition questions. Mentioning only the Product Planning HOQ loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **No Engineering Interpretation:** The final answer to a weight-calculation problem must include a sentence on **what the engineering team should DO with the ranking**. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Quick-Recall — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- **QFD = Customer-driven product development methodology** that translates **Voice of Customer (VOC)** into engineering requirements.
- **Origin:** Yoji Akao, Japan, 1966, at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries.
- **Primary Tool:** **House of Quality (HOQ)** — a matrix shaped like a house.
- **Four Phases of QFD:** (1) Product Planning, (2) Parts Deployment, (3) Process Planning, (4) Production Planning. Each phase has its own HOQ.
- **Whats (left wall) = Customer Requirements.** **Hows (top row) = Technical Requirements.** The **center** is the **Relationship Matrix**. The **roof** is the **Technical Correlation Matrix**.
- **Relationship Scoring:** 9 (Strong, ●), 3 (Moderate, ○), 1 (Weak, △), 0 (None).
- **Roof Symbols:** ++ (strong positive), + (positive), − (negative), −− (strong negative), blank (independent).
- **Absolute Weight Formula:** $W_{i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{j} \cdot R_{ij}$ — multiply customer importance by the relationship score for each pair, then sum across all CRs.
- **Relative Weight Formula:** $RW_{i} = \dfrac{W_{i}}{W_{T}} \times 100$. Sum of all relative weights = 100%.
- **Higher relative weight = Higher engineering priority.**
- **Competitive Assessment** appears in **two places**: customer-side (left margin) and technical-side (top margin).
- **Target Values** are the right-column measurable engineering goals.
- **Cascade Principle:** The "Whats" of one phase become the "Hows" of the next phase.
- **QFD integration:** QFD is used in **Six Sigma**, **DFSS (Design for Six Sigma)**, and **DFMA (Design for Manufacturing and Assembly)** workflows.
- **Benefits of QFD:** Reduces time-to-market, lowers rework cost, increases customer satisfaction, and aligns cross-functional teams (marketing, R&D, manufacturing) on a single customer-driven plan.
- **Limitations of QFD:** Time-consuming to build initially; requires accurate VOC data; may oversimplify qualitative trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: QFD Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** QFD Process # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, team-based planning and product development methodology that systematically captures the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** and translates customer requirements (the *WHATs*) into specific, measurable technical/engineering characteristics (the *HOWs*) at every stage of product or service design, production, and delivery. The QFD process was developed in **Japan in 1972** by **Dr. Yoji Akao** at the Mitsubishi Heavy Industries shipyard, originally as a method to ensure quality during the design phase. It was later popularized in the West by **John Hauser and Don Clausing** (1988) under the visual representation famously known as the **"House of Quality (HOQ)"**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** QFD is the cornerstone tool that links the customer's latent needs with the firm's internal engineering capabilities. In KTU board examinations, students must be able to draw and explain the **House of Quality**, identify the **6–7 sub-matrices** it contains, and discuss the **QFD Four-Phase Matrix Deployment Model**. ## 1.2 The QFD Process — Definition The **QFD Process** is the step-by-step systematic procedure used to deploy quality from the customer's perspective down through every functional department of an organization (Marketing → Engineering → Manufacturing → Sales). It ensures that the customer's voice is not "lost in translation" between departments. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Building a House with the Customer's Blueprint" > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** > Imagine you want to build a custom home. You, the *customer*, tell the architect: *"I want a bright, airy, low-cost, durable house."* These are your **WHATs** (requirements). The architect converts these vague wishes into engineering specs: *north-facing windows, 10-foot ceilings, AAC blocks, M25 concrete grade* — these are the **HOWs** (technical responses). The **QFD Process** is the master communication framework that makes sure every engineer, contractor, and electrician in the chain builds **exactly what you asked for**, without their own personal bias creeping in. The **House of Quality** is the *single page* on which the architect, customer, and builder all write and debate — so there is **one shared truth**. ## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Vocabulary) - **Voice of Customer (VOC):** The actual, raw, unfiltered statements, complaints, and desires expressed by customers. - **WHATs (Customer Requirements – CRs):** Refined, prioritized customer needs (e.g., "easy to use", "low fuel consumption"). - **HOWs (Technical Requirements – TRs / Engineering Characteristics – ECs):** Quantifiable technical descriptors (e.g., torque in Nm, weight in kg). - **House of Quality (HOQ):** The matrix diagram that visually captures the QFD planning process. - **Relationship Matrix:** The central grid showing the strength of linkage between each WHAT and HOW. - **Roof of the House:** The triangular matrix showing correlations (synergies/conflicts) among the HOWs themselves. - **Target Value:** The benchmark engineering specification a team commits to achieve. - **Absolute & Relative Weight:** Importance scores of customer requirements and technical requirements respectively. - **Benchmarking Row:** The competitive analysis comparing your product against competitors on each WHAT. > [!TIP] > For KTU exams, memorize the **6 building blocks of the House of Quality** — this is a guaranteed 3-mark short-answer question. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The QFD Process — Step-by-Step Operational Logic The QFD Process is a **multi-stage deployment** that moves from abstract customer needs to concrete production instructions. The classical flow is: ### **Step 1: Identify Customer Requirements (WHATs)** - Gather data through surveys, focus groups, interviews, complaint logs, warranty cards. - Refine raw VOC statements into actionable, non-overlapping **Customer Requirements (CRs)**. - Assign **Importance Rating (I)** to each CR on a **1 to 5 scale** (1 = least important, 5 = most important). ### **Step 2: Identify Technical Requirements (HOWs)** - Engineering team translates CRs into measurable **Technical Requirements (TRs)** / **Engineering Characteristics (ECs)**. - These must be quantifiable, independent, and controllable. ### **Step 3: Construct the Relationship Matrix (Center of HOQ)** - For each WHAT–HOW pair, assign a **relationship strength**: - **9 = Strong relationship** (●) - **3 = Moderate relationship** (○) - **1 = Weak relationship** (△) - **Blank = No relationship** ### **Step 4: Build the Roof of the House (Correlation Matrix among HOWs)** - Evaluate how the HOWs interact with each other: - **++ = Strong Positive Synergy** (both improve together) - **+ = Positive Synergy** - **– – = Strong Negative Conflict** (one improves, other degrades) - **– = Negative Conflict** - **Blank = Independent** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Roof is critical because it forces engineers to **identify trade-offs** *before* committing to a design, eliminating costly late-stage rework. ### **Step 5: Competitive Benchmarking (Customer Side)** - Score the company's product and competitors on each CR (e.g., 1 to 5 scale). - This produces the **"Customer Perception of Performance"** rows. ### **Step 6: Competitive Benchmarking (Technical Side)** - Score competitors on each TR (measurable specs). - Produces the **"Technical Benchmarking"** row. ### **Step 7: Compute Absolute and Relative Weights of Technical Requirements** - This is the **quantitative core** of QFD — the formula is given below. ### **Step 8: Set Target Values** - Engineering sets specific, measurable targets for each TR based on the weights, competition, and capability. ### **Step 9: Deploy Downstream (Phases of QFD)** - The output of HOQ-1 becomes the input (WHATs) of HOQ-2, and so on. ## 2.2 The Four Phases of QFD (Akao's Deployment Model) | Phase | Input (WHATs) | Output (HOWs) | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Phase 1 – Product Planning** | Customer Requirements | Technical Requirements | Translate customer needs into engineering specs (House of Quality) | | **Phase 2 – Parts Deployment** | TRs from Phase 1 | Critical Part Characteristics | Identify which components deliver each TR | | **Phase 3 – Process Planning** | Critical Part Characteristics | Process Parameters | Decide manufacturing process variables | | **Phase 4 – Production Planning** | Process Parameters | Production Controls / Day-to-Day Instructions | Set shop-floor controls and quality checks | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they appear in KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical/analytical questions.** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Scale | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Importance Rating (IR) | Assigned directly from customer survey | $1$ to $5$ (integer) | | 2 | Row Weight (RW) of a CR | $RW_j = \dfrac{IR_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} IR_i}$ | $0 < RW \leq 1$ | | 3 | Absolute Weight of a TR | $AW_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (RW_j \times R_{ij})$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Relative Weight of a TR | $RelW_i = \dfrac{AW_i}{\sum_{k=1}^{m} AW_k} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | | 5 | Relationship Strength ($R_{ij}$) | $9 \vert 3 \vert 1$ | Discrete | | 6 | Customer Satisfaction Score (CSi) | $CS_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j} (IR_{ij} \times Perf_{ij})}{\sum_{j} IR_{ij}}$ | Scale 1–5 | | 7 | Sales Point (Si) | $S_i = 1.5 \times IR_i$ (if competitive advantage exists), else $S_i = 1.0 \times IR_i$ | Multiplier | **Where:** - $IR_j$ = Importance Rating of $j$-th customer requirement - $n$ = Number of customer requirements - $m$ = Number of technical requirements - $R_{ij}$ = Relationship strength between $i$-th TR and $j$-th CR - $RW_j$ = Normalized row weight of $j$-th CR - $AW_i$ = Absolute weight of $i$-th TR - $RelW_i$ = Relative weight of $i$-th TR (%) ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry:** Toyota, Ford, BMW use HOQ to design vehicles that meet global safety + fuel economy + comfort demands. - **Software Industry:** Microsoft, IBM use QFD to map user stories to system specifications. - **Healthcare:** Hospitals use QFD to translate patient comfort/experience metrics into staffing, room layout, and equipment decisions. - **Aerospace & Defense:** NASA, Boeing use QFD for mission-critical component design where customer = pilot/mission controller. - **Service Sector:** Banks use QFD to map "fast service" (WHAT) into "average wait time < 5 min" (HOW). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Computing the House of Quality > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Problem Type:** "A team has identified 4 Customer Requirements and 4 Technical Requirements. The relationship matrix and importance ratings are given. Compute the **Absolute Weight**, **Relative Weight**, and rank the TRs." ### **Given Data** | Customer Requirement (WHAT) | Importance Rating ($IR$) | | :--- | :---: | | CR1 — Easy to Operate | 5 | | CR2 — Low Fuel Cost | 4 | | CR3 — Comfortable Seating | 3 | | CR4 — Durable | 4 | | **Total** | **16** | **Relationship Matrix (R):** Relationship strengths ($9, 3, 1$) | | TR1 (Weight, kg) | TR2 (Engine CC) | TR3 (Seat Foam Density) | TR4 (Steel Grade) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | CR1 (Easy) | 9 | 3 | 1 | 1 | | CR2 (Fuel) | 9 | 9 | 1 | 3 | | CR3 (Comfort) | 1 | 1 | 9 | 1 | | CR4 (Durable) | 3 | 3 | 1 | 9 | ### **Step 1: Compute Row Weights (RW)** $$ RW_j = \frac{IR_j}{\sum_{i=1}^{4} IR_i} = \frac{IR_j}{16} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} RW_1 &= \frac{5}{16} = 0.3125 \\ RW_2 &= \frac{4}{16} = 0.2500 \\ RW_3 &= \frac{3}{16} = 0.1875 \\ RW_4 &= \frac{4}{16} = 0.2500 \end{aligned} $$ *Logic:* Row weights normalize raw importance ratings so they sum to **1.0**, allowing fair weighted summation. ### **Step 2: Compute Absolute Weights of TRs** Formula: $$ AW_i = \sum_{j=1}^{4} (RW_j \times R_{ij}) $$ **For TR1 (Weight, kg):** $$ \begin{aligned} AW_{TR1} &= (0.3125 \times 9) + (0.2500 \times 9) + (0.1875 \times 1) + (0.2500 \times 3) \\ &= 2.8125 + 2.2500 + 0.1875 + 0.7500 \\ &= 6.0000 \end{aligned} $$ **For TR2 (Engine CC):** $$ \begin{aligned} AW_{TR2} &= (0.3125 \times 3) + (0.2500 \times 9) + (0.1875 \times 1) + (0.2500 \times 3) \\ &= 0.9375 + 2.2500 + 0.1875 + 0.7500 \\ &= 4.1250 \end{aligned} $$ **For TR3 (Seat Foam Density):** $$ \begin{aligned} AW_{TR3} &= (0.3125 \times 1) + (0.2500 \times 1) + (0.1875 \times 9) + (0.2500 \times 1) \\ &= 0.3125 + 0.2500 + 1.6875 + 0.2500 \\ &= 2.5000 \end{aligned} $$ **For TR4 (Steel Grade):** $$ \begin{aligned} AW_{TR4} &= (0.3125 \times 1) + (0.2500 \times 3) + (0.1875 \times 1) + (0.2500 \times 9) \\ &= 0.3125 + 0.7500 + 0.1875 + 2.2500 \\ &= 3.5000 \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 3: Compute Total of Absolute Weights** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum AW &= 6.0000 + 4.1250 + 2.5000 + 3.5000 \\ &= 16.1250 \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 4: Compute Relative Weights (%)** $$ RelW_i = \frac{AW_i}{\sum AW_k} \times 100\% $$ $$ \begin{aligned} RelW_{TR1} &= \frac{6.0000}{16.1250} \times 100\% = 37.21\% \\ RelW_{TR2} &= \frac{4.1250}{16.1250} \times 100\% = 25.58\% \\ RelW_{TR3} &= \frac{2.5000}{16.1250} \times 100\% = 15.50\% \\ RelW_{TR4} &= \frac{3.5000}{16.1250} \times 100\% = 21.71\% \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 5: Rank the Technical Requirements** | Rank | TR | Engineering Characteristic | Relative Weight | Engineering Priority | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **1** | TR1 | Weight (kg) | **37.21%** | **Highest** | | 2 | TR2 | Engine CC | 25.58% | High | | 3 | TR4 | Steel Grade | 21.71% | Medium | | 4 | TR3 | Seat Foam Density | 15.50% | Lowest | ### **Step 6: Engineering Interpretation** > [!NOTE] > The team must invest the **maximum R&D effort, tightest tolerance control, and best supplier quality** into **TR1 (Weight reduction)**. Reducing the vehicle weight has the strongest cross-impact across all 4 customer requirements. **TR3 (Seat foam)** gets lower priority despite being strongly related to Comfort, because the *importance* customers attach to comfort is itself lower. ### **Verification Check (Sanity)** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum RelW &= 37.21 + 25.58 + 15.50 + 21.71 \\ &= 100.00\% \;\; \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The QFD Process — Sequential Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["VOC Collection
Surveys, Interviews, Complaints"] --> B["Identify Customer Requirements WHATs"] B --> C["Assign Importance Ratings 1 to 5"] C --> D["Identify Technical Requirements HOWs"] D --> E["Build Relationship Matrix Center of HOQ"] E --> F["Build Correlation Roof Top of HOQ"] F --> G["Competitive Benchmarking Customer Side"] G --> H["Technical Benchmarking Engineering Side"] H --> I["Compute Absolute and Relative Weights"] I --> J["Set Target Values and Priorities"] J --> K["Deploy to Phase 2 Parts Planning"] K --> L["Deploy to Phase 3 Process Planning"] L --> M["Deploy to Phase 4 Production Planning"] ``` ## 4.2 House of Quality — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HOQ["HOUSE OF QUALITY ARCHITECTURE"] direction TB subgraph Roof["ROOF - Correlation Matrix among HOWs"] Corr["Strong Positive / Positive / Negative / Strong Negative"] end subgraph Top["TOP - Technical Requirements HOWs"] Hows["TR1 TR2 TR3 TR4 TR5"] end subgraph Center["CENTER - Relationship Matrix"] Rel["9 Strong 3 Medium 1 Weak Blank None"] end subgraph Left["LEFT - Customer Requirements WHATs"] Whats["CR1 CR2 CR3 CR4"] end subgraph Bottom["BOTTOM - Technical Benchmarking and Targets"] Bench["Our Product Competitor A Competitor B Target Value"] end subgraph Right["RIGHT - Importance and Weights"] Weights["Absolute Weight Relative Weight Rank"] end Roof --- Top Top --- Center Left --- Center Center --- Bottom Center --- Weights end ``` ## 4.3 Four-Phase Deployment Cascade ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Phase 1
PRODUCT PLANNING
CRs to TRs"] --> P2["Phase 2
PARTS DEPLOYMENT
TRs to Part Specs"] P2 --> P3["Phase 3
PROCESS PLANNING
Part Specs to Process Params"] P3 --> P4["Phase 4
PRODUCTION PLANNING
Process Params to Shop Floor Controls"] ``` ## 4.4 Inputs vs Outputs of the QFD Process | Input Layer | Process Stage | Output Layer | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Voice of Customer (VOC) | Customer Needs Analysis | Prioritized CRs (WHATs) | | CRs + Engineering Inputs | HOQ Matrix Construction | Weighted TRs (HOWs) + Targets | | TRs | Parts Deployment | Critical Part Characteristics | | Part Characteristics | Process Planning | Process Parameters, Control Limits | | Process Parameters | Production Planning | Operator Work Instructions, QC Checkpoints | > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Viva:** *"Voice → WHATs → HOWs → Parts → Process → Production"* — the **6-word QFD deployment chain**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Mention the origin and the two key elements of QFD.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1 mark) **Definition:** QFD is a structured planning methodology that translates customer requirements (WHATs) into specific technical/engineering requirements (HOWs) throughout the stages of product development. - (1 mark) **Origin:** QFD was developed in **1972** in **Japan** by **Dr. Yoji Akao** at Mitsubishi Heavy Industries; it was introduced to the West in 1988 by **Hauser and Clausing**. - (1 mark) **Two key elements:** (i) The **House of Quality (HOQ)** matrix, and (ii) the **Four-Phase Deployment Model** (Product, Parts, Process, Production Planning). --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List and briefly explain the four phases of QFD deployment.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1 mark) **Phase 1 – Product Planning:** Translates customer requirements (CRs) into technical requirements (TRs) using the House of Quality. - (1 mark) **Phase 2 – Parts Deployment:** Identifies which critical parts/components contribute to each TR. - (1 mark) **Phase 3 – Process Planning:** Determines the manufacturing process parameters and machine settings needed to meet the part specifications. - (Bonus coverage – note for completion) **Phase 4 – Production Planning:** Establishes day-to-day operating instructions, quality controls, and inspection standards for the shop floor. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse *Importance Rating* with *Row Weight*. IR is the *raw customer score*; Row Weight is the *normalized* (divided by sum) version. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** The Relationship matrix uses **discrete values (9, 3, 1)**, NOT percentages or decimals. Using 0.9, 0.3, 0.1 by mistake will yield a totally wrong absolute weight — examiners deduct **3 full marks**. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to *rank* TRs after computing relative weights. A HOQ without a priority rank is incomplete. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In QFD theory questions, students forget to draw/label the **Roof (Correlation Matrix)**. Always mention it. > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Confusing **Voice of Customer (VOC)** with **Customer Requirements (CRs)** — VOC is the *raw* data; CRs are the *engineered, refined* list. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Draw the **House of Quality (HOQ)** and label all its components. Explain the function of each component in the QFD process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The following data is collected for a scooter design team: | CR | Description | IR | | :--- | :--- | :---: | | CR1 | Easy to Handle | 4 | | CR2 | High Mileage | 5 | | CR3 | Stylish Looks | 3 | | CR4 | Low Maintenance | 4 | **Relationship Matrix:** | | TR1 (Kerb Weight) | TR2 (Engine CC) | TR3 (Body Finish) | TR4 (Service Interval) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | CR1 | 9 | 3 | 1 | 1 | | CR2 | 9 | 9 | 1 | 3 | | CR3 | 1 | 1 | 9 | 1 | | CR4 | 3 | 1 | 1 | 9 | **Compute the Absolute and Relative Weights of each Technical Requirement. Rank the TRs in order of engineering priority.** **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — House of Quality (7 marks):** Draw the HOQ with **six labelled components** (2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explanation): 1. **Customer Requirements (WHATs) – Left Wall:** Prioritized, refined customer needs with importance ratings. **Function:** Anchors the design to the voice of the customer. (1 mark) 2. **Technical Requirements (HOWs) – Ceiling/Top:** Measurable engineering descriptors. **Function:** Provides the language of engineering implementation. (1 mark) 3. **Relationship Matrix – Center:** Strength of correlation between each WHAT and HOW. **Function:** Identifies which TRs most strongly satisfy each CR. (1 mark) 4. **Roof of the House – Top Triangle:** Correlation among HOWs. **Function:** Reveals engineering trade-offs and synergies before design commitment. (1 mark) 5. **Customer Importance Ratings & Competitive Benchmarking – Left & Bottom:** Importance weights and market comparison scores. **Function:** Prioritizes CRs and contextualizes the design against competition. (1 mark) 6. **Technical Importance Weights & Target Values – Right & Bottom:** Absolute/relative weights and target specifications. **Function:** Drives R&D resource allocation and design specifications. (1 mark) > Additional mention: Competitive assessment rows (1 mark) for completeness. **Part (b) — Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1: Row Weights (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum IR &= 4 + 5 + 3 + 4 = 16 \\ RW_1 &= 4/16 = 0.250 \\ RW_2 &= 5/16 = 0.3125 \\ RW_3 &= 3/16 = 0.1875 \\ RW_4 &= 4/16 = 0.250 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Absolute Weights (3 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} AW_{TR1} &= (0.250 \times 9) + (0.3125 \times 9) + (0.1875 \times 1) + (0.250 \times 3) \\ &= 2.250 + 2.8125 + 0.1875 + 0.750 = 6.000 \\ \\ AW_{TR2} &= (0.250 \times 3) + (0.3125 \times 9) + (0.1875 \times 1) + (0.250 \times 1) \\ &= 0.750 + 2.8125 + 0.1875 + 0.250 = 4.000 \\ \\ AW_{TR3} &= (0.250 \times 1) + (0.3125 \times 1) + (0.1875 \times 9) + (0.250 \times 1) \\ &= 0.250 + 0.3125 + 1.6875 + 0.250 = 2.500 \\ \\ AW_{TR4} &= (0.250 \times 1) + (0.3125 \times 3) + (0.1875 \times 1) + (0.250 \times 9) \\ &= 0.250 + 0.9375 + 0.1875 + 2.250 = 3.625 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Relative Weights & Ranking (2 marks)** $$ \sum AW = 6.000 + 4.000 + 2.500 + 3.625 = 16.125 $$ $$ \begin{aligned} RelW_{TR1} &= \frac{6.000}{16.125} \times 100 = 37.21\% \\ RelW_{TR2} &= \frac{4.000}{16.125} \times 100 = 24.81\% \\ RelW_{TR3} &= \frac{2.500}{16.125} \times 100 = 15.50\% \\ RelW_{TR4} &= \frac{3.625}{16.125} \times 100 = 22.48\% \end{aligned} $$ **Final Ranking Table:** | Rank | TR | Technical Requirement | Relative Weight | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | | **1** | TR1 | Kerb Weight | **37.21%** | | 2 | TR2 | Engine CC | 24.81% | | 3 | TR4 | Service Interval | 22.48% | | 4 | TR3 | Body Finish | 15.50% | **Engineering Decision:** Engineering effort should be **maximized on TR1 (Kerb Weight)** — reducing weight has the highest cross-impact on all customer requirements. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **QFD Four-Phase Matrix Deployment Model** in detail. How does the House of Quality of one phase become the input of the next phase? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** concept. What are the **tools and techniques** used to capture VOC? Explain the **Affinity Diagram** and **Tree Diagram** methods used to refine VOC into Customer Requirements. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Four-Phase QFD Deployment (7 marks):** | Phase | Description | HOQ Inputs | HOQ Outputs | Marks | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | 1 | **Product Planning:** Translates customer requirements (CRs) into technical requirements (TRs). This is the *primary* HOQ. | WHATs = CRs | HOWs = TRs; Target values, weights | (2) | | 2 | **Parts Deployment:** Maps TRs from Phase 1 onto critical *part characteristics* of the product. | WHATs = TRs from Phase 1 | HOWs = Part-level engineering specs | (2) | | 3 | **Process Planning:** Decides which *manufacturing processes* and their parameters can achieve the part characteristics. | WHATs = Part characteristics | HOWs = Process parameters, tolerances, machine settings | (2) | | 4 | **Production Planning:** Sets *day-to-day operating instructions* and quality controls for the shop floor. | WHATs = Process parameters | HOWs = SOPs, QC checkpoints, Kaizen targets | (1) | **Cascade Logic (Bonus 1 mark):** The "HOWs" of Phase $i$ become the "WHATs" of Phase $i+1$, ensuring *unbroken traceability* from the customer's original voice to the final shop-floor instruction. **Part (b) — VOC and Refinement Tools (7 marks):** - (1 mark) **VOC Definition:** The raw, unfiltered, expressed and latent expectations, preferences, and aversions of customers regarding a product or service. - (2 marks) **VOC Capture Tools:** (i) Surveys & Questionnaires, (ii) Focus Groups, (iii) In-depth Customer Interviews, (iv) Complaint & Warranty Card Analysis, (v) Social Media & Online Review Mining, (vi) Ethnographic Observation, (vii) Conjoint Analysis. - (2 marks) **Affinity Diagram:** VOC statements (often 50–200 raw items) are written on cards. A cross-functional team silently groups similar cards into **clusters (affinities)** under natural category headings. It is a *divergent-then-convergent* technique to convert chaos into logical groupings (e.g., "Comfort", "Economy", "Aesthetics"). - (2 marks) **Tree Diagram (Logic Tree):** The customer requirements (WHATs) from the Affinity Diagram are arranged in a **hierarchical tree** — primary needs at the top, secondary needs branching below, tertiary needs at the leaves. This is the refined, structured input to the House of Quality. **Conclusion:** Affinity + Tree Diagrams together transform **chaotic VOC data → structured Customer Requirements** ready for QFD deployment. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Bullet the following before the exam:** - **QFD** = Quality Function Deployment — translates *Voice of Customer* into *Engineering Specs*. - **Origin:** **Yoji Akao (Japan, 1972)**; popularized by **Hauser & Clausing (1988)**. - **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the visual matrix of QFD; it has **6 building blocks** (CRs, TRs, Relationship Matrix, Roof, Importance Weights, Competitive Benchmarking). - **WHATs** = Customer Requirements (CRs); **HOWs** = Technical Requirements (TRs). - **Relationship Strength Symbols:** 9 (Strong ●), 3 (Medium ○), 1 (Weak △), Blank (None). - **Roof Symbols:** ++, +, – –, – show **synergy or conflict** among HOWs. - **Row Weight:** $RW_j = IR_j \div \sum IR$ — *normalizes* importance to sum to 1. - **Absolute Weight:** $AW_i = \sum (RW_j \times R_{ij})$ — *weighted sum* of relationships. - **Relative Weight:** $RelW_i = (AW_i / \sum AW) \times 100\%$ — *ranks* TRs in %. - **Four Phases of QFD Deployment:** **Product → Parts → Process → Production** Planning. - **Phase 1 HOQ** is the most important — it directly links customer to engineering. - **Output of Phase $i$ = Input of Phase $i+1$** (cascade principle). - **VOC Capture Tools:** Surveys, focus groups, interviews, complaint analysis, social media mining. - **VOC Refinement Tools:** **Affinity Diagram** (clustering) → **Tree Diagram** (hierarchy). - **Benefits of QFD:** Reduces design changes by 30–50%, shortens development cycle, improves cross-functional communication, ensures customer-focus. - **Limitation:** Time-consuming to build; can become a "wallpaper" exercise if not driven by real data. - **Most-asked KTU question:** *"Explain the House of Quality with a neat diagram."* (3–7 marks). - **Numerical question pattern:** Given IRs and a 9-3-1 matrix → compute **AW and RelW** and **rank TRs**. > **End of Notes — Module 4 / QFD Process — PECHT413 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Benefits" Mean in TQM Context?

In the context of **PECHT413 – Total Quality Management**, Module 4 generally covers advanced TQM frameworks and systems, namely **Six Sigma**, **Benchmarking**, **Quality Auditing**, and the **ISO 9000 Quality Management System (QMS)**. The topic "Benefits" refers to the **tangible and intangible advantages** that an organization derives from the successful implementation of these quality frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Benefits* in TQM refer to the measurable improvements in **product quality, process efficiency, customer satisfaction, cost reduction, employee engagement, and competitive advantage** that an organization achieves through systematic deployment of quality tools such as Six Sigma, Benchmarking, Quality Audits, and ISO 9000-based QMS.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Health Check-up" of an Organization

Imagine your body as an **organization**:
- **Six Sigma** is like a **personalized fitness regime** — strict, data-driven, and aimed at achieving "near-perfect health."
- **Benchmarking** is like **comparing your fitness levels with elite athletes** to set realistic goals.
- **Quality Audits** are **annual medical check-ups** that verify your body is functioning correctly.
- **ISO 9000 Certification** is like a **government-issued health certificate** that assures others (customers) that you are fit and reliable.

**The "Benefits"** = the outcomes of this holistic health regimen: **longer life, lower medical bills, higher energy, better reputation, and trust from everyone around you.**

## 1.3 Three Major Categories of Benefits

> [!NOTE]
> All quality frameworks in Module 4 deliver benefits that fall into three master categories:

| Category | Description | Example Metric |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Tangible Benefits** | Quantifiable, measurable in money or numbers | Cost reduction of **15–30%**, defect rate drop to **3.4 DPMO** |
| **Intangible Benefits** | Qualitative, strategic, long-term value | Brand reputation, employee morale, customer loyalty |
| **Strategic Benefits** | Long-term competitive positioning | Market share growth, regulatory compliance, global access |

## 1.4 Key Constant — The Six Sigma Benchmark

The most important numerical anchor in Module 4 is the **Six Sigma Quality Level**:

$$\text{Six Sigma Quality} = 3.4 \text{ Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)}$$

This represents a process operating at **99.99966% accuracy** — the gold standard that organizations chase, and the central metric around which the "benefits" of Six Sigma are calculated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Benefits of Six Sigma

Six Sigma, developed by **Motorola in 1986** and popularized by **General Electric (under Jack Welch)**, delivers benefits in five principal streams:

### A. Financial / Cost Benefits
- **Reduction in Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — internal failure costs (rework, scrap) and external failure costs (warranty, returns) plummet.
- Typical reported savings: **$100,000 to $1 million per project** in medium-scale industries.
- ROI on Six Sigma deployment: often **10:1 to 30:1** within 3 years.

### B. Operational Benefits
- Cycle-time reduction (often **30–50%**).
- Yield improvement.
- Capacity release without capital expenditure.

### C. Customer Benefits
- Improved product reliability → higher **Net Promoter Score (NPS)**.
- Lower warranty claims.
- Faster complaint resolution.

### D. Cultural / Human Resource Benefits
- Creation of a structured problem-solving culture.
- Career progression through **Belt Hierarchy** (Green Belt, Black Belt, Master Black Belt).
- Empowered, data-driven workforce.

### E. Strategic Benefits
- Sustainable competitive differentiation.
- Foundation for **Lean Six Sigma** integration.
- Long-term shareholder value creation.

## 2.2 Benefits of Benchmarking

Benchmarking (formalized by **Robert C. Camp of Xerox** in the 1980s) provides:

1. **Identification of Performance Gaps** — quantifies where you stand vs. industry leaders.
2. **Adoption of Best Practices** — accelerates learning by leveraging proven methods.
3. **Goal Setting Based on Reality** — sets realistic, evidence-based targets rather than arbitrary ones.
4. **Culture of Continuous Improvement** — fosters humility and external orientation.
5. **Customer Satisfaction Improvement** — by emulating industry-leading service standards.

## 2.3 Benefits of Quality Auditing

Quality audits (Internal, External, Third-party/Registration) deliver:

- **Compliance Assurance** — meeting **ISO 9001**, statutory, and customer requirements.
- **Risk Identification** — proactive detection of non-conformities.
- **Process Maturity** — driving the organization up the **CMMI-style maturity ladder**.
- **Stakeholder Confidence** — for investors, regulators, and customers.
- **Continuous Improvement Trigger** — corrective and preventive action (CAPA) loop.

## 2.4 Benefits of ISO 9000 / QMS

The **ISO 9000 family** (first published in **1987**, revised in 1994, 2000, 2008, **2015**) brings:

- **International Credibility** — passport to global markets.
- **Customer Confidence** — assurance of consistent quality.
- **Process Standardization** — eliminates tribal knowledge, codifies best practices.
- **Reduced Wastage** — fewer reworks and rejections.
- **Improved Communication** — common language across departments and supply chains.
- **Legal and Regulatory Compliance** — baseline for many industry-specific standards (e.g., **AS9100** for aerospace, **ISO/TS 16949** for automotive).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Unit |
|---|-------------------|------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Defects Per Million Opportunities** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^{6}$ | Six Sigma benefit quantification (defects/M) |
| 2 | **Process Sigma Level** | $\text{Sigma Level} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2 \cdot \sigma}$ | Quality level measurement |
| 3 | **Yield (First Pass Yield)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units (First Pass)}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100\%$ | Operational benefit (in %) |
| 4 | **Rolled Throughput Yield** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | Multi-stage process benefit (in %) |
| 5 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Prevention Cost}$ | Financial benefit (in currency) |
| 6 | **Return on Quality (ROQ)** | $\text{ROQ} = \dfrac{\text{Quality-Related Savings}}{\text{Quality Investment}} \times 100\%$ | Strategic benefit metric (in %) |
| 7 | **Six Sigma Goal** | $\text{DPMO} \leq 3.4$ | Target benchmark (defects/M) |
| 8 | **Customer Satisfaction Index** | $\text{CSI} = \dfrac{\text{Sum of Satisfaction Scores}}{\text{Maximum Possible Score}} \times 100$ | Customer benefit (in %) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the bedrock of any **Quality Engineer / Black Belt's dashboard** in manufacturing (automotive, aerospace, electronics), service industries (BPOs, banking, healthcare), and even software industries (CMM/CMMI, Agile quality metrics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of DPMO (Six Sigma Core Metric)

The derivation follows the philosophy that **every product has multiple "opportunities" to fail**, and we want to standardize defect measurement across products of different complexity.

### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let:
- $D$ = Total number of defects observed
- $U$ = Total number of units produced
- $O$ = Number of opportunities for defect per unit
- $N$ = Total opportunities = $U \times O$

### Step 2: Express Defects as a Ratio

The fraction of opportunities that resulted in defects:

$$\text{Defect Rate} = \frac{D}{N} = \frac{D}{U \times O}$$

This is a probability (dimensionless, between 0 and 1).

### Step 3: Scale to "Per Million" for Readability

Multiply by $10^6$ so even tiny probabilities become readable whole numbers:

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^{6}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

> **Problem:** A circuit board manufacturer produces **50,000 units/month**. Each board has **120 soldering opportunities**. In a month, **85 defects** are found. Calculate DPMO.

**Solution:**

Given: $D = 85$, $U = 50000$, $O = 120$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{85}{50000 \times 120} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{85}{6{,}000{,}000} \times 10^{6} \\
&= \frac{85 \times 10^{6}}{6 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{85}{6} \\
&= 14.17 \text{ DPMO}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The process is operating at approximately **5.6 Sigma**, which is **far superior** to the industry average of 3–4 Sigma.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)

COPQ quantifies the financial benefit of TQM implementation. It is the sum of two cost families:

### Step 1: Failure Costs (Costs Arising from Defects)

- **Internal Failure Costs:** Scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime caused by defects found in-house.
- **External Failure Costs:** Warranty claims, returns, recalls, litigation, lost goodwill.

### Step 2: Non-Failure Costs (Costs Incurred Regardless)

- **Appraisal Costs:** Inspection, testing, calibration, quality audits.
- **Prevention Costs:** Training, quality planning, supplier capability development, design reviews.

### Step 3: Total COPQ Formula

$$\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{Internal}} + C_{\text{External}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Prevention}}$$

### Step 4: Worked Example

> **Problem:** A company reports:
> - Internal failure costs = ₹ 8,00,000
> - External failure costs = ₹ 12,00,000
> - Appraisal costs = ₹ 3,00,000
> - Prevention costs = ₹ 2,00,000
>
> The company invests ₹ 5,00,000 in a new Six Sigma project that **reduces internal failure by 40%** and **external failure by 30%**.
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Initial COPQ
> (b) COPQ after Six Sigma
> (c) Return on Quality (ROQ)

**Solution:**

**(a) Initial COPQ:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{initial}} &= 8{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 25{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) COPQ after Six Sigma project:**

- New Internal Failure Cost = $8{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.40) = 4{,}80{,}000$
- New External Failure Cost = $12{,}00{,}000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 8{,}40{,}000$
- Appraisal and Prevention costs remain (these are fixed investments, not reduced by the project; assume they double as the company invests more):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ}_{\text{new}} &= 4{,}80{,}000 + 8{,}40{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 18{,}20{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Return on Quality (ROQ):**

Savings = $25{,}00{,}000 - 18{,}20{,}000 = 6{,}80{,}000$

Investment = ₹ 5,00,000

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ROQ} &= \frac{\text{Savings}}{\text{Investment}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6{,}80{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 136\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The company recovered **₹ 1.36 for every ₹ 1 invested** — a healthy, bankable TQM project.

---

## 3.3 Six Sigma Level Conversion Table (Standard Memorization Table)

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Defect Rate (%) |
|:-----------:|:----:|:---------:|:---------------:|
| 1σ | 691,462 | 30.85 | 69.15 |
| 2σ | 308,538 | 69.15 | 30.85 |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32 | 6.68 |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38 | 0.62 |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977 | 0.023 |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966** | **0.00034** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Exams:** Every increase of 1 Sigma level **roughly reduces DPMO by an order of magnitude** (10× improvement). Moving from 3σ to 6σ cuts defects by **~20,000 times**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
