## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Q1. Define Quality System. List any two ISO 9000 family standards.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Quality System (QS)** is a formalized set of interrelated processes, procedures, and resources that an organization uses to direct and control activities related to quality, in line with **ISO 9000:2015**.

Two ISO 9000 family standards:

1. **ISO 9000:2015** — *Quality management systems — Fundamentals and vocabulary* (defines terms and principles).
2. **ISO 9001:2015** — *Quality management systems — Requirements* (the certifiable standard).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each standard — 3 marks total.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q2. What is a Quality Manual? Mention its typical contents.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Quality Manual** is a Tier-1 document that specifies the scope, exclusions, and documented information of an organization's QMS.

Typical contents:
1. **Title, scope, and applicability** of the QMS.
2. **Quality Policy** and **Quality Objectives**.
3. **Description of interactions** between QMS processes.
4. **Reference to documented procedures** and their locations.
5. **Document control information** — issue number, approval authority.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for contents — 3 marks total.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### **Question A: Choice 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain the seven stages of Quality System implementation in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The seven stages of Quality System implementation (e.g., **ISO 9001:2015**) are:

**Stage 1 — Top Management Decision & Commitment (1 Mark):**
Management decides to pursue QMS certification. A **Management Representative (MR)** is appointed. A formal **Quality Policy** is drafted, committing the organization to compliance.

**Stage 2 — Gap Analysis (1 Mark):**
A clause-by-clause comparison is done between current practices and the requirements of ISO 9001:2015. The output is a **Gap Analysis Report** listing:
- Current vs. required state
- Specific gaps per clause
- Prioritized action plan

**Stage 3 — Documentation (1 Mark):**
The four-tier documentation hierarchy is created:
- **Quality Manual** — top-level
- **Procedures (QSPs)** — process-level
- **Work Instructions / SOPs** — task-level
- **Records / Forms** — evidence

**Stage 4 — Training & Awareness (1 Mark):**
All employees are trained. Internal auditors receive specialized training (16–24 hours). Effectiveness is measured by **TE (Training Effectiveness)**.

**Stage 5 — Implementation & Operation (1 Mark):**
The system is run as documented. PDCA is applied at every process level. Records are generated and maintained.

**Stage 6 — Internal Audit & Management Review (1 Mark):**
Internal audits identify **Major NCs, Minor NCs, Observations, and OFIs**. Management Review (Clause 9.3) is conducted with mandatory inputs.

**Stage 7 — Certification Audit (1 Mark):**
External auditors from an accredited CB conduct **Stage-1 (documentation)** and **Stage-2 (on-site)** audits. Upon successful compliance, an **ISO 9001:2015 certificate valid for 3 years** is awarded. Surveillance audits occur annually.

*(Award 1 mark per stage with brief explanation — 7 marks total.)*

---

**(b) Discuss the documentation hierarchy of a Quality Management System. Why is documentation important? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The documentation hierarchy of a QMS follows a four-tier pyramid:

**Tier 1 — Quality Manual (1 Mark):**
The top-level document describing the QMS scope, exclusions, processes, and their interactions. It references all Tier 2 procedures.

**Tier 2 — Procedures / QSPs (1 Mark):**
Documented procedures for each major activity. Examples include:
- Control of Documents
- Control of Records
- Internal Audit
- Corrective & Preventive Action
- Management Review

**Tier 3 — Work Instructions / SOPs (1 Mark):**
Detailed, step-by-step instructions for specific tasks performed by operators or technicians.

**Tier 4 — Records / Forms (1 Mark):**
Objective evidence of activities performed — e.g., inspection reports, calibration logs, training records, audit reports.

**Why Documentation is Important (3 Marks):**

1. **Clarity & Consistency:** Ensures every employee performs tasks the same way regardless of location or shift.
2. **Knowledge Retention:** Acts as a repository so that procedures are not lost when employees leave.
3. **Auditability:** Auditors verify compliance only through documented evidence. Without records, there is *no proof* of compliance.
4. **Training Aid:** Documents serve as references for new employee onboarding.
5. **Continual Improvement:** Records enable trend analysis — a core requirement of **Clause 9.1.3** of ISO 9001:2015.
6. **Risk Mitigation:** Documented procedures reduce the risk of failure from human error.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students write *only* the four tiers without explaining *why* documentation is required. KTU examiners explicitly award marks for justification — explaining **Clarity, Auditability, Knowledge Retention, and Risk Mitigation** is essential for full marks.

---

#### **Question B: Choice 2 (14 Marks) — Alternative Option**

**(a) What is an Internal Quality Audit? Explain the audit process. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> An **Internal Quality Audit (IQA)** is a systematic, independent, and documented process conducted by the organization's own trained personnel to determine whether QMS activities comply with planned arrangements and ISO 9001:2015 requirements (Clause 9.2).

**Audit Process (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Audit Planning (1 Mark):**
Annual audit schedule is prepared. Audit criteria, scope, and frequency are decided. **Clause 9.2.2** requires:
- Defined frequency (at least once per year)
- Coverage of all clauses
- Risk-based scheduling

**Step 2 — Auditor Selection (1 Mark):**
Trained, competent auditors are assigned. Auditors must **not** audit their own work (independence requirement).

**Step 3 — Preparation (1 Mark):**
Audit checklists are prepared. Documents to be audited are reviewed. Opening meeting is scheduled.

**Step 4 — On-Site Audit (1 Mark):**
Opening meeting → document review → interviews with process owners → physical observation of activities → recording of evidence.

**Step 5 — Reporting (1 Mark):**
**Audit Report** with findings categorized as:
- **Major NC** — total absence of a required element
- **Minor NC** — partial or one-time failure
- **Observation (OB)** — potential issue, not yet a nonconformity
- **OFI** — opportunity for improvement

**Step 6 — Follow-Up (1 Mark):**
Corrective actions are taken for all NCs. Auditor verifies closure of NCs.

**Step 7 — Management Review Input (1 Mark):**
Audit results are presented as input to the **Management Review (Clause 9.3)**.

---

**(b) Differentiate between ISO 9001:2015 implementation and Six Sigma implementation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ISO 9001:2015 | Six Sigma |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | International (ISO) | Motorola (1986) — Bill Smith |
| **Focus** | Process standardization + risk management | Variation reduction + defect elimination |
| **Approach** | Process approach + PDCA | DMAIC (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) |
| **Scope** | Whole organization | Specific projects with measurable CTQs |
| **Duration** | 9–18 months for full implementation | 3–6 months per project |
| **Certification** | Yes — third-party certificate from CB | No formal certification; only **belt levels** (Green, Black, Master Black) |
| **Documentation** | Mandatory, four-tier hierarchy | Project-specific deliverables |
| **Metric** | Process conformity, customer satisfaction | DPMO, Sigma level (Z), Cpk |
| **Outcome** | Certified QMS, market credibility | Cost savings, defect reduction |
| **Suitable For** | Any industry — manufacturing, services, education | Repetitive processes with measurable defects |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU):** Students often describe ISO 9001 and Six Sigma *separately* but fail to draw a **side-by-side comparison**. KTU 14-mark questions on comparison *require* a comparison table for full marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in PECHT413 Module 4 — Implementation of Quality System:**
> 1. **Conflating ISO 9000 with ISO 9001.** ISO 9000 is *vocabulary*; ISO 9001 is *requirements*. Writing the wrong one loses **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting Clause 4.1 and 4.2 (Context of Organization).** This is the *new* addition in ISO 9001:2015 and KTU's most-tested clause in 2023–2024.
> 3. **Skipping PDCA integration in answers.** Every QMS process must reference PDCA; examiners award **1–2 marks** for explicit PDCA linkage.
> 4. **Omitting the difference between Major and Minor NCs.** When asked about audit findings, students *must* distinguish.
> 5. **Writing 7 stages in random order.** The order is fixed: **Top Management → Gap Analysis → Documentation → Training → Implementation → Internal Audit → Certification.**
> 6. **Not mentioning PDCA, Risk-Based Thinking, and Customer Focus** as the *philosophy* of ISO 9001:2015.
> 7. **Generic answers without examples.** Use a real-world example (e.g., a hospital implementing NABH, or an engineering college implementing NAAC) to score above 80%.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PECHT413 Module 4:**

- ✅ **Quality System** = formalized, documented set of processes, procedures, and resources to direct & control quality activities (ISO 9000:2015).
- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015** has **10 clauses**; only **Clauses 4–10 are auditable**.
- ✅ **7 Quality Management Principles** = Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management.
- ✅ **7 Implementation Stages** = Top Management Decision → Gap Analysis → Documentation → Training → Implementation → Internal Audit & Management Review → Certification Audit.
- ✅ **Documentation Pyramid** = Quality Manual (Tier 1) → Procedures (Tier 2) → SOPs/Work Instructions (Tier 3) → Records/Forms (Tier 4).
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** = Plan → Do → Check → Act. Applied at *every* process level in ISO 9001:2015.
- ✅ **Internal Audit Findings** = Major NC, Minor NC, Observation, OFI.
- ✅ **Management Review (Clause 9.3)** is conducted by **top management** with **mandatory inputs** (audit results, customer satisfaction, process performance, etc.).
- ✅ **CMMI has 5 levels** = Initial → Managed → Defined → Quantitatively Managed → Optimizing.
- ✅ **Six Sigma uses DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.
- ✅ **Kaizen** = small, continuous, daily improvements; *no formal certification*.
- ✅ **ISO 9001 certificate validity = 3 years**; **surveillance audit = annually**.
- ✅ **Cost of Quality (CoQ) = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure**.
- ✅ **COPQ** = Internal + External failure cost (industry KPI as % of sales).
- ✅ **Risk-Based Thinking** is the *new* philosophy introduced in ISO 9001:2015.
- ✅ **KTU's favorite comparative question** = ISO 9001 vs. Six Sigma, ISO 9001 vs. CMMI, ISO 9000 vs. ISO 9001.

> 🎯 **Topper's Quick Tip:** Always anchor your answer to **ISO 9001:2015 specifically** (not generic ISO). Use **Clause numbers** to show technical depth — examiners instantly recognize the difference between a 12-mark and a 14-mark answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Documentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Documentation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, **Documentation** refers to the structured, controlled set of written, electronic, or visual records that describe, direct, standardize, and provide evidence of the policies, processes, procedures, and activities performed within an organization's Quality Management System (QMS). As per **ISO 9001:2015** (the global benchmark cited in the KTU syllabus), documentation serves three primary purposes: 1. **Communication of Information** — transmitting the intent and direction of management. 2. **Evidence of Conformity** — demonstrating that planned activities actually occurred. 3. **Knowledge Sharing & Preservation** — retaining organizational knowledge across personnel changes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Documentation in TQM is the systematic creation, control, and maintenance of quality records, manuals, procedures, and work instructions that ensure consistency, traceability, and continuous improvement across all levels of the organization.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Cockpit Manual" Intuition Imagine you are boarding a commercial airplane. Before every flight, the pilots do **not** rely on memory. They follow a precisely documented **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) Checklist**: pre-flight inspection, fuel check, runway alignment, takeoff sequence. Every step is documented, version-controlled, and signed. **Why?** Because: - A tired pilot on a red-eye flight must perform identically to a fresh pilot at dawn. - If something goes wrong, investigators must trace *exactly* what was followed. - A new co-pilot must learn the airline's exact process — not guess it. **Documentation in TQM is the same concept applied to an organization.** It is the airline's manual that ensures every employee, every shift, every branch delivers the **same quality, the same way, every time** — regardless of who is on duty. Without documentation, quality becomes dependent on memory, mood, or individual skill — which is the **enemy of consistency**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Insight:** Documentation does *not* replace people — it **empowers** people by removing guesswork, enabling training, and creating an auditable trail of evidence. ## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Documentation Although Documentation is qualitative, certain **standard metrics** appear in KTU questions: | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Meaning | |---|---|---| | Documentation Levels | L | **4 levels** (Manual → Procedures → Work Instructions → Records) | | ISO 9001 Required Documents | D | **7 mandatory document types** (per ISO 9001:2015) | | Document Review Cycle | Tr | Typically **annually** or after major process change | | Retention Period | R | **Minimum 3 years** for most quality records (industry-dependent) | > [!IMPORTANT] > Remember these as the "**4-7-3 rule**" — 4 levels, 7 mandatory ISO documents, 3-year minimum retention. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet ## 2.1 Why Documentation is the Backbone of TQM Documentation is **not** bureaucracy — under TQM, it is the nervous system that transmits quality intent. The reasons KTU emphasizes this topic: ### 2.1.1 The Five Pillars of TQM Documentation 1. **Consistency (Repeatability):** A documented process yields the same output regardless of operator. *Statistical Process Control* requires documented procedures to be effective. 2. **Training & Onboarding:** New employees learn faster from documented work instructions than from shadowing. 3. **Traceability:** Every defect, customer complaint, or audit finding can be traced back to a documented baseline. 4. **Auditability:** Internal and external auditors (e.g., ISO certification bodies) demand documented evidence. 5. **Continual Improvement (Kaizen):** Without a baseline document, you cannot measure improvement. *"You cannot improve what you have not documented."* ## 2.2 The Four-Level Documentation Hierarchy This is the **most-tested concept** in KTU exams. Every organization following ISO 9001 organizes documentation into four hierarchical tiers: | Level | Document Type | Audience | Content | Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Level 1** | **Quality Manual** | Top Management, Customers, Auditors | Quality policy, objectives, scope of QMS, exclusions, procedures map | XYZ Pvt Ltd Quality Manual v3.2 | | **Level 2** | **Quality Procedures (SOPs)** | Middle Management, Process Owners | "What" and "Who" — describes process flow, responsibilities, references to work instructions | SOP for Incoming Material Inspection | | **Level 3** | **Work Instructions** | Shop-floor Operators, Engineers | "How" — step-by-step task details, tools, parameters, safety | WI: How to calibrate a pressure gauge | | **Level 4** | **Records / Forms** | Quality Department, Auditors | Objective evidence that activities were performed | Inspection reports, test certificates, calibration logs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Manual Plans, Procedure Directs, Instruction Executes, Record Proves"* — **M-P-D-I-P**. ## 2.3 The Seven Mandatory Documents (ISO 9001:2015) The KTU syllabus explicitly tests this list. While ISO 9001:2015 became more flexible (allowing electronic formats), these documents are still essentially required: 1. **Scope of the QMS** (Clause 4.3) 2. **Quality Policy** (Clause 5.2) 3. **Quality Objectives** (Clause 6.2) 4. **Criteria for Evaluation and Selection of Suppliers** (Clause 8.4) 5. **Documented Information Required by ISO 9001** (general) 6. **Internal Audit Programme and Results** (Clause 9.2) 7. **Evidence of the Results of Management Review** (Clause 9.3) > [!NOTE] > Earlier versions (ISO 9001:2008) listed **6 mandatory procedures**: Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, Nonconformity, Corrective Action, Preventive Action. The 2015 version merged many but KTU may still ask the 2008 list. ## 2.4 Document Control — The Living System A document without control is just paper. The **Document Control Cycle** has six stages: 1. **Creation / Identification** — Author identifies need, drafts the document. 2. **Review** — Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) verify accuracy. 3. **Approval & Authorization** — Designated authority signs off (often Quality Manager + Process Owner). 4. **Distribution & Access Control** — Released to relevant personnel; obsolete copies withdrawn. 5. **Storage, Retrieval & Preservation** — Maintained in secure, retrievable form (physical or digital). 6. **Disposal / Archival of Obsolete Documents** — Old versions marked "OBSOLETE" or "SUPERSEDED" to prevent misuse. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Documentation Audit Metrics Although qualitative, KTU sometimes tests these quantitative aspects of documentation effectiveness: $$ \text{Documentation Coverage Index (DCI)} = \frac{\text{Number of documented critical processes}}{\text{Total number of critical processes identified}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Document Compliance Rate (DCR)} = \frac{\text{Number of activities executed as per documented procedure}}{\text{Total number of activities audited}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Document Update Frequency (DUF)} = \frac{\text{Number of document revisions in a period}}{\text{Time period (years)}} $$ $$ \text{Record Retrieval Time (RRT)} = \text{Average time to retrieve any quality record on demand} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU Part B questions, you may be asked to "design a documentation system" — always present the **4-tier hierarchy** first, then map the 6-stage control cycle. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Documentation Application | |---|---| | **Mechanical Manufacturing** | CNC program sheets, machining parameter logs, tool calibration records | | **Civil Engineering** | Mix design reports, slump test records, reinforcement inspection logs | | **Electrical Engineering** | PCB testing protocols, transformer oil test certificates, earthing records | | **Software Engineering** | Requirements specs, test cases, change-request logs (still applies TQM principles) | | **Biotechnology / Pharma** | Batch manufacturing records, validation protocols — **regulated by law** | | **Automotive (IATF 16949)** | PPAP, APQP, FMEA, Control Plans — all forms of documentation | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Document Creation & Control Workflow ## 3.1 Exhaustive 6-Stage Document Creation & Control Algorithm Below is the **complete** step-by-step logic for managing a document in a TQM environment. KTU questions often test this as a process flow question (14 marks). ### Stage 1 — Document Request & Identification $$ \text{Step 1.1: Need Identified} \rightarrow \text{Trigger} = \{\text{Audit Finding, Process Change, New Product, Customer Complaint, Regulatory Update}\} $$ A need is identified by a Process Owner or Quality Manager. The trigger could be: - A nonconformity report revealing that the current procedure is unclear. - A new regulatory requirement (e.g., updated BIS standard). - Introduction of a new product line. The Process Owner then submits a **Document Request Form (DRF)** with: - Proposed document title. - Reason for new/revised document. - Target audience and level (1, 2, 3, or 4). - Linked process and ISO 9001 clause (if applicable). ### Stage 2 — Drafting $$ \text{Step 2.1: Author Drafts} \rightarrow \text{Format} = \{\text{Title, Doc No., Rev No., Issue Date, Effective Date, Purpose, Scope, Procedure, References, Revision History}\} $$ The author drafts the document following a **standard template** approved by the Quality Department. Standard headers must include: | Header Field | Purpose | |---|---| | Document Number | Unique identifier (e.g., QP-MFG-001) | | Revision Number | Begins at 00, increments per change | | Issue Date | Date document was approved | | Effective Date | Date document becomes operational | | Page X of Y | Prevents partial use of multi-page docs | | Prepared / Reviewed / Approved | Three signature blocks | ### Stage 3 — Review $$ \text{Step 3.1: Cross-Functional Review} \rightarrow \text{Reviewers} = \{\text{Quality Mgr, Process Owner, Safety Officer, Customer Rep (if applicable)}\} $$ The draft is circulated to at least **3 stakeholders** for technical accuracy, regulatory compliance, and usability. Review comments are logged in a **Document Review Sheet (DRS)**. > [!NOTE] > If reviewers disagree, a **Review Meeting** is convened. The author revises and resubmits — this loop may iterate **2–4 times** before consensus. ### Stage 4 — Approval & Authorization $$ \text{Step 4.1: Final Sign-off} \rightarrow \text{Authority Matrix} = \{\text{Level 1: MD/CEO; Level 2: Functional Head; Level 3: Supervisor; Level 4: Quality Exec}\} $$ Each document level has a defined approval authority (this itself is a documented procedure — *meta-documentation*). Once approved, the document receives: - A **unique document number**. - A **revision number** (starts at 00). - An **effective date**. ### Stage 5 — Distribution & Access Control $$ \text{Step 5.1: Controlled Distribution} \rightarrow \text{Distribution List (DL)} = \{\text{Authorized Personnel Only}\} $$ Distribution is done via: - **Hard copies:** Marked "CONTROLLED COPY — Copy No. X of Y" in red. A **Master List of Documents (MLD)** tracks who holds which copy. - **Electronic copies:** Stored on a secure server / DMS (Document Management System) with role-based access. Each download is logged. > [!IMPORTANT] > Uncontrolled copies (e.g., training handouts) must be marked **"UNCONTROLLED IF PRINTED"** — a standard ISO 9001 watermark. ### Stage 6 — Storage, Retrieval & Obsolescence $$ \text{Step 6.1: Active Storage} \rightarrow \text{Retention Period (R)} = \min\{\text{Regulatory Min, Business Need, Audit Cycle}\} $$ $$ \text{Step 6.2: Obsolescence Marking} \rightarrow \text{Obsolete Doc} = \{\text{Stamped "SUPERSEDED", Removed from Active DL, Archived for Minimum 3 Years}\} $$ Obsolete documents are: - **Withdrawn** from all points of use to prevent misuse. - **Archived** (often with a stamp "OBSOLETE" in diagonal red) for traceability — auditors may want to see *what was in force at the time* of a past incident. - Retained for the legally mandated retention period. ## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — Designing a Documentation System for a Manufacturing Firm **Scenario (KTU-style 14-mark question):** *"You are the Quality Manager of an automotive parts manufacturing company. Design a documentation system covering the four-tier hierarchy for the process of 'Incoming Material Inspection'. Show document control flow."* **Solution Outline (Step-by-Step):** **Step 1 — Identify the process scope and QMS clause link.** $$ \text{Process: Incoming Material Inspection} \rightarrow \text{ISO 9001 Clause 8.4 (Control of Externally Provided Processes, Products and Services)} $$ **Step 2 — Map the four-tier hierarchy for this specific process.** | Level | Document | Document No. | Owner | |---|---|---|---| | L1 | Quality Manual | QM-001 | MD | | L2 | SOP: Incoming Material Inspection | SOP-IMI-001 | Quality Manager | | L3 | WI: Visual Inspection of Steel Rods | WI-IMI-001 | Inspection Supervisor | | L4 | Inspection Records (Forms) | FRM-IMI-001 | Inspector | **Step 3 — Show the document control cycle.** $$ \text{Request (DRF)} \rightarrow \text{Draft} \rightarrow \text{Review (3 SMEs)} \rightarrow \text{Approve (QM Sign)} \rightarrow \text{Distribute (DL of 5 copies)} \rightarrow \text{Use} \rightarrow \text{Review Annually} \rightarrow \text{Update if Needed (Rev 01)} \rightarrow \text{Obsolete Old Copies} $$ **Step 4 — Document control responsibilities.** - **Author:** Inspection Supervisor. - **Reviewer:** Production Manager + Purchase Manager. - **Approver:** Quality Manager. - **Custodian:** Document Control Cell (DCC). - **Auditor:** Internal Quality Auditor (quarterly). This is the *gold-standard* structure KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark answer. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Four-Level Documentation Hierarchy (KTU-Favorite) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Level 1: Quality Manual
Defines WHAT the QMS does
Audience: Top Mgmt + Auditors] --> B[Level 2: Quality Procedures SOPs
Defines WHO does WHAT
Audience: Middle Mgmt + Process Owners] B --> C[Level 3: Work Instructions
Defines HOW to do the task
Audience: Shop floor + Operators] C --> D[Level 4: Records and Forms
Provides PROOF of execution
Audience: Quality Dept + Auditors] A -.->|Approved by MD| A1[Revision 00 to 99
Reviewed Annually] B -.->|Approved by Functional Head| B1[Linked to ISO Clause] C -.->|Approved by Supervisor| C1[Step by Step Format] D -.->|Approved by Quality Mgr| D1[Retained 3 Plus Years] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a47,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#2e75b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 style A1 fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#cccccc,color:#000000 style B1 fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#cccccc,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#cccccc,color:#000000 style D1 fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#cccccc,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Six-Stage Document Control Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Stage 1: Need Identification
DRF Raised] --> S2[Stage 2: Drafting
Standard Template Used] S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Cross Functional Review
Reviewers Logged in DRS] S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Approval and Authorization
Sign off by Authority] S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Controlled Distribution
Hardcopy or DMS with DL] S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Storage and Obsolescence
Active Use then Archive] S6 --> S7{Periodic Review
Annual or Post Incident} S7 -->|Needs Update| S2 S7 -->|Still Valid| S5 style S1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#c55a11,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style S3 fill:#ffc000,stroke:#bf8f00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style S4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#507e32,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style S5 fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style S6 fill:#7030a0,stroke:#4a1f6b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style S7 fill:#404040,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Documentation Interaction with TQM Elements (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUTS TO DOCUMENTATION SYSTEM"] I1[Customer Requirements] I2[Regulatory Standards] I3[ISO 9001 and Industry Standards] I4[Internal Process Knowledge] end subgraph CORE["DOCUMENTATION SYSTEM CORE"] D1[Document Creation Cell] D2[Document Control Cell] D3[Records Management Cell] D4[Internal Audit Cell] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUTS AND EVIDENCE"] O1[Quality Manual] O2[SOPs and Work Instructions] O3[Quality Records] O4[Audit Reports] end subgraph FEEDBACK["CONTINUAL IMPROVEMENT LOOP"] F1[Kaizen Suggestions] F2[CAPA Reports] F3[Management Review Input] end I1 --> D1 I2 --> D1 I3 --> D1 I4 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D2 --> D4 D2 --> O1 D2 --> O2 D3 --> O3 D4 --> O4 O1 -.-> F1 O3 -.-> F2 O4 -.-> F3 F1 --> D1 F2 --> D1 F3 --> D1 style I1 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#000000 style I2 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#000000 style I3 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#000000 style I4 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#5b9bd5,color:#000000 style D1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a47,color:#ffffff style D2 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a47,color:#ffffff style D3 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a47,color:#ffffff style D4 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a2a47,color:#ffffff style O1 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#507e32,color:#000000 style O2 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#507e32,color:#000000 style O3 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#507e32,color:#000000 style O4 fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#507e32,color:#000000 style F1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000 style F2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000 style F3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **"Define the term 'Documentation' as applied in TQM. List any two benefits of documentation."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Documentation in TQM refers to the **structured, controlled set of manuals, procedures, work instructions, and records** that describe the organization's Quality Management System (QMS), provide evidence of conformity, and facilitate communication of quality intent across all levels. **Benefits (1 mark — any two):** 1. Ensures **consistency and repeatability** of processes regardless of personnel changes. 2. Provides **objective evidence** for audits and certifications (ISO 9001). 3. Facilitates **training and knowledge preservation** within the organization. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **"Distinguish between a Quality Manual and a Work Instruction. Give one example of each."** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Quality Manual | Work Instruction | |---|---|---| | **Level** | Level 1 (Top) | Level 3 (Bottom) | | **Scope** | Entire QMS | Specific task | | **Audience** | Top management, auditors | Shop-floor operators | | **Content** | Policy, scope, structure | Step-by-step task details | | **Example** | *"XYZ Quality Manual Rev 3"* | *"WI-OP-007: How to operate the lathe"* | The Quality Manual states **what** the QMS does at a high level, while the Work Instruction describes **how** to perform a single specific task. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **"Explain the four-level documentation hierarchy in a Quality Management System. With a suitable example, describe the six-stage document control cycle. Highlight the importance of controlling obsolete documents."** #### **Part (a) — Four-Level Documentation Hierarchy (7 marks)** The four-level hierarchy is a structured approach to organize all QMS documentation, ensuring that information flows from broad policy to granular task-level detail. **Level 1 — Quality Manual (2 marks):** - The topmost document describing the organization's QMS scope, quality policy, objectives, and the structure of procedures. - Approved by top management (MD/CEO). - *Example:* "Acme Industries Quality Manual, Doc No. QM-001, Rev 04." **Level 2 — Quality Procedures / SOPs (2 marks):** - Document the "what" and "who" of each major process. - Cross-functional, process-level documents. - *Example:* "SOP for Incoming Material Inspection, Doc No. SOP-IMI-001, Rev 02." **Level 3 — Work Instructions (1.5 marks):** - Step-by-step instructions for performing specific tasks. - Operator-level, machine-specific, or task-specific. - *Example:* "WI-OP-007: Operating CNC Lathe – Mazak QT-200." **Level 4 — Records and Forms (1.5 marks):** - Objective evidence that activities were performed as documented. - Retained as proof for audits and traceability. - *Example:* "Daily CNC Production Log, Form No. FRM-OP-007." #### **Part (b) — Six-Stage Document Control Cycle + Obsolescence (7 marks)** | Stage | Activity | Responsible | |---|---|---| | 1 | Need identification (DRF raised) | Process Owner | | 2 | Drafting per standard template | Author | | 3 | Cross-functional review | SMEs | | 4 | Approval and authorization | Designated Authority | | 5 | Controlled distribution (hard/soft) | Document Control Cell | | 6 | Storage, retrieval, periodic review | Quality Department | **Importance of Controlling Obsolete Documents (2 marks):** 1. Prevents accidental use of outdated procedures, which could cause defects, safety issues, or non-compliance. 2. Retains traceability — auditors may need to verify *what procedure was in force* at the time of a past incident. 3. Standard practice: stamp "SUPERSEDED" diagonally in red, remove from all active distribution points, but archive for at least **3 years** (or as per regulatory requirement). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **"Discuss the documentation requirements as per ISO 9001:2015. Design a documentation system for the process of 'Customer Complaint Handling' in a manufacturing organization. List the records you would maintain."** #### **Part (a) — ISO 9001:2015 Documentation Requirements (7 marks)** **Introduction (1 mark):** ISO 9001:2015 follows a **risk-based approach** and is less prescriptive than ISO 9001:2008, allowing organizations flexibility in documentation format. However, certain **documented information** is mandatory. **Seven Mandatory Documents (5 marks):** 1. **Scope of the QMS** (Clause 4.3) — boundaries and applicability. 2. **Quality Policy** (Clause 5.2) — top management's commitment. 3. **Quality Objectives** (Clause 6.2) — measurable goals at relevant functions. 4. **Criteria for Evaluation and Selection of Suppliers** (Clause 8.4) — outsourcing controls. 5. **Documented Information** required to support QMS operation (general). 6. **Internal Audit Programme and Audit Results** (Clause 9.2) — must be retained. 7. **Results of Management Review** (Clause 9.3) — must be retained as evidence. **Significance (1 mark):** These documents collectively demonstrate that the organization has a planned, controlled, and evidence-based QMS aligned with international best practices. #### **Part (b) — Documentation System for Customer Complaint Handling (7 marks)** **Process Flow:** $$ \text{Customer Complaint} \rightarrow \text{Logging in CRM} \rightarrow \text{Acknowledge to Customer} \rightarrow \text{Root Cause Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Corrective Action} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Closure} \rightarrow \text{Feedback to Customer} $$ **Documentation Hierarchy:** | Level | Document | Doc No. | Owner | |---|---|---|---| | L1 | Quality Manual (refers to complaint process) | QM-001 | MD | | L2 | SOP: Customer Complaint Handling | SOP-CCH-001 | Quality Manager | | L3 | WI: How to Log a Complaint in CRM | WI-CCH-001 | CRM Administrator | | L4 | Complaint Register & Investigation Forms | FRM-CCH-001 to 005 | Quality Executive | **Records to be Maintained (2 marks):** 1. **Customer Complaint Register** — date, customer name, product, issue, status. 2. **Acknowledgment Letter / Email** — proof of receipt. 3. **Root Cause Analysis Report** — 5-Why or Fishbone analysis output. 4. **Corrective Action Report (CAR)** — action taken, responsibility, target date. 5. **Verification Record** — proof that corrective action was effective. 6. **Closure Report** — signed off by Quality Manager. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "Document" with "Record":** Documents (Manual, SOP, WI) tell people *what to do*. Records *prove* it was done. Mixing these loses marks. > 2. **Skipping the Distribution Control step:** Many students write 5 stages instead of 6. Always include **Distribution & Access Control** as a distinct stage. > 3. **Forgetting the Obsolete Document Rule:** Examiners specifically look for "stamped SUPERSEDED + archive retained for 3 years" — easy 2 marks lost. > 4. **Quoting the old ISO 9001:2008 list incorrectly:** The 2008 version had **6 mandatory procedures**; the 2015 version has **documented information** distributed across clauses. Mixing both without clarification loses marks. > 5. **Ignoring the PDCA link:** Documentation supports the **Plan–Do–Check–Act** cycle. Mentioning PDCA explicitly in any 14-mark answer adds depth. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Documentation in TQM** - ✅ **Definition:** Structured, controlled records that communicate, prove, and preserve QMS information. - ✅ **4-7-3 Rule:** 4 hierarchy levels, 7 mandatory ISO 9001:2015 documents, 3-year minimum retention. - ✅ **Hierarchy Mnemonic:** **M-P-D-I-P** = Manual → Procedure → (Work) Instruction → (Records prove it was done). - ✅ **Six Control Stages:** Need → Draft → Review → Approve → Distribute → Store/Obsolete. - ✅ **Obsolete Documents:** Mark SUPERSEDED + Archive (never destroy immediately). - ✅ **Controlled vs Uncontrolled Copy:** Controlled copies are numbered and tracked; uncontrolled copies bear the "UNCONTROLLED IF PRINTED" watermark. - ✅ **Master List of Documents (MLD):** Tracks all controlled copies — must always be up to date. - ✅ **Document Numbering Convention:** Unique ID like `SOP-MFG-001-Rev03` — Department-Type-Serial-Revision. - ✅ **PDCA Link:** Documentation supports every phase — Plan = Manual/SOPs, Do = Work Instructions, Check = Records, Act = CAPA Reports. - ✅ **ISO 9001:2015 Shift:** From "documents and records" to "documented information" — embracing both paper and electronic formats. - ✅ **Benefit Statement:** "You cannot improve what you have not documented." - ✅ **Audit Triggers Documentation:** Internal audits, customer audits, and certification audits all rely on documented evidence. - ✅ **Common KTU Keywords:** Traceability, auditability, version control, change control, distribution list, retention period, controlled copy.

topics.title: Quality Auditing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT413 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Quality Auditing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Quality Auditing** is defined as a *systematic, independent, and documented process* for obtaining audit evidence and evaluating it objectively against pre-determined audit criteria, to determine the extent to which the audit criteria are fulfilled, in accordance with **ISO 19011:2018** — the international standard governing *Management System Auditing*. According to KTU 2024 PECHT413 Module 4 syllabus, Quality Auditing is the cornerstone verification mechanism in TQM that confirms whether an organization's documented quality policies, procedures, and processes are actually being implemented as planned, and whether they remain effective in achieving quality objectives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **ISO 19011:2018 Definition (Verbatim):** *"An audit is a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining audit evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria are fulfilled."* The three foundational pillars of this definition are: - **Systematic** → Methodical, planned, structured approach - **Independent** → Free from bias and conflicts of interest - **Documented** → Evidence-based, traceable records ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: The "Health Check-up" Model** Think of a Quality Audit exactly like an annual medical check-up for a person: | Medical Check-up | Quality Audit | |---|---| | Patient's medical history | Organization's QMS documentation | | Doctor (independent specialist) | Trained, independent Auditor | | Diagnostic tests (blood, ECG) | Audit evidence collection (records, observations, interviews) | | Health parameters (BP, Sugar) | Audit criteria (ISO 9001 clauses) | | Medical report | Audit Report | | Prescribed treatment & follow-up | Corrective Action & Follow-up Audit | Just as a doctor objectively assesses whether your body conforms to healthy parameters, an auditor objectively assesses whether your organization conforms to quality standards. The audit is not a "police inspection" — it is a **diagnostic tool** that helps the organization improve. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights) - **Auditee** — The organization or person being audited. - **Audit Client** — The person or organization requesting an audit. - **Auditor** — The person conducting the audit. - **Audit Team** — One or more auditors performing an audit. - **Audit Criteria** — The set of policies, procedures, or requirements used as a reference (the "ruler"). - **Audit Evidence** — Records, statements of fact, or other verifiable information (the "measurement"). - **Audit Findings** — Results of the evaluation of collected audit evidence against audit criteria. - **Audit Conclusion** — Outcome of an audit after considering all findings. - **Conformity (Conformance)** — Fulfillment of a requirement. - **Nonconformity (NC)** — Non-fulfillment of a requirement. - **Observation** — A statement of fact made during an audit and substantiated by evidence. - **Major Nonconformity** — A nonconformity that affects the ability of the QMS to achieve intended results. - **Minor Nonconformity** — A nonconformity that does not affect the overall ability of the QMS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Memorize the difference between **Major** and **Minor** Nonconformity — this is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Principles of Auditing (ISO 19011:2018) The seven principles make auditing a reliable and credible tool. Every auditor is ethically bound to uphold these: 1. **Integrity** — The foundation of professionalism. Auditors must be honest, truthful, and fair. 2. **Fair Presentation** — Audit findings must accurately represent the actual conditions. No exaggeration or suppression. 3. **Due Professional Care** — Auditors must apply diligence and good judgment. 4. **Confidentiality** — Audit information must be kept secure and not disclosed to third parties. 5. **Independence** — Auditors must be free from bias and conflicts of interest. 6. **Evidence-based Approach** — Rational conclusions must be based on verifiable evidence. 7. **Risk-based Approach** — Audit focus should be on areas of highest risk and significance. ### 2.2 Types of Quality Audits (KTU High-Yield Classification) #### A. Based on Scope / Subject Matter | Audit Type | What is Audited | KTU Frequency in Exams | |---|---|---| | **System Audit** | The entire QMS against ISO 9001 | Very High | | **Process Audit** | A specific process (e.g., production, HR) | Very High | | **Product Audit** | A specific product against specifications | High | | **Service Audit** | A service against service standards | Medium | #### B. Based on Who Conducts the Audit | Audit Type | Conducted By | Purpose | Frequency | |---|---|---|---| | **First-Party Audit (Internal)** | Own organization's auditors | Self-assessment, improvement | Quarterly/Half-yearly | | **Second-Party Audit (External/Supplier)** | Customer or customer's representative | Supplier evaluation | Before contract, periodically | | **Third-Party Audit (Certification/Regulatory)** | Independent certification body (e.g., TÜV, BV) | Certification, regulatory | Annually (surveillance), every 3 years (recertification) | ### 2.3 The Six-Phase Audit Process (ISO 19011 Model) The audit process is divided into **6 distinct phases** — the KTU syllabus requires students to know each phase in detail. **Phase 1 — Initiating the Audit** - Appoint audit team leader - Determine audit scope, criteria, and objectives - Establish initial contact with auditee - Confirm feasibility of audit **Phase 2 — Preparing Audit Activities** - Document review (QMS manual, procedures) - Prepare audit plan (schedule, dates, areas) - Assign work to audit team members - Prepare working documents (checklists, forms) **Phase 3 — Conducting the Audit** - Conduct opening meeting - Collect audit evidence (interviews, document review, observation) - Generate audit findings - Conduct closing meeting **Phase 4 — Preparing Audit Report** - Prepare and approve audit report - Distribute report to relevant parties **Phase 5 — Completing the Audit** - Distribute audit report - Retain audit documents as per procedure **Phase 6 — Conducting Audit Follow-up** - Verify correction - Verify corrective action effectiveness - Issue follow-up report ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The formulas below are crucial for solving numerical problems in **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** calculations, which frequently appear in KTU board exams linked to auditing outcomes. | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cost of Quality (Total CoQ)** | $CoQ = P_{rev} + A_{pp} + I_{FC} + E_{FC}$ | Currency (₹ / $) | | 2 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = I_{FC} + E_{FC}$ | Currency | | 3 | **Cost of Good Quality (COGQ)** | $COGQ = P_{rev} + A_{pp}$ | Currency | | 4 | **CoQ as % of Sales** | $CoQ\% = \dfrac{CoQ}{Total\ Revenue} \times 100$ | % | | 5 | **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** | $CoC = P_{rev} + A_{pp}$ | Currency | | 6 | **Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)** | $CoNC = I_{FC} + E_{FC}$ | Currency | | 7 | **Quality Ratio** | $QR = \dfrac{P_{rev} + A_{pp}}{I_{FC} + E_{FC}}$ | Ratio (no unit) | | 8 | **Savings from Prevention** | $\Delta S = (I_{FC,old} - I_{FC,new}) + (E_{FC,old} - E_{FC,new}) - \Delta P_{rev} - \Delta A_{pp}$ | Currency | | 9 | **Audit Effectiveness Index** | $AEI = \dfrac{N_{NC\ resolved\ in\ time}}{N_{NC\ raised}} \times 100$ | % | | 10 | **Audit Coverage Ratio** | $ACR = \dfrac{N_{processes\ audited}}{N_{total\ processes}} \times 100$ | % | **Where:** - $P_{rev}$ = Prevention Cost (training, quality planning, design review) - $A_{pp}$ = Appraisal Cost (inspection, testing, audits) - $I_{FC}$ = Internal Failure Cost (scrap, rework, reinspection) - $E_{FC}$ = External Failure Cost (warranty claims, returns, complaints) ### 2.5 Categories of Quality Costs (Linked to Audits) | Category | Examples | Audit Linkage | |---|---|---| | **Prevention Costs** | Quality training, design reviews, supplier capability assessments, process capability studies | Audit programme itself is a **prevention cost** | | **Appraisal Costs** | Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, calibration, **quality audits** | Direct cost of conducting audits | | **Internal Failure Costs** | Scrap, rework, downtime, reinspection, failure analysis | Nonconformities raised by internal audits | | **External Failure Costs** | Warranty claims, field service, returns, complaint handling, lawsuits | Nonconformities missed by internal audits | ### 2.6 Auditor Competence Framework A competent auditor must possess: 1. **Personal Attributes** — Ethical, open-minded, diplomatic, observant, perceptive, tenacious, decisive, self-reliant. 2. **Knowledge** — Audit principles, audit procedures, QMS requirements, applicable standards, organizational context. 3. **Skills** — Applying audit principles, conducting interviews, observing activities, reviewing documents, reporting findings. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Application:** In manufacturing firms, quality audits are mandatory before ISO 9001, AS9100 (aerospace), IATF 16949 (automotive), and ISO 13485 (medical devices) certification. Engineering students will encounter audit findings during industrial internships as a routine part of supplier quality engineering. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Quality Auditing is fundamentally a *qualitative* subject. However, **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** is the primary quantitative area tested by KTU. Below are exhaustive worked solutions and logical derivations. --- ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cost of Quality Calculation **Problem:** A precision engineering firm provides the following annual quality data: | Item | Amount (₹ Lakhs) | |---|---| | Quality training programme | 5.0 | | Design review of products | 3.5 | | Incoming material inspection | 4.0 | | In-process inspection | 6.0 | | Scrap (rejected in-house) | 7.5 | | Rework labour | 5.5 | | Warranty claims from customers | 9.0 | | Field service repairs | 6.5 | | Final product testing | 3.0 | | Supplier audit cost | 2.0 | **Total annual revenue = ₹ 500 Lakhs.** **Calculate:** 1. Total Cost of Quality (CoQ) 2. Cost of Good Quality (COGQ) and Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) 3. CoQ as a percentage of sales 4. Quality Ratio (QR) 5. Comment on the firm's quality cost structure. **Step 1 — Classify each item into the four CoQ categories:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{rev} &= \text{Quality training} + \text{Design review} + \text{Supplier audit cost} \\ &= 5.0 + 3.5 + 2.0 = 10.5\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{pp} &= \text{Incoming inspection} + \text{In-process inspection} + \text{Final testing} \\ &= 4.0 + 6.0 + 3.0 = 13.0\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} I_{FC} &= \text{Scrap} + \text{Rework labour} \\ &= 7.5 + 5.5 = 13.0\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{FC} &= \text{Warranty claims} + \text{Field service repairs} \\ &= 9.0 + 6.5 = 15.5\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Total CoQ:** $$ \begin{aligned} CoQ &= P_{rev} + A_{pp} + I_{FC} + E_{FC} \\ &= 10.5 + 13.0 + 13.0 + 15.5 \\ &= 52.0\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Calculate COGQ and COPQ:** $$ \begin{aligned} COGQ &= P_{rev} + A_{pp} = 10.5 + 13.0 = 23.5\ \text{Lakhs} \\ COPQ &= I_{FC} + E_{FC} = 13.0 + 15.5 = 28.5\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate CoQ as % of Sales:** $$ \begin{aligned} CoQ\% &= \dfrac{CoQ}{\text{Total Revenue}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{52.0}{500} \times 100 \\ &= 10.4\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate Quality Ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} QR &= \dfrac{P_{rev} + A_{pp}}{I_{FC} + E_{FC}} = \dfrac{23.5}{28.5} = 0.825 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Analytical Comment:** Since $QR < 1$, the firm is **spending more on failure costs than on conformance costs**. This is a classic sign of a *reactive* quality culture. Recommendation: **Increase $P_{rev}$ spending** (training, design reviews) to drive down $I_{FC}$ and $E_{FC}$ — this is the *Juran optimization principle* applied to CoQ. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Savings from Prevention (Audit Investment ROI) **Problem:** A firm invests an additional ₹ 8 Lakhs in prevention activities (a new internal audit programme). As a result: - Internal failure cost reduces from ₹ 20 to ₹ 12 Lakhs - External failure cost reduces from ₹ 30 to ₹ 18 Lakhs - Appraisal cost increases by ₹ 2 Lakhs (auditor salary) **Calculate net savings and ROI.** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta I_{FC} &= 20 - 12 = 8\ \text{Lakhs (saved)} \\ \Delta E_{FC} &= 30 - 18 = 12\ \text{Lakhs (saved)} \\ \Delta P_{rev} &= 8\ \text{Lakhs (added)} \\ \Delta A_{pp} &= 2\ \text{Lakhs (added)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Net Savings} &= \Delta I_{FC} + \Delta E_{FC} - \Delta P_{rev} - \Delta A_{pp} \\ &= 8 + 12 - 8 - 2 \\ &= 10\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} ROI &= \dfrac{\text{Net Savings}}{\Delta P_{rev} + \Delta A_{pp}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{10}{10} \times 100 = 100\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Every ₹ 1 invested in the audit programme returns ₹ 2 in net savings — strong justification for sustaining the prevention programme. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Audit Effectiveness Index **Problem:** During an annual internal audit programme, 45 nonconformities (NCs) were raised. Of these, 38 were resolved and closed within the agreed timeline, 5 were resolved late, and 2 remained unresolved at year-end. **Calculate AEI and interpret.** $$ \begin{aligned} AEI &= \dfrac{N_{NC\ resolved\ in\ time}}{N_{NC\ raised}} \times 100 \\ &= \dfrac{38}{45} \times 100 = 84.44\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** An AEI of 84.44% indicates a *moderately effective* corrective action system. Best-practice industry benchmark is **≥ 95%**. Root cause: the 5 late closures and 2 unresolved NCs require management review escalation. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Audit Types | Parameter | First-Party (Internal) | Second-Party (Supplier) | Third-Party (Certification) | |---|---|---|---| | **Conducted by** | Own staff | Customer | Independent body (TÜV, BV, DNV) | | **Objective** | Improvement & conformity | Supplier selection & monitoring | Certification / Registration | | **Frequency** | Continuous / Periodic | Before contract, then periodic | Annual surveillance | | **Confidentiality** | High | High | Public (certificates are public) | | **Legal weight** | Low | Moderate | High (contractual & regulatory) | | **Auditor relationship** | Colleague / Co-worker | Purchaser representative | Independent professional | | **Cost to organization** | Low (in-house) | Moderate (customer bears) | High (certification body fees) | | **Findings use** | Internal CAPA | Supplier development / de-listing | Issue / suspend / withdraw certificate | --- ### 3.5 The Audit Logic Chain (KTU Analytical Flow) For every finding, the auditor follows a strict logical chain: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Criteria} &\xrightarrow{\text{compared with}} \text{Evidence} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{evaluated using}} \text{Verification} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{yields}} \text{Findings} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{aggregated into}} \text{Conclusions} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{documented in}} \text{Audit Report} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{triggers}} \text{Corrective Action} \\ &\xrightarrow{\text{verified in}} \text{Follow-up Audit} \end{aligned} $$ This chain is the **spinal cord** of the entire audit programme. A break at any link renders the audit non-credible. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Six-Phase Audit Process Flow (ISO 19011:2018) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Initiate the Audit] --> B[Phase 2: Prepare Audit Activities] B --> C[Phase 3: Conduct the Audit] C --> D[Phase 4: Prepare Audit Report] D --> E[Phase 5: Complete the Audit] E --> F[Phase 6: Conduct Audit Follow-up] F --> G[Closure of Audit Cycle] G --> A A --> A1[Appoint Team Leader] A --> A2[Define Scope, Criteria, Objectives] A --> A3[Establish Contact with Auditee] A --> A4[Confirm Feasibility] B --> B1[Document Review] B --> B2[Prepare Audit Plan] B --> B3[Assign Team Tasks] B --> B4[Prepare Checklists] C --> C1[Opening Meeting] C --> C2[Collect Evidence] C --> C3[Generate Findings] C --> C4[Closing Meeting] D --> D1[Draft Report] D --> D2[Review & Approve] D --> D3[Distribute Report] E --> E1[Archive Documents] E --> E2[Final Communication] F --> F1[Verify Correction] F --> F2[Verify Effectiveness] F --> F3[Issue Follow-up Report] ``` --- ### 4.2 Types of Quality Audits — Hierarchical Classification ```mermaid graph TD RootA[Quality Audits] --> A1[By Scope] RootA --> A2[By Who Conducts] A1 --> A1a[System Audit] A1 --> A1b[Process Audit] A1 --> A1c[Product Audit] A1 --> A1d[Service Audit] A2 --> A2a[First Party - Internal] A2 --> A2b[Second Party - Supplier] A2 --> A2c[Third Party - Certification] A1a --> A1aD[Entire QMS vs ISO 9001] A1b --> A1bD[Specific process vs procedure] A1c --> A1cD[Product vs specifications] A1d --> A1dD[Service vs SLA] A2a --> A2aD[Conducted by own staff] A2b --> A2bD[Conducted by customer] A2c --> A2cD[Conducted by independent body] ``` --- ### 4.3 The Audit Logic Chain — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Audit Criteria
Standards / Procedures] --> P2[Audit Evidence
Records / Observation / Interview] P2 --> P3[Verification
Objective Comparison] P3 --> P4[Audit Findings
Conformity / NC / Observation] P4 --> P5[Audit Conclusion
Aggregate Result] P5 --> P6[Audit Report
Documented Evidence] P6 --> P7[Corrective Action
Root Cause Removal] P7 --> P8[Follow-up Audit
Effectiveness Check] P8 --> P9[Closure] P9 -.feedback loop.-> P1 ``` --- ### 4.4 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — Category Map ```mermaid graph TD CoQ[Total Cost of Quality CoQ] --> Conf[Cost of Conformance] CoQ --> NonConf[Cost of Non-Conformance] Conf --> Prev[Prevention Costs] Conf --> App[Appraisal Costs] NonConf --> IFC[Internal Failure Costs] NonConf --> EFC[External Failure Costs] Prev --> Prev1[Quality Training] Prev --> Prev2[Design Reviews] Prev --> Prev3[Quality Planning] Prev --> Prev4[Internal Audit Programme] App --> App1[Inspection and Testing] App --> App2[Calibration] App --> App3[External Audits] IFC --> IFC1[Scrap and Reject] IFC --> IFC2[Rework and Repair] IFC --> IFC3[Reinspection] IFC --> IFC4[Failure Analysis] EFC --> EFC1[Warranty Claims] EFC --> EFC2[Field Service] EFC --> EFC3[Customer Complaints] EFC --> EFC4[Product Recall and Liability] ``` --- ### 4.5 Auditor Competence Framework ```mermaid graph TD Comp[Auditor Competence] --> PA[Personal Attributes] Comp --> K[Knowledge Base] Comp --> S[Audit Skills] PA --> PA1[Ethical] PA --> PA2[Open-Minded] PA --> PA3[Diplomatic] PA --> PA4[Observant] PA --> PA5[Perceptive] PA --> PA6[Tenacious] PA --> PA7[Decisive] PA --> PA8[Self-Reliant] K --> K1[Audit Principles and Procedures] K --> K2[QMS Standards ISO 9001] K --> K3[Organizational Context] K --> K4[Applicable Regulations] S --> S1[Interviewing] S --> S2[Document Review] S --> S3[Process Observation] S --> S4[Report Writing] S --> S5[Team Leadership] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Define Quality Audit. List any four principles of auditing as per ISO 19011:2018.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Quality Audit is a systematic, independent, and documented process for obtaining audit evidence and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which audit criteria are fulfilled (ISO 19011:2018). **Any Four Principles (½ mark each = 2 marks):** 1. **Integrity** — Foundation of professionalism; auditor must be honest and truthful. 2. **Fair Presentation** — Audit findings must reflect true conditions without exaggeration. 3. **Due Professional Care** — Diligence and good judgment in conducting audits. 4. **Confidentiality** — Audit information must not be disclosed improperly. 5. **Independence** — Auditor must be free from bias and conflict of interest. 6. **Evidence-based Approach** — Conclusions must be based on verifiable evidence. 7. **Risk-based Approach** — Audit focus aligned with organizational risk. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between First-Party, Second-Party, and Third-Party audits. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | Basis | First-Party (Internal) | Second-Party (External/Supplier) | Third-Party (Certification) | |---|---|---|---| | **Conducted by** | Own organization | Customer or their agent | Independent certification body | | **Purpose** | Self-assessment, improvement | Supplier evaluation | Certification/Registration | | **Frequency** | Continuous/Periodic | Before & during contract | Annual surveillance | | **Cost to firm** | Low (in-house) | Low (customer bears) | High (certification fee) | | **Result** | Internal CAPA | Supplier approval/development | Issue/suspend/withdraw certificate | --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- **PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the **six phases** of the quality audit process as per **ISO 19011:2018** with a neat flowchart. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A manufacturing company incurred the following quality costs during a year: > > | Item | Amount (₹ Lakhs) | > |---|---| > | Quality training | 4 | > | Design verification | 6 | > | Incoming inspection | 8 | > | In-process testing | 10 | > | Scrap and rework | 14 | > | Customer returns | 18 | > | Warranty repairs | 12 | > | Final product audit | 4 | > | Supplier quality audit | 2 | > > **Total Sales = ₹ 400 Lakhs.** > > **Calculate:** (i) Total Cost of Quality, (ii) CoQ as % of Sales, (iii) Quality Ratio, and (iv) State whether the firm should invest more in prevention. **(7 Marks)** --- **MODEL ANSWER — Part (a) — 7 Marks** The six phases of quality audit process as per ISO 19011:2018 are: **Phase 1: Initiating the Audit (1 mark)** - Appoint audit team leader and members. - Determine audit scope, criteria, and objectives. - Establish initial contact with the auditee. - Confirm feasibility of conducting the audit. **Phase 2: Preparing Audit Activities (1 mark)** - Review relevant QMS documentation (quality manual, procedures). - Prepare the audit plan (dates, areas, schedule). - Assign responsibilities to audit team members. - Prepare working documents — checklists, sampling plans, forms. **Phase 3: Conducting the Audit (2 marks)** - Conduct an **opening meeting** with auditee's top management — introduce team, confirm scope, agree logistics. - Collect **audit evidence** through interviews, document review, and direct observation of activities. - Generate **audit findings** (conformity / nonconformity / observation) against criteria. - Hold periodic team meetings to consolidate findings. - Conduct **closing meeting** — present findings, agree timelines for corrective action. **Phase 4: Preparing Audit Report (1 mark)** - Draft the report including scope, criteria, findings, conclusions, and recommendations. - Review and approve by audit team leader. - Distribute the report to designated recipients. **Phase 5: Completing the Audit (1 mark)** - Distribute the audit report formally. - Retain audit records as per document control procedure. - Close out all working documents. **Phase 6: Conducting Audit Follow-up (1 mark)** - Verify that **correction** has been taken on each nonconformity. - Verify that **corrective action** has addressed the root cause. - Verify **effectiveness** of the corrective action. - Issue a follow-up report. **Flowchart (to be drawn in answer sheet):** ```mermaid flowchart TD Ph1[Phase 1: Initiate] --> Ph2[Phase 2: Prepare] Ph2 --> Ph3[Phase 3: Conduct] Ph3 --> Ph4[Phase 4: Report] Ph4 --> Ph5[Phase 5: Complete] Ph5 --> Ph6[Phase 6: Follow-up] ``` --- **MODEL ANSWER — Part (b) — 7 Marks (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)** **Step 1 — Categorize the data:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{rev} &= \text{Quality training} + \text{Design verification} + \text{Supplier quality audit} \\ &= 4 + 6 + 2 = 12\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{pp} &= \text{Incoming inspection} + \text{In-process testing} + \text{Final product audit} \\ &= 8 + 10 + 4 = 22\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} I_{FC} &= \text{Scrap and rework} = 14\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{FC} &= \text{Customer returns} + \text{Warranty repairs} \\ &= 18 + 12 = 30\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Total Cost of Quality:** $$ \begin{aligned} CoQ &= P_{rev} + A_{pp} + I_{FC} + E_{FC} \\ &= 12 + 22 + 14 + 30 = 78\ \text{Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — CoQ as % of Sales:** $$ \begin{aligned} CoQ\% &= \dfrac{78}{400} \times 100 = 19.5\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Quality Ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} QR &= \dfrac{P_{rev} + A_{pp}}{I_{FC} + E_{FC}} = \dfrac{12 + 22}{14 + 30} = \dfrac{34}{44} = 0.773 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation and Recommendation (2 marks):** Since $QR = 0.773 < 1$, the firm spends **less on conformance** than on **non-conformance**. External failure cost (₹ 30L) is the highest single category, indicating defective products are reaching customers. The firm should: - **Invest more in prevention** (₹ 12L → ₹ 18–20L) — expand training, design verification, supplier audits. - Expect that prevention investment will reduce $I_{FC}$ and $E_{FC}$ substantially, lowering total CoQ. **Conclusion:** As per **Juran's CoQ optimization principle**, the firm is in the *high-cost region* and should shift its spending leftward (more prevention) to reach the optimum CoQ point. --- ### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]** > **(a)** Explain the **three types of quality audits** (First, Second, Third Party) in detail with their objectives, advantages, and limitations. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **seven principles of auditing** as per ISO 19011:2018 and explain why each is critical for a credible audit. **(7 Marks)** --- **MODEL ANSWER — Part (a) — 7 Marks** **1. First-Party Audit (Internal Audit) (2 marks):** - **Definition:** Audit conducted by the organization's own trained auditors or by the quality department. - **Objective:** Self-assessment, identify improvement opportunities, ensure continuing suitability of QMS. - **Advantages:** Cost-effective, frequent, no conflict of interest with internal management, easy follow-up. - **Limitations:** Possible lack of objectivity (auditor knows the system too well), no external credibility, may have political bias within departments. **2. Second-Party Audit (External / Supplier Audit) (2 marks):** - **Definition:** Audit conducted by a customer (or their representative) on a supplier. - **Objective:** Evaluate supplier's capability, ensure supplier conformity to contractual requirements. - **Advantages:** Customer gets direct visibility of supplier processes, builds trust, enables supplier development. - **Limitations:** May focus only on customer's specific requirements, smaller scope than full QMS audit, possible commercial conflict of interest. **3. Third-Party Audit (Certification / Regulatory) (2 marks):** - **Definition:** Audit conducted by an independent, accredited certification body (e.g., TÜV SÜD, Bureau Veritas, DNV) to issue a formal certificate. - **Objective:** Independent certification against international standards (e.g., ISO 9001, AS9100, IATF 16949). - **Advantages:** Highest credibility, market-recognized, breaks supplier-customer deadlock, regulatory compliance. - **Limitations:** Expensive, infrequent (annual), auditor unfamiliar with organization's culture, may produce only a snapshot view. **Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Parameter | First Party | Second Party | Third Party | |---|---|---|---| | Auditor | Own staff | Customer | Independent body | | Cost | Low | Low | High | | Credibility | Internal only | Commercial | Public/Certified | | Frequency | Continuous | As per contract | Annual | --- **MODEL ANSWER — Part (b) — 7 Marks — The Seven Principles** 1. **Integrity (1 mark):** Foundation of professionalism. The auditor must be honest, truthful, and fair in all dealings. Auditees must trust that findings are free from personal agenda. *Critical because:* without integrity, audit findings lose all credibility and the entire audit programme collapses. 2. **Fair Presentation (1 mark):** Findings, conclusions, and reports must reflect the true state of affairs. Obstacles encountered and unresolved divergences of opinion must be honestly reported. *Critical because:* suppressed findings create false sense of compliance, leading to system failures. 3. **Due Professional Care (1 mark):** Auditors must apply diligence, reasoned judgment, and skill. They should not jump to conclusions or generalize from a single sample. *Critical because:* careless auditing leads to either false positives (over-reporting) or false negatives (missing critical nonconformities). 4. **Confidentiality (1 mark):** Auditors must treat all audit information as confidential and not disclose it to unauthorized parties. *Critical because:* audit reports contain proprietary process information; breach of confidentiality causes legal and competitive damage. 5. **Independence (1 mark):** Auditors must be independent of the activity being audited and free from bias and conflict of interest. *Critical because:* an auditor who reports to a process owner cannot objectively assess that process. 6. **Evidence-based Approach (1 mark):** Conclusions must be based on verifiable evidence — records, observations, interviews. *Critical because:* findings based on hearsay or assumption are not legally or technically defensible. 7. **Risk-based Approach (1 mark):** Audit resources should be focused on areas of highest risk and significance to the organization. *Critical because:* with finite audit time, auditors must prioritize; risk-based thinking ensures critical processes get more attention. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Quality Auditing Answers** > > 1. **Confusing "audit" with "inspection":** Inspection is product-focused; audit is system/process-focused. Students who interchange these terms lose 1–2 marks immediately. > 2. **Confusing Major vs Minor Nonconformity:** Major NC = *affects the ability of the QMS to achieve intended results*. Minor NC = *does not affect the overall ability*. Memorize this distinction. > 3. **Wrong classification in CoQ numericals:** Students often classify *supplier audit cost* as appraisal (it is **prevention**), or *warranty cost* as internal failure (it is **external failure**). Read each item carefully. > 4. **Skipping the opening/closing meetings:** In audit process descriptions, the **opening meeting** and **closing meeting** are compulsory sub-steps of Phase 3. Omitting them loses 1 mark. > 5. **Mixing up audit types:** "Internal vs External" is NOT the same as "First vs Second vs Third Party." External audits can be either Second or Third Party. > 6. **No flowchart in 7-mark process questions:** The KTU 2024 scheme mandates a flowchart for process questions. A textual answer without a diagram loses 1–2 marks. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Auditing** - **Definition:** Systematic, Independent, Documented — process for obtaining evidence and comparing with criteria. - **Standard:** ISO 19011:2018 (Management System Auditing Guidelines). - **Three Audit Types:** First Party (Internal), Second Party (Supplier), Third Party (Certification). - **Six Phases:** Initiate → Prepare → Conduct → Report → Complete → Follow-up. - **Three Key Roles:** Auditee, Audit Client, Auditor (and Audit Team). - **Seven Principles:** Integrity, Fair Presentation, Due Professional Care, Confidentiality, Independence, Evidence-based, Risk-based. - **Two NC Types:** Major NC (affects QMS results) vs Minor NC (does not affect QMS results). - **Cost of Quality:** $CoQ = P_{rev} + A_{pp} + I_{FC} + E_{FC}$ - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):** $COPQ = I_{FC} + E_{FC}$ - **Quality Ratio Benchmark:** $QR > 1$ indicates good; $QR < 1$ indicates poor. - **Juran's Principle:** Shift investment from appraisal/failure to **prevention** for optimal CoQ. - **Audit Reports** are objective, factual, evidence-based, and must be approved before distribution. - **Follow-up audit** is mandatory to verify effectiveness of corrective action — not optional. - **Auditor Competence:** Personal Attributes + Knowledge + Skills. - **Audit Evidence Types:** Records, Statements of Fact, Observation, Interviews, Physical Inspection. - **Audit Criteria Types:** Standards (ISO 9001), Procedures, Customer Requirements, Regulatory Requirements. - **Remember the difference:** Audit (system/process) ≠ Inspection (product). - **Best practice benchmark:** AEI ≥ 95%, CoQ < 5–10% of sales for mature QMS. > **End of Module 4 — Quality Auditing Notes | PECHT413 TQM | KTU 2024 Scheme**
